topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Modelling - Introduction to Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Control System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired output (response) with a specified performance.

In the language of modern engineering, a control system is an **interconnection of components** forming a system configuration that will provide a desired system response. The **input** is excited by an external stimulus (the reference or command), and the **output** is the controlled variable that we want to behave in a specific way.

$$ y(t) = f\big(x(t),\, \text{system dynamics},\, \text{environment}\big) $$

The study of control systems is the science of **modeling**, **analyzing**, and **designing** such systems to ensure that the output tracks the input accurately, rejects disturbances, and remains stable under parameter variations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Car Cruise Control" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Car Cruise Control Example):**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and set the cruise control to **100 km/h**. You, as the driver, are no longer the controller ŌĆö the car's electronic control unit (ECU) is.
> 
> 1. **Desired speed (Reference Input)**: 100 km/h
> 2. **Actual speed (Controlled Variable/Output)**: The speedometer reading
> 3. **Sensor (Feedback Element)**: The speed sensor on the wheel
> 4. **Controller (ECU)**: Compares actual vs. desired and decides
> 5. **Actuator (Throttle body)**: Adjusts fuel flow
> 6. **Disturbance**: Uphill road, wind, passenger load
> 
> If the car begins to climb a hill, the actual speed drops. The sensor detects this, the controller computes the error, and the throttle opens more ŌĆö restoring speed. This is **feedback control**, the heart of all control engineering.

This is a perfect example of a **closed-loop control system** because the output is *fed back* and compared with the input. The genius of feedback is that it makes the system **self-correcting** and **robust** to disturbances and model uncertainties.

## 1.3 Historical Perspective (Must-Know for KTU 2-Mark Questions)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Historical Facts:**
> - **James Watt's Centrifugal Governor (1788)**: The first major industrial feedback control device for steam engine speed regulation.
> - **Minorsky's Work (1922)**: First systematic study of automatic ship steering using PID concepts.
> - **Nyquist (1932)**: Introduced the **Nyquist Stability Criterion**.
> - **Bode (1945)**: Developed **Bode Plots** for frequency response analysis.
> - **Kalman (1960)**: Pioneered **state-space analysis** and the **Kalman Filter**.

## 1.4 Classification of Control Systems (High-Yield Topic)

Control systems are broadly classified along several axes:

### (a) Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Control Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Most Fundamental Classification ŌĆö Asked in Every KTU Exam:**

| Feature | **Open-Loop System** | **Closed-Loop System** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | **Absent** ŌĆö output never returns | **Present** ŌĆö output is measured and returned |
| **Accuracy** | **Low** ŌĆö depends on calibration | **High** ŌĆö self-correcting |
| **Stability** | **Inherently stable** (no feedback loop) | **Must be analyzed** ŌĆö can become unstable |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| **Sensitivity to Parameter Variation** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Cost & Complexity** | **Cheaper, simpler** | **Expensive, more components** |
| **Examples** | Toaster, washing machine (timer-based), traffic light, hand dryer | Cruise control, AC thermostat, missile guidance, op-amp circuits |
| **Symbolic Transfer Relation** | $y(s) = G(s) \, R(s)$ | $y(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \, R(s)$ |

### (b) Linear vs. Non-Linear Systems
- **Linear**: Obeys superposition ($y = ax_1 + bx_2$) and homogeneity. Can be analyzed by **Laplace transform**, **Fourier transform**, etc.
- **Non-Linear**: Does not obey superposition. Requires special methods (describing functions, phase-plane, Lyapunov).

### (c) Time-Invariant vs. Time-Varying
- **Time-Invariant (LTI)**: Parameters do not change with time.
- **Time-Varying**: Parameters change with time (e.g., a satellite whose mass decreases as fuel burns).

### (d) Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time
- **Continuous-Time**: Signals defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., analog systems).
- **Discrete-Time**: Signals defined at instants $t = kT$ (e.g., digital computers, sampled-data systems).

### (e) SISO vs. MIMO
- **SISO**: Single-Input Single-Output (e.g., one motor speed control).
- **MIMO**: Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (e.g., aircraft control ŌĆö pitch, yaw, roll).

## 1.5 Essential Terminology (KTU Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions You MUST Memorize:**

- **Plant**: The physical object or process to be controlled (e.g., furnace, motor, robot arm).
- **Process**: Another name for the plant in process-control industries.
- **System**: A combination of components that act together to perform a function.
- **Reference Input ($r(t)$)**: The desired value of the output, set by the user.
- **Controlled Variable ($c(t)$ or $y(t)$)**: The actual output quantity being measured and regulated.
- **Manipulated Variable ($m(t)$)**: The quantity adjusted by the controller to influence the output.
- **Error Signal ($e(t)$)**: The difference between the desired input and the feedback signal, $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
- **Controller ($C(s)$)**: The brain of the system ŌĆö implements P, PI, PD, or PID law.
- **Actuator**: Converts the controller's command into physical action (e.g., motor, valve).
- **Sensor / Transducer**: Measures the output and converts it to a comparable signal.
- **Disturbance ($d(t)$)**: An unwanted, unpredictable input that affects the output adversely.
- **Feedback Element ($H(s)$)**: A component that converts the output into a form suitable for comparison with the reference.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop Control System ŌĆö Architecture & Equations

An open-loop system **does not measure the output** and does **not feed it back** for comparison. The control action is **independent** of the output.

### Block Diagram (Logical Flow)

$$ R(s) \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{C(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{G(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; Y(s) $$

Where:
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $C(s)$ = Controller transfer function
- $G(s)$ = Plant transfer function
- $Y(s)$ = Output

### Transfer Function Derivation
For an open-loop system, blocks in **cascade** multiply:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = C(s) \cdot G(s) $$

**Why?** Because in the time domain, the output of the first block becomes the input of the second, so convolution in time becomes multiplication in the Laplace domain.

### Real-World Engineering Example: Automatic Washing Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blind" Toaster & Timer-Based Washing Machine Analogy:**
> 
> A toaster heats the bread for a **fixed time** (say 2 minutes) regardless of whether the bread is already golden-brown or still cold. The heating time is **pre-set**; there is no temperature sensor measuring the actual toast color.
> 
> **Engineering Utility**: Open-loop systems are used where:
> 1. The input-output relationship is **well-known** and **linear**.
> 2. Disturbances are **negligible** or constant.
> 3. **Cost** is a major constraint (e.g., consumer appliances).

### Drawback ŌĆö Why Open-Loop Fails
If the supply voltage drops (disturbance), the heater produces less heat and the bread remains uncooked. **The system cannot self-correct**, because no feedback exists.

## 2.2 Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System ŌĆö Architecture & Equations

The **single most important architecture** in control engineering. The output is measured by a sensor, fed back through a feedback element, and **subtracted** from the reference to produce an error signal.

### Block Diagram Logic
The output of the summing junction is the **error**:

$$ E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \, Y(s) $$

The forward path multiplies the controller and plant:

$$ Y(s) = G(s) \, C(s) \, E(s) = G(s) \, C(s) \, \big[R(s) - H(s) Y(s)\big] $$

### Closed-Loop Transfer Function (The Most Important Equation in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Equation ŌĆö Master This:**
> 
> $$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s) \, G(s)}{1 + C(s) \, G(s) \, H(s)}$$

For a **unity feedback system** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s) \, G(s)}{1 + C(s) \, G(s)} = \frac{G_{eq}(s)}{1 + G_{eq}(s)} $$

### Effect of Disturbance (Why Feedback Wins)

With disturbance $D(s)$ entering at the plant input, the response is:

$$ Y(s) = \frac{C(s)\,G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)\,H(s)} \, R(s) \;+\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + C(s)\,G(s)\,H(s)} \, D(s) $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: As the loop gain $C(s)G(s)H(s) \to \infty$, the disturbance term $\dfrac{G(s)}{1+C(s)G(s)H(s)} \to 0$. This is the **mathematical reason feedback rejects disturbances**.

### The Three Magic Benefits of Negative Feedback
1. **Reduces Steady-State Error** (better tracking)
2. **Increases Bandwidth** (faster response)
3. **Reduces Sensitivity to Parameter Variations**
4. **Linearizes a non-linear system** (operating point stabilization)
5. **Improves disturbance rejection** as shown above.

### The One Cost of Feedback
> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off**: Feedback can make a stable system **unstable** if not designed carefully. Stability analysis (Routh-Hurwitz, Nyquist, Root Locus) is essential. Gain too high ŌåÆ oscillations ŌåÆ instability.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All Key Formulas for Module 1 ŌĆö Memorize This Table.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-Loop Transfer Function | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = C(s) \, G(s)$ | Output depends only on input |
| 2 | Closed-Loop TF (Unity Feedback) | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{CG}{1+CG}$ | $H(s)=1$ |
| 3 | Closed-Loop TF (General) | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{CG}{1+CGH}$ | Standard form |
| 4 | Error TF (Unity) | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+CG}$ | $E(s) = R - Y$ |
| 5 | Feedback TF | $\dfrac{B(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{CGH}{1+CGH}$ | Output of feedback path |
| 6 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + C(s)G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Determines **stability** |
| 7 | Order of System | Highest power of $s$ in denominator | $1^{st}, 2^{nd}, n^{th}$ order |
| 8 | DC Gain | $K = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$ | Steady-state amplification |
| 9 | Sensitivity | $S^{T}_{G} = \dfrac{\partial T/T}{\partial G/G} = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | For unity feedback |
| 10 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s)$ | Final Value Theorem |
| 11 | Step Input ($r(t) = 1$) | $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ | Most common test signal |
| 12 | Ramp Input ($r(t) = t$) | $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ | Velocity test |
| 13 | Parabolic Input ($r(t) = t^2/2$) | $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^{3}}$ | Acceleration test |
| 14 | System Type Number | $N$ = number of pure integrators ($\tfrac{1}{s}$) in open-loop | Type-0, Type-1, Type-2 |
| 15 | Position Error Constant | $K_{p} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | Type-0 system |
| 16 | Velocity Error Constant | $K_{v} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, G(s)H(s)$ | Type-1 system |
| 17 | Acceleration Error Constant | $K_{a} = \lim_{s \to 0} s^{2} \, G(s)H(s)$ | Type-2 system |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Systems Are Used Today:**

1. **Aerospace**: Autopilot, drone attitude control (closed-loop PID at MHz).
2. **Automotive**: Cruise control, ABS braking, active suspension, lane keeping.
3. **Robotics**: Joint position/velocity control, force feedback in surgery bots.
4. **Process Industry**: Temperature, pressure, flow, level control in refineries.
5. **Power Systems**: Automatic Voltage Regulators (AVR), load frequency control.
6. **Biomedical**: Insulin pump, anesthesia delivery, prosthetic limbs.
7. **Consumer Electronics**: Image stabilization in cameras, gimbal control in phones.

The concept of **system modeling** is the very first step: before you can analyze or design, you must mathematically describe how the physical system behaves. This is the focus of **Module 1** ŌĆö deriving the differential equations for mechanical, electrical, thermal, and hydraulic systems, then converting them to transfer functions via the Laplace transform.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **exhaustive step-by-step mathematical modeling** that KTU expects in the 14-mark Part B questions. We derive the transfer function for the three most important systems: **(i) Mass-Spring-Damper**, **(ii) RLC Electrical Circuit**, **(iii) DC Servomotor**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Mass-Spring-Damper Mechanical System

### Physical Setup
A mass $M$ is connected to a fixed wall via a spring of stiffness $K$ and a damper with damping coefficient $B$. An external force $f(t)$ is applied. The displacement of the mass is $y(t)$. We assume a **frictionless horizontal surface** (no gravity component in the equation of motion).

> **Free-Body Diagram (verbal description):** The mass $M$ is pulled by the applied force $f(t)$ to the right. The spring exerts a restoring force $-K y(t)$ to the left. The damper exerts a velocity-proportional force $-B \dot{y}(t)$ to the left.

### Step 1: Identify All Forces Acting on the Mass
1. **Applied force**: $+f(t)$ (assumed positive to the right)
2. **Spring force** (Hooke's Law): $-K y(t)$ ŌĆö proportional to displacement, opposite to motion
3. **Damping force** (Newtonian viscous friction): $-B \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt}$ ŌĆö proportional to velocity, opposite to motion

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
The net force equals mass times acceleration:

$$ \sum F = M \cdot a(t) = M \cdot \frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} $$

Therefore:

$$ f(t) - K y(t) - B \frac{dy(t)}{dt} = M \frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} $$

### Step 3: Rearrange into Standard Form (Output on Left)

$$ M \frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + K y(t) = f(t) $$

### Step 4: Take the Laplace Transform (Assuming Zero Initial Conditions)
Using the standard result: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^{n}y}{dt^{n}}\right\} = s^{n} Y(s)$ when $y(0)=\dot{y}(0)=0$.

$$ M s^{2} Y(s) + B s Y(s) + K Y(s) = F(s) $$

### Step 5: Factor $Y(s)$ and Solve for the Transfer Function

$$ Y(s) \left[ M s^{2} + B s + K \right] = F(s) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function of Mass-Spring-Damper System:**
> 
> $$\boxed{\;\frac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K}\;}$$

### Step 6: Express in Standard Second-Order Form
Divide numerator and denominator by $M$:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1/M}{s^{2} + \left(\dfrac{B}{M}\right) s + \dfrac{K}{M}} $$

Compare with the canonical form $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$:

$$ \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} \quad , \quad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}} $$

These are the **natural frequency** and **damping ratio** ŌĆö central concepts in transient response analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö RLC Series Electrical Circuit

### Physical Setup
A series RLC circuit driven by a voltage source $v(t)$. The resistor $R$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$ are in series. The current is $i(t)$ and the capacitor voltage (output) is $v_{C}(t)$.

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
The sum of voltage drops around the loop equals the source:

$$ v(t) = v_{R}(t) + v_{L}(t) + v_{C}(t) $$

### Step 2: Express Each Element Voltage in Terms of Current or Charge
Using the **constitutive relations** of circuit elements:

$$ v_{R}(t) = R \, i(t) $$

$$ v_{L}(t) = L \, \frac{di(t)}{dt} $$

$$ v_{C}(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int i(t) \, dt \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad i(t) = C \, \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt} $$

### Step 3: Write the Integro-Differential Equation
Substituting back into KVL:

$$ v(t) = R i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int i(t) \, dt $$

### Step 4: Convert Current to Capacitor Voltage
Since $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt}$, and its derivative is $\dfrac{di(t)}{dt} = C \dfrac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}}$:

$$ v(t) = R \, C \, \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt} + L \, C \, \frac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}} + v_{C}(t) $$

### Step 5: Rearrange in Standard Form (Output on Left)

$$ LC \, \frac{d^{2}v_{C}(t)}{dt^{2}} + RC \, \frac{dv_{C}(t)}{dt} + v_{C}(t) = v(t) $$

### Step 6: Take the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)

$$ L C \, s^{2} V_{C}(s) + R C \, s \, V_{C}(s) + V_{C}(s) = V(s) $$

### Step 7: Solve for the Transfer Function

$$ V_{C}(s) \left[ LC s^{2} + RC s + 1 \right] = V(s) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function of Series RLC Circuit:**
> 
> $$\boxed{\;\frac{V_{C}(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1}{LC s^{2} + RC s + 1} = \frac{1/LC}{s^{2} + \left(\dfrac{R}{L}\right) s + \dfrac{1}{LC}}\;}$$

**Remarkable Observation**: The mathematical form is **identical** to the mass-spring-damper system! This is the principle of **analogous systems** ŌĆö mechanical, electrical, thermal, and hydraulic systems all share isomorphic differential equations.

| Mechanical Quantity | Electrical Analog |
|---|---|
| Force $f(t)$ | Voltage $v(t)$ |
| Mass $M$ | Inductance $L$ |
| Damper $B$ | Resistance $R$ |
| Spring $K$ | Inverse Capacitance $\dfrac{1}{C}$ |
| Displacement $y(t)$ | Charge $q(t)$ or $\int v_C dt$ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö DC Servomotor (Armature-Controlled)

### Physical Setup
A DC motor where the **field current is held constant**, and the **armature voltage $v_{a}(t)$** is the control input. The output is the **angular position $\theta(t)$** of the shaft. The motor drives a load with moment of inertia $J_{m}$ and viscous friction $B_{m}$.

### Step 1: Armature Circuit Equation (Electrical Side)
Apply KVL to the armature circuit (resistance $R_{a}$, inductance $L_{a}$, back-EMF $e_{b}$):

$$ v_{a}(t) = R_{a} i_{a}(t) + L_{a} \frac{di_{a}(t)}{dt} + e_{b}(t) $$

The **back-EMF** is proportional to angular velocity:

$$ e_{b}(t) = K_{b} \, \omega(t) = K_{b} \, \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} $$

where $K_{b}$ is the back-EMF constant (in V┬Ęs/rad).

### Step 2: Mechanical Torque Equation
The motor produces torque proportional to armature current:

$$ T_{m}(t) = K_{t} \, i_{a}(t) $$

This torque overcomes inertia and friction:

$$ T_{m}(t) = J_{m} \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}} + B_{m} \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} $$

### Step 3: Substitute $i_{a}(t) = T_{m}(t)/K_{t}$ into the Armature Equation

$$ v_{a}(t) = R_{a} \cdot \frac{T_{m}(t)}{K_{t}} + L_{a} \cdot \frac{1}{K_{t}} \frac{dT_{m}(t)}{dt} + K_{b} \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} $$

### Step 4: Substitute $T_{m}(t)$ in Terms of $\theta(t)$

$$ v_{a}(t) = \frac{R_{a}}{K_{t}} \left[ J_{m} \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}} + B_{m} \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} \right] + \frac{L_{a}}{K_{t}} \left[ J_{m} \frac{d^{3}\theta(t)}{dt^{3}} + B_{m} \frac{d^{2}\theta(t)}{dt^{2}} \right] + K_{b} \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} $$

### Step 5: Take the Laplace Transform

$$ V_{a}(s) = \frac{R_{a}}{K_{t}} \left[ J_{m} s^{2} + B_{m} s \right] \Theta(s) + \frac{L_{a}}{K_{t}} \left[ J_{m} s^{3} + B_{m} s^{2} \right] \Theta(s) + K_{b} s \, \Theta(s) $$

### Step 6: Collect Terms in $\Theta(s)$

$$ V_{a}(s) = \left[ \frac{L_{a} J_{m}}{K_{t}} s^{3} + \left( \frac{R_{a} J_{m} + L_{a} B_{m}}{K_{t}} \right) s^{2} + \left( \frac{R_{a} B_{m}}{K_{t}} + K_{b} \right) s \right] \Theta(s) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function of DC Servomotor (Position Output):**
> 
> $$\boxed{\;\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_{a}(s)} = \frac{K_{t}}{s \left[ L_{a} J_{m} s^{2} + (R_{a} J_{m} + L_{a} B_{m}) s + (R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}) \right] }\;}$$

### Step 7: Simplified Version (Neglecting $L_{a}$ for Low-Frequency Analysis)
If armature inductance $L_{a}$ is small, the TF simplifies to a **second-order** form:

$$ \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_{a}(s)} \approx \frac{K_{t}}{s \left[ R_{a} J_{m} s + (R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}) \right]} = \frac{K_{m}}{s (T_{m} s + 1)} $$

where $K_{m} = \dfrac{K_{t}}{R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}}$ is the **motor gain constant** and $T_{m} = \dfrac{R_{a} J_{m}}{R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}}$ is the **mechanical time constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Extra $1/s$?** The output is **angular position** $\theta$, but the intermediate variable is **angular velocity** $\omega = d\theta/dt$. Velocity-to-position integration in the Laplace domain is division by $s$.

---

## 3.4 Summary Table ŌĆö All Three Transfer Functions

| System | Transfer Function | Order | Key Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass-Spring-Damper | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K}$ | **2nd** | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{K/M}$, $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{KM})$ |
| Series RLC | $\dfrac{V_{C}(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{1/LC}{s^{2} + (R/L) s + 1/LC}$ | **2nd** | $\omega_{n} = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, $\zeta = (R/2)\sqrt{C/L}$ |
| DC Servomotor | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_{a}(s)} = \dfrac{K_{t}}{s (L_{a} J_{m} s^{2} + \cdots)}$ | **3rd** (with $L_a$) or **2nd** (without) | $K_{m}, T_{m}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: The mapping $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$, $f \leftrightarrow v$ is the **Force-Voltage (F-V) analogy**. The mapping $M \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$, $f \leftrightarrow i$ is the **Force-Current (F-I) analogy**. These appear in viva questions frequently.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown inside quoted labels), and no reserved keywords are used as node IDs.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Open-Loop Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputR["REFERENCE INPUT R(s)"] --> controllerC["CONTROLLER C(s)"]
    controllerC --> plantG["PLANT OR PROCESS G(s)"]
    plantG --> outputY["CONTROLLED OUTPUT Y(s)"]
    disturbD["DISTURBANCE D(s)"] -.-> plantG
    style inputR fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style controllerC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style plantG fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style outputY fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style disturbD fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The reference input $R(s)$ flows into the controller $C(s)$, which commands the plant $G(s)$, producing the output $Y(s)$. Notice **no return path** from output to input. The disturbance $D(s)$ (dashed line) adds noise to the plant, and there is **no mechanism** to correct for it.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Closed-Loop (Negative Feedback) Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputR["REFERENCE INPUT R(s)"] --> sumJ["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    sumJ --> controllerC["CONTROLLER C(s)"]
    controllerC --> plantG["PLANT G(s)"]
    plantG --> outputY["CONTROLLED OUTPUT Y(s)"]
    outputY --> feedbackH["FEEDBACK ELEMENT H(s)"]
    feedbackH --> sumJ
    disturbD["DISTURBANCE D(s)"] -.-> plantG
    style inputR fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style sumJ fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style controllerC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style plantG fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style outputY fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style feedbackH fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style disturbD fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The output $Y(s)$ is now tapped and passed through the **feedback element $H(s)$** to produce $B(s) = H(s)Y(s)$. At the **summing junction**, $B(s)$ is **subtracted** from the reference $R(s)$ to form the **error signal** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. The error drives the controller, which in turn commands the plant ŌĆö closing the loop. Even if the disturbance $D(s)$ acts on the plant, the controller senses the resulting output error and **automatically compensates**.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Modern Feedback Control System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph USER_LAYER["USER LAYER"]
        refA["DESIRED SETPOINT"]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER["CONTROL LAYER"]
        compA["COMPARATOR SUBTRACTOR"]
        errSig["ERROR SIGNAL e(t)"]
        ctrlUnit["CONTROLLER P OR PI OR PD OR PID"]
        ctrlOut["CONTROL SIGNAL u(t)"]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION_LAYER["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        actuatorA["ACTUATOR MOTOR OR VALVE"]
        plantA["PLANT PHYSICAL PROCESS"]
    end
    subgraph MEASUREMENT_LAYER["MEASUREMENT LAYER"]
        sensorA["SENSOR TRANSDUCER"]
        transdA["SIGNAL CONDITIONER"]
        fbSig["FEEDBACK SIGNAL b(t)"]
    end
    refA --> compA
    fbSig --> compA
    compA --> errSig
    errSig --> ctrlUnit
    ctrlUnit --> ctrlOut
    ctrlOut --> actuatorA
    actuatorA --> plantA
    plantA --> sensorA
    sensorA --> transdA
    transdA --> fbSig
    style refA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style compA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style errSig fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ctrlUnit fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ctrlOut fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style actuatorA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style plantA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style sensorA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style transdA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style fbSig fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram**: This is the **full functional architecture** of a modern control system, broken into four layers. The **information flow** is: Setpoint ŌåÆ Comparator ŌåÆ Error ŌåÆ Controller ŌåÆ Actuator ŌåÆ Plant ŌåÆ Sensor ŌåÆ Signal Conditioner ŌåÆ Feedback ŌåÆ back to Comparator. This same architecture is used in industrial PLCs, automotive ECUs, and aircraft flight computers.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram Reduction ŌĆö Mason's Gain Formula Visual

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["INPUT R(s)"] --> nodeB["FORWARD PATH G1(s)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["NODE X1"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FORWARD PATH G2(s)"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["OUTPUT Y(s)"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["FEEDBACK H1(s)"]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG["PARALLEL PATH G3(s)"]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram**: This shows a more complex block diagram with **two forward paths** ($G_1 G_2$ and $G_3$) and **one feedback loop** ($G_1 G_2 H_1$). This is exactly the type of problem where **Mason's Gain Formula** is applied in KTU exams:
> 
> $$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\sum_{k} P_{k} \Delta_{k}}{\Delta}$$
> 
> where $P_{k}$ is the $k$-th forward path gain, $\Delta$ is the determinant, and $\Delta_{k}$ is the cofactor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define a control system. Distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer (Concise ŌĆö 3-Mark Style):

> **Definition**: A control system is an interconnection of components designed to regulate a process variable to a desired value. The output is called the **controlled variable**, and the desired value is the **reference input**.

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Depends on calibration | Self-correcting |
| Example | Electric hand dryer (fixed timer) | Air conditioner with thermostat |
| Disturbance Handling | Cannot reject disturbances | Automatically rejects disturbances |

> The **air conditioner** measures room temperature via a **thermistor** (sensor), compares it with the set-point (say 24┬░C) in the controller, and switches the compressor on/off. This is a **closed-loop** thermostat. In contrast, a **hand dryer** runs its blower for a fixed time regardless of whether hands are wet or dry ŌĆö **open-loop**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**What is a transfer function? State its important properties.**

#### Model Answer:

> **Definition**: The transfer function of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, **under the assumption of zero initial conditions**.

$$ G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} \bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}} $$

> **Important Properties** (List any three for 3 marks):
> 1. **Defined only for LTI systems**.
> 2. **Independent of input magnitude** ŌĆö it is an intrinsic property of the system.
> 3. **Defined for zero initial conditions only**.
> 4. **If $G(s)$ is known**, the output for any input can be computed: $Y(s) = G(s) X(s)$.
> 5. **Poles and zeros** of $G(s)$ completely characterize the system dynamics.
> 6. **The characteristic equation** is the denominator set to zero: $\Delta(s) = 0$.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a **mass-spring-damper mechanical system** with mass $M$, damping coefficient $B$, and spring constant $K$, where force $f(t)$ is the input and displacement $y(t)$ is the output. Express the result in standard second-order form and identify the natural frequency and damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **series RLC electrical circuit** has $R = 4 \, \Omega$, $L = 1 \, \text{H}$, and $C = 0.25 \, \text{F}$. The input is the applied voltage $v(t)$ and the output is the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$. **(i)** Derive the transfer function $\frac{V_C(s)}{V(s)}$. **(ii)** Identify whether the system is overdamped, underdamped, or critically damped. **(iii)** Find the steady-state output for a unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A(a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1** (1 mark): Draw the free-body diagram. Forces on mass: applied $f(t)$ right, spring $-Ky(t)$ left, damper $-B\dot{y}(t)$ left.

**Step 2** (1 mark): Apply Newton's second law $Ma = \sum F$:

$$ M\ddot{y}(t) + B\dot{y}(t) + K y(t) = f(t) $$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Take Laplace transform (zero ICs):

$$ M s^{2} Y(s) + B s Y(s) + K Y(s) = F(s) $$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Solve for TF:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{M s^{2} + B s + K} $$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Divide by $M$ for standard form:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1/M}{s^{2} + \dfrac{B}{M} s + \dfrac{K}{M}} $$

**Step 6** (2 marks): Compare with $\dfrac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$:

$$ \boxed{\;\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} \quad \text{and} \quad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}\;} $$

---

#### Part A(b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1** (1 mark): Apply KVL to the series loop:
$$ v(t) = R i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int i(t) \, dt $$

**Step 2** (1 mark): Convert current to capacitor voltage using $i = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$:

$$ v(t) = LC \frac{d^{2} v_{C}}{dt^{2}} + RC \frac{d v_{C}}{dt} + v_{C}(t) $$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Take Laplace transform and solve for TF:

$$ \frac{V_{C}(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1/LC}{s^{2} + (R/L) s + 1/LC} $$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Substitute $R = 4$, $L = 1$, $C = 0.25$:

$$ \frac{V_{C}(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1/(1 \times 0.25)}{s^{2} + (4/1) s + 1/(1 \times 0.25)} = \frac{4}{s^{2} + 4s + 4} = \frac{4}{(s+2)^{2}} $$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Compute damping ratio: $\omega_{n} = 2$, $2\zeta\omega_{n} = 4 \Rightarrow \zeta = 1$. Since $\zeta = 1$, the system is **critically damped**.

**Step 6** (2 marks): For unit step, $V(s) = 1/s$:

$$ V_{C}(s) = \frac{4}{s(s+2)^{2}} $$

Apply **partial fractions**:

$$ \frac{4}{s(s+2)^{2}} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{(s+2)^{2}} $$

Multiply both sides by $s(s+2)^{2}$:

$$ 4 = A(s+2)^{2} + B s(s+2) + C s $$

Set $s = 0$: $\;4 = 4A \Rightarrow A = 1$.
Set $s = -2$: $\;4 = -2C \Rightarrow C = -2$.
Compare $s^{2}$ coefficients: $\;0 = A + B \Rightarrow B = -A = -1$.

Therefore:

$$ V_{C}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{2}{(s+2)^{2}} $$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$ v_{C}(t) = 1 - e^{-2t} - 2t e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0 $$

**Steady-state output** (Final Value Theorem):

$$ v_{C,ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s V_{C}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{4}{(s+2)^{2}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \, \text{V} $$

> **Verification**: At $t \to \infty$, $e^{-2t} \to 0$ and $2te^{-2t} \to 0$, so $v_C(\infty) = 1 \, \text{V}$. Ō£ō

---

### Question B ŌĆö Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **basic structure of a closed-loop control system**. List the advantages and disadvantages of feedback. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **armature-controlled DC servomotor**, derive the transfer function relating the angular position $\theta(s)$ to the armature voltage $V_a(s)$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part B(a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1** (1 mark): Draw the closed-loop block diagram (as shown in Section 4.2 of these notes) with summing junction, controller, plant, and feedback path.

**Step 2** (1 mark): Identify all components:
- **Reference input** $R(s)$ ŌĆö desired value
- **Comparator** ŌĆö produces $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$
- **Error signal** $E(s)$
- **Controller** $C(s)$ ŌĆö implements control law
- **Plant** $G(s)$ ŌĆö physical process
- **Output** $Y(s)$ ŌĆö controlled variable
- **Feedback element** $H(s)$ ŌĆö sensor

**Step 3** (1 mark): Derive the closed-loop transfer function:

$$ \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s) H(s)} $$

**Step 4** (3 marks): List **advantages** of negative feedback:
1. **Reduces sensitivity** to parameter variations and component drift.
2. **Improves disturbance rejection**.
3. **Reduces steady-state error** (better tracking).
4. **Increases bandwidth** (faster transient response).
5. **Linearizes** mildly non-linear systems.

**Step 5** (1 mark): List **disadvantages** of feedback:
1. **Complexity** ŌĆö additional components (sensors, controllers).
2. **Cost** ŌĆö extra hardware.
3. **Instability** ŌĆö improper design can lead to oscillation or divergence.
4. **Gain reduction** ŌĆö overall gain drops from $CG$ to $CG/(1+CGH)$.

---

#### Part B(b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Field current $i_f$ is held constant (field flux $\phi$ is constant).
- Motor constants $K_b$ (back-EMF) and $K_t$ (torque) are constant.
- Load inertia $J_m$ and friction $B_m$ are lumped at the motor shaft.
- Initially, all initial conditions are zero.

**Step 1** (1 mark): Armature circuit equation:

$$ V_{a}(s) = (R_{a} + L_{a} s) I_{a}(s) + K_{b} s \Theta(s) $$

**Step 2** (1 mark): Mechanical torque equation:

$$ T_{m}(s) = (J_{m} s^{2} + B_{m} s) \Theta(s) = K_{t} I_{a}(s) $$

From this, $I_{a}(s) = \dfrac{(J_{m} s + B_{m}) s \Theta(s)}{K_{t}}$.

**Step 3** (1 mark): Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the armature equation:

$$ V_{a}(s) = (R_{a} + L_{a} s) \cdot \frac{(J_{m} s + B_{m}) s \Theta(s)}{K_{t}} + K_{b} s \Theta(s) $$

**Step 4** (2 marks): Factor out $s\Theta(s)$:

$$ V_{a}(s) = s\Theta(s) \left[ \frac{(R_{a} + L_{a} s)(J_{m} s + B_{m})}{K_{t}} + K_{b} \right] $$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Multiply numerator and rearrange:

$$ V_{a}(s) = s\Theta(s) \left[ \frac{L_{a} J_{m} s^{2} + (R_{a} J_{m} + L_{a} B_{m}) s + R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}}{K_{t}} \right] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Transfer Function (Position Output):**
> 
> $$\boxed{\;\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_{a}(s)} = \frac{K_{t}}{s \left[ L_{a} J_{m} s^{2} + (R_{a} J_{m} + L_{a} B_{m}) s + (R_{a} B_{m} + K_{t} K_{b}) \right] }\;}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign at the summing junction** ŌĆö feedback is *subtracted*, not added. Writing $E(s) = R + B$ instead of $E = R - B$ loses 1-2 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **Not specifying zero initial conditions** when defining the transfer function. This is a compulsory assumption in KTU valuation. Failure to mention it can cost 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Confusing the symbols** $Y(s)$ vs $C(s)$ for output and controller. Capital $C(s)$ is **controller** and capital $Y(s)$ is **output**. Use lowercase $c(t)$ if you mean the controlled output in time domain.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to derive the characteristic equation** $1 + C(s)G(s)H(s) = 0$. Many 14-mark questions have a sub-part asking for stability, and this equation is the prerequisite.
> 
> 5. **In motor derivations, writing the back-EMF as $K_b \theta$ instead of $K_b \omega = K_b s\Theta$**. Back-EMF is proportional to **velocity**, not position. This single error loses 2-3 marks.
> 
> 6. **Not simplifying the final transfer function** to a recognizable standard form ($\omega_n$, $\zeta$). Examiners want the canonical second-order form.
> 
> 7. **Mixing up the Force-Voltage and Force-Current analogies** in mechanical-electrical comparison questions. Memorize the table from Section 3.4.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist the night before your KTU exam:**
> 
> Ō£ģ **Core Definitions**
> - Control system, plant, process, system, controller, actuator, sensor
> - Reference input, controlled variable, error signal, manipulated variable
> - Disturbance, feedback element, summing junction
> 
> Ō£ģ **Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop**
> - Open: no feedback, cheap, inaccurate, no disturbance rejection
> - Closed: feedback present, accurate, self-correcting, can be unstable
> - Closed-loop TF: $T(s) = \dfrac{CG}{1+CGH}$
> - Characteristic equation: $1 + CGH = 0$
> 
> Ō£ģ **Mechanical System Modeling**
> - Mass-Spring-Damper TF: $\dfrac{Y(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}$
> - $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$, $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{KM})$
> - Newton's second law: $F = Ma$
> 
> Ō£ģ **Electrical System Modeling**
> - Series RLC TF (output = capacitor voltage): $\dfrac{V_C(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{1/LC}{s^2 + (R/L)s + 1/LC}$
> - KVL: sum of drops = source
> - $\omega_n = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, $\zeta = (R/2)\sqrt{C/L}$
> 
> Ō£ģ **DC Servomotor Modeling**
> - Position TF: $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s[L_a J_m s^2 + (R_a J_m + L_a B_m)s + (R_a B_m + K_t K_b)]}$
> - Armature: $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a (di_a/dt) + K_b \omega$
> - Mechanical: $T_m = K_t i_a = J_m \ddot{\theta} + B_m \dot{\theta}$
> - Back-EMF is proportional to **velocity**, not position
> 
> Ō£ģ **Analogies (Memorize for Viva)**
> - F-V analogy: $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$, $f \leftrightarrow v$
> - F-I analogy: $M \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$, $f \leftrightarrow i$
> 
> Ō£ģ **Historical Facts (2-Mark KTU Questions)**
> - James Watt ŌĆö Centrifugal Governor (1788)
> - Minorsky ŌĆö Ship steering (1922)
> - Nyquist ŌĆö Stability criterion (1932)
> - Bode ŌĆö Frequency response (1945)
> - Kalman ŌĆö State-space (1960)
> 
> Ō£ģ **Standard Test Inputs (Laplace Domain)**
> - Unit impulse: $R(s) = 1$
> - Unit step: $R(s) = 1/s$
> - Unit ramp: $R(s) = 1/s^{2}$
> - Unit parabola: $R(s) = 1/s^{3}$
> 
> Ō£ģ **Block Diagram Reduction Rules**
> - **Cascade**: $G_1 G_2$ (multiply)
> - **Parallel**: $G_1 + G_2$ (add)
> - **Feedback loop**: $\dfrac{G}{1 \pm GH}$
> - **Moving a summing junction** behind a block: multiply by block
> - **Moving a takeoff point** ahead of a block: divide by block
> 
> Ō£ģ **Time-Domain Specifications (Preview for Module 2)**
> - Rise time, peak time, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error
> - For 2nd order underdamped: $\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$
> - Settling time: $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta \omega_n)$ (2% criterion)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Control Systems - Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Control Systems - Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control System?

A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired objective. In engineering terms, it is a system that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of other devices or systems using **control loops**.

Mathematically, a control system can be represented as a mapping from an **input (reference / desired output)** to an **output (controlled variable)**. The fundamental goal is to make the output equal to (or track) the desired reference value despite the presence of disturbances, parameter variations, or non-linearities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the distinction between **Open Loop** and **Closed Loop** systems, the role of **feedback**, and the ability to identify the type from real-world engineering examples. This is a **2-marker / 3-marker favorite** in Part A and frequently forms a 7-mark sub-part in Module 1 questions.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Classes of Control Systems

Control systems are broadly classified into two categories based on the presence or absence of **feedback**:

1. **Open Loop Control System** ŌĆö No feedback path; output is not measured.
2. **Closed Loop Control System** ŌĆö Feedback path exists; output is measured and compared with the reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Standard):**
> - **Open Loop System:** *"A system in which the control action is independent of the output is called an open loop control system."*
> - **Closed Loop System:** *"A system in which the control action depends on the output in some way is called a closed loop control system."*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Understanding Intuitively

### Real-World Analogy: The Geyser / Electric Water Heater

Imagine you walk into your bathroom in the morning and want hot water. There are two ways to do it:

**Scenario A ŌĆö Open Loop (No Feedback):**
You set a **timer knob** to "10 minutes" and walk away. The geyser heats water for exactly 10 minutes regardless of whether the water is already piping hot or still lukewarm. There is **no thermometer** monitoring the actual water temperature. If the inlet water was very cold that day, you get lukewarm water. If it was summer and water was already warm, you might get scalding water.

This is an **Open Loop** system ŌĆö the controller (timer) does not "know" the result (temperature).

**Scenario B ŌĆö Closed Loop (With Feedback):**
You set a **thermostat** to 60 ┬░C. A temperature sensor continuously measures the water temperature and feeds this back to the controller. The controller **compares** the measured temperature with the set point (60 ┬░C) and **decides** to keep heating or to stop. The water temperature reaches exactly 60 ┬░C and **stays there**, no matter what the inlet water temperature was.

This is a **Closed Loop** system ŌĆö the controller uses feedback to **self-correct**.

### Other Quick Intuitive Examples

| Application | Open Loop Example | Closed Loop Example |
|---|---|---|
| Washing Machine | Wash timer (fixed 15 min) | Automatic washing machine (senses cloth dirtiness) |
| Traffic Light | Fixed timer (30 s green) | Sensor-based adaptive traffic signal |
| Fan | Regulator at fixed speed (no temperature sensor) | Inverter AC (temperature sensor + feedback) |
| Toaster | Manual timer-based toaster | Automatic bread toaster (uses color/resistance sensor) |
| Vehicle | Accelerator-only (old cars) | Cruise control (compares actual speed to set speed) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question says *"Give an example ofŌĆ”"* in KTU exams, choose an example you understand deeply. The most common KTU answers are: **washing machine (open loop)** and **automatic washing machine / AC / servo position control (closed loop)**.

---

## 1.4 Essential Terminology (KTU Frequently Asked)

Before we go deeper, here are the **standard vocabulary terms** you must know for the 2-mark and 3-mark questions:

- **Plant (Process):** The physical object or system to be controlled. *Example:* Heater, motor, robot arm.
- **Controller:** The device (electronic, mechanical, or computer) that generates the control signal.
- **Actuator:** The element that physically carries out the control action. *Example:* Motor, valve, heater coil.
- **Sensor (Measuring Element):** The device that measures the output. *Example:* Thermistor, tachometer, LVDT.
- **Reference Input (Set Point):** The desired value of the output. *Example:* 60 ┬░C temperature.
- **Controlled Variable (Output):** The actual quantity being regulated. *Example:* Actual water temperature.
- **Error Signal:** The difference between reference input and measured output. Symbol: $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$.
- **Feedback Signal:** The portion of output that is sent back for comparison. Symbol: $b(t) = H(s) \cdot c(t)$.
- **Disturbance:** An unwanted external input that affects the output. *Example:* Cold wind on a heater.
- **Manipulated Variable:** The variable that the controller adjusts to influence the output. *Example:* Voltage to a heater.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open Loop Control System ŌĆö Deep Analysis

### Operational Block Diagram (Textual)

An open loop system has a **single forward path** and **no feedback**. The flow is purely:

$$\text{Reference Input} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator / Plant} \rightarrow \text{Output}$$

There is **no return path**. The output is never measured.

### Mathematical Model

Let the controller transfer function be $G_c(s)$ and the plant transfer function be $G_p(s)$. The total forward path transfer function in an open loop system is:

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

The relationship between reference input $R(s)$ and output $C(s)$ is:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot R(s)$$

### Characteristics of Open Loop Systems

- **No Feedback:** The output is not measured or fed back.
- **Simple Construction:** Fewer components, hence cheaper and easier to design.
- **Stable by Default:** Since there is no feedback loop, **instability due to loop oscillations is impossible** (assuming each individual component is stable).
- **Inaccurate:** Output deviates from desired value when disturbances, parameter drift, or environmental changes occur.
- **Calibration-Sensitive:** The accuracy depends entirely on the **calibration** of the controller.
- **Non-Adaptive:** Cannot self-correct for unexpected changes.

### Advantages of Open Loop Systems

1. Simple in construction and design.
2. Economical (low cost).
3. Easy to maintain ŌĆö fewer components to service.
4. No stability issues.
5. Convenient when output is difficult or expensive to measure.

### Disadvantages of Open Loop Systems

1. **Inaccurate** ŌĆö output differs from desired value.
2. **No disturbance rejection** ŌĆö external disturbances directly affect output.
3. **Sensitive to parameter variations** (e.g., aging of components).
4. **Recalibration required** if conditions change.
5. **Not reliable** for critical applications.

### Real-World Examples of Open Loop Systems

- **Traffic light controller** (fixed timing)
- **Washing machine** with mechanical timer
- **Toaster**
- **Electric hand drier** (fixed heating time)
- **Stepper motor** driving a printer head (no position feedback in some designs)
- **Bread maker** with simple timer
- **Automatic water tap** in some designs (timed flow)

---

## 2.2 Closed Loop Control System ŌĆö Deep Analysis

### Operational Block Diagram (Textual)

A closed loop system has a **forward path** and a **feedback path**. The flow is:

$$\text{Reference Input} \rightarrow \text{Summing Junction} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Plant} \rightarrow \text{Output}$$

A portion of the output is **measured by a sensor** with transfer function $H(s)$, and this **feedback signal** is **subtracted** from the reference input at the **summing junction (error detector)**.

### Mathematical Model ŌĆö Derivation of Closed Loop Transfer Function

Let:
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $E(s)$ = Error signal
- $C(s)$ = Output
- $B(s)$ = Feedback signal = $H(s) \cdot C(s)$
- $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ = Forward path transfer function

**Step 1:** Error signal at summing junction:
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 2:** Forward path equation:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $C(s)$:
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 4:** Closed Loop Transfer Function:
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in the entire Control System course. Memorize it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$
> This is the **closed-loop transfer function (CLTF)** for a **negative feedback** system. For **positive feedback**, the sign in the denominator changes to $1 - G(s)H(s)$.

### Special Case: Unity Feedback

When $H(s) = 1$ (sensor has unity gain, e.g., direct tachometer reading in V/(rad/s) after normalization), the CLTF simplifies to:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

This is the **most commonly tested form** in KTU Module 1 problems.

### Characteristics of Closed Loop Systems

- **Negative Feedback:** Output is compared with reference; error drives the controller.
- **Reduced Error:** Steady-state error can be minimized.
- **Disturbance Rejection:** Feedback naturally rejects disturbances.
- **Increased Bandwidth:** System can respond to a wider range of input frequencies.
- **Improved Accuracy:** Output closely follows the reference.
- **Reduced Sensitivity:** Less affected by parameter variations.
- **Possible Instability:** Closed loop systems **can become unstable** if not properly designed (gain too high, phase margin too small).

### Advantages of Closed Loop Systems

1. **High accuracy** ŌĆö output tracks reference closely.
2. **Excellent disturbance rejection.**
3. **Reduced sensitivity to parameter variations.**
4. **Improved transient response** (with proper design).
5. **Increased bandwidth.**
6. **Self-correcting** in nature.

### Disadvantages of Closed Loop Systems

1. **Complex** in construction and design.
2. **Costly** ŌĆö extra sensors and feedback components.
3. **Stability problems** ŌĆö may oscillate or become unstable.
4. **Maintenance is harder** due to more components.
5. **Reduced overall gain** (because $G/(1+GH) < G$ for positive denominator).

### Real-World Examples of Closed Loop Systems

- **Automatic electric iron** (thermostat feedback)
- **Servomotor position control** (LVDT feedback)
- **Cruise control** in cars
- **Automatic washing machine** (smart sensors)
- **Inverter air conditioner** (temperature feedback)
- **Voltage stabilizer** (servo feedback)
- **Human body temperature regulation** (biological example)
- **Missile guidance system**
- **Robotic arm** with position encoders

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open Loop Transfer Function | $C(s)/R(s) = G(s)$ | No feedback |
| 2 | Closed Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback) | $C(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | **Most important** |
| 3 | Closed Loop Transfer Function (Positive Feedback) | $C(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Unstable if $G(s)H(s) = 1$ |
| 4 | Unity Feedback CLTF | $C(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| 5 | Error Signal | $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ | Unity feedback |
| 6 | Error Signal (General) | $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$ | Non-unity feedback |
| 7 | Feedback Signal | $B(s) = H(s) C(s)$ | ŌĆö |
| 8 | Forward Path Gain | $G(s) = G_c(s) G_p(s)$ | Product of cascaded blocks |
| 9 | Loop Gain | $L(s) = G(s) H(s)$ | Product around the loop |
| 10 | Sensitivity (Closed Loop) | $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T/T}{\partial G/G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Reduction of sensitivity to plant variation |
| 11 | Effect of Disturbance $D(s)$ on Output | $C(s) = \dfrac{G_1(s) G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)} R(s) + \dfrac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)} D(s)$ | Disturbance rejection improves as $G_1 \to \infty$ |
| 12 | Steady-State Error (Unity Feedback) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | Final Value Theorem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Transfer functions are **dimensionless ratios** (output unit / input unit). For example, a motor's $G(s)$ might have units of **rad/s per volt**.

---

## 2.4 Effect of Feedback on System Performance (Deep Concept)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** A 7-mark question often asks *"Discuss the effects of feedback on system performance."* Here is the structured answer KTU examiners expect.

### 1. Effect on Stability
- Feedback can either **stabilize** an unstable open loop system (e.g., using a properly tuned PID) or **destabilize** a stable one (if loop gain is excessive).
- Mathematical condition: A closed loop system is stable if all roots of the **characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ lie in the **left half of the s-plane**.

### 2. Effect on Steady-State Error
- Feedback **reduces** the steady-state error.
- For unity feedback, error: $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.
- As $|G(s)|$ increases, error decreases ŌĆö but gain cannot be increased indefinitely without losing stability.

### 3. Effect on Sensitivity
- The **sensitivity of closed loop transfer function $T$** to variation in plant $G$ is:
$$S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$
- If $|G(s)H(s)| \gg 1$, then $S_G^T \approx \dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)} \to 0$. Closed loop is **less sensitive** to plant variations.

### 4. Effect on Disturbance Rejection
- From the disturbance equation above, the denominator $1 + G_1 G_2 H$ appears in both terms. A **larger loop gain** makes the disturbance effect on output **smaller**.

### 5. Effect on Bandwidth
- Feedback **increases the bandwidth** of the system ŌĆö the system can respond to higher frequency inputs.

### 6. Effect on Overall Gain
- Overall closed loop gain is **less than** the open loop gain. Specifically:
$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} < G(s)$$ 
when $1 + G(s)H(s) > 1$. This is the **trade-off**: we sacrifice some gain for better accuracy and disturbance rejection.

### 7. Effect on Transient Response
- Feedback can be used to **modify** rise time, settling time, and overshoot ŌĆö through **compensator design** (covered in later modules).

---

## 2.5 Comprehensive Comparison Table (KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low / Poor | High |
| Stability | Always stable (no oscillation) | May become unstable |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Disturbance Rejection | None | Excellent |
| Sensitivity to Parameter Variation | High | Low |
| Calibration | Manual, frequent | Automatic |
| Bandwidth | Low | Higher |
| Steady-State Error | Large | Small (can be zero) |
| Reliability | Low | High |
| Examples | Traffic light, washing machine timer | AC, servo motor, cruise control |
| Response Speed | Fast | Slightly slower (sensor delay) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Closed Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

We re-derive the **CLTF** below step-by-step. This is the **most important derivation** in Module 1.

**Consider a standard negative feedback loop with the following elements:**

- Forward path transfer function: $G(s) = G_c(s) G_p(s)$ (controller cascaded with plant)
- Feedback path transfer function: $H(s)$ (sensor gain)
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Feedback signal: $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$
- Error signal: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the error signal equation:**

At the summing junction, the error is the difference between reference and feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the feedback expression:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the forward path equation:**

The output is the product of forward path gain and error signal:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $E(s)$ from Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand the right-hand side:**

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left:**

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Divide both sides to isolate $C(s)/R(s)$:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** This is the **Closed Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)** for a **negative feedback** system. The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **return difference** and setting it to zero gives the **characteristic equation** of the closed loop system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CLTF for Positive Feedback

For **positive feedback**, the summing junction adds instead of subtracts:

$$E(s) = R(s) + B(s) = R(s) + H(s) C(s)$$

Following the same algebraic steps:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

$$C(s) \left[ 1 - G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Positive feedback is **rarely used in control systems** because the system becomes unstable when $G(s)H(s) \to 1$. It is used in **oscillators** (which intentionally produce sustained oscillations).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of CLTF with Disturbance in the Plant

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1.

**Setup:** A plant is split into two blocks $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ in series, with a disturbance $D(s)$ entering **between** them.

**Equations:**

- Error: $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$
- Output contribution from reference: $G_1(s) G_2(s) E(s)$
- Output contribution from disturbance: $G_2(s) D(s)$
- Total output: 
$$C(s) = G_1(s) G_2(s) E(s) + G_2(s) D(s)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $E(s)$:**

$$C(s) = G_1(s) G_2(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \right] + G_2(s) D(s)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand:**

$$C(s) = G_1(s) G_2(s) R(s) - G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s) C(s) + G_2(s) D(s)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Collect $C(s)$ on the left:**

$$C(s) + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s) C(s) = G_1(s) G_2(s) R(s) + G_2(s) D(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Factor:**

$$C(s) \left[ 1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s) \right] = G_1(s) G_2(s) R(s) + G_2(s) D(s)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = \frac{G_1(s) G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)} R(s) + \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)} D(s)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö By superposition**, the two transfer functions are:

$$T_{R}(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \bigg|_{D=0} = \frac{G_1(s) G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)}$$

$$T_{D}(s) = \frac{C(s)}{D(s)} \bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exam:** Both transfer functions have the **same denominator** $1 + G_1 G_2 H$. The disturbance rejection improves as $|G_1|$ increases. This is why controllers are placed **before** the disturbance point (i.e., $G_1$ is the controller, $G_2$ is the plant).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Sensitivity Function

**Definition:** Sensitivity of $T(s)$ to a parameter $x$ is defined as:

$$S_x^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial x / x} = \frac{x}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö CLTF:**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Partial derivative of $T$ with respect to $G$:**

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{ \left[ 1 + G H \right] \cdot 1 - G \cdot H }{ \left[ 1 + G H \right]^2 } = \frac{1 + G H - G H}{ \left[ 1 + G H \right]^2 } = \frac{1}{ \left[ 1 + G H \right]^2 }$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into sensitivity formula:**

$$S_G^T = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{G}{\dfrac{G}{1 + G H}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + G H)^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify:**

$$S_G^T = (1 + G H) \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + G H)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$
> The **larger** $|G(s)H(s)|$ is, the **smaller** the sensitivity ŌĆö meaning closed loop performance is **insensitive** to plant parameter variations.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Sample 7-Mark Problem Style)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A unity feedback control system has an open loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}$. Determine the closed loop transfer function. Comment on the steady-state error for a unit step input.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the form:** Unity feedback means $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply CLTF formula:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the denominator:**

$$1 + \frac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{(s+1)(s+2) + 10}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 2 + 10}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 12}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final CLTF:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)} \cdot \frac{(s+1)(s+2)}{s^2 + 3s + 12} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 12}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Steady-State Error for Unit Step:**

Error transfer function for unity feedback:
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$

Using Final Value Theorem:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{10}{(1)(2)}} = \frac{1}{1 + 5} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The closed loop system has steady-state error of $\dfrac{1}{6}$ for unit step. To reduce this, we would need a higher loop gain ŌĆö but that might affect stability (a topic for Module 2 ŌĆö Routh-Hurwitz criterion).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Open Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["R(s) Reference Input"] --> B["G_c(s) Controller"]
    B --> C["G_p(s) Plant or Process"]
    C --> D["C(s) Output Controlled Variable"]
    D_note["No feedback path ŌĆö output is NOT measured"]
    D ~~~ D_note
```

**Reading the diagram:** The signal flows strictly from left to right. There is **no arrow returning** from output to input. The block $G_c$ represents the controller (e.g., a knob setting), and $G_p$ represents the plant (e.g., a heater coil).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Closed Loop (Negative Feedback) Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["R(s) Reference"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> B["G_c(s) Controller"]
    B --> C["G_p(s) Plant"]
    C --> D["C(s) Output"]
    D --> E["H(s) Feedback Sensor"]
    E --> F["B(s) Feedback Signal"]
    F --> SUM1
    SUM1_label["Summing Junction: E(s) = R(s) - B(s)"]
    SUM1 ~~~ SUM1_label
```

**Reading the diagram:** The summing junction is a circle with `+` and `ŌłÆ` signs. The feedback signal $B(s)$ enters with a **negative sign** (negative feedback). The loop is **closed** by the arrow from $H(s)$ back to the summing junction.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Closed Loop with Disturbance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["R(s) Reference"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> B["G_c(s) Controller"]
    B --> C1["G_1(s) Plant Section 1"]
    C1 --> SUM2(("+"))
    D["D(s) Disturbance"] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> C2["G_2(s) Plant Section 2"]
    C2 --> E["C(s) Output"]
    E --> F["H(s) Sensor"]
    F --> G["B(s) Feedback"]
    G --> SUM1
    D_note["Disturbance enters between plant sections"]
    D ~~~ D_note
```

**Reading the diagram:** Disturbance $D(s)$ enters the system at the point between $G_1$ and $G_2$. This is the standard model used in **disturbance rejection analysis** ŌĆö a 7-mark KTU favorite.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Open Loop vs Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        O1["Set Point r(t)"] --> O2["Controller u(t)"]
        O2 --> O3["Plant y(t)"]
        O3 --> O4["No Measurement Step"]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        C1["Set Point r(t)"] --> C2["Summing Junction: e = r - b"]
        C2 --> C3["Controller u(t)"]
        C3 --> C4["Plant y(t)"]
        C4 --> C5["Sensor b(t)"]
        C5 --> C2
    end
    O4 -.->|Comparison| C5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The key architectural difference is the **closed cycle** in the Closed Loop System. The Open Loop ends at the output. The closed loop **cycles back** to the summing junction.

---

## 4.5 Signal Flow Graph (Mason's Gain Formula Preview)

The Mason's gain formula for CLTF will be studied in detail in Module 2, but here is the conceptual signal flow graph of a basic closed loop:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["R(s)"] -- "1" --> N2(("E(s)"))
    N2 -- "G(s)" --> N3["C(s)"]
    N3 -- "H(s)" --> N4(("B(s)"))
    N4 -- "-1" --> N2
    N3_path["C(s) = G(s) E(s),  E(s) = R(s) - B(s)"]
    N3 ~~~ N3_path
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each branch is labeled with the **transfer function** of the block. The negative sign at the feedback branch enforces negative feedback. Mason's formula will give the same result: $C(s)/R(s) = G/(1+GH)$.
