# Mason's Gain Formula ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mason's Gain Formula (MGF)** is a systematic, topology-based analytical technique used to compute the **overall transfer function (gain)** between any input node and any output node of a **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** without requiring the manual reduction of loops and parallel/series branches.

Formally stated by **Samuel Jefferson Mason** in 1956, the formula is:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \, \Delta_k}{\Delta}
$$

Where every term has a precise topological meaning inside the SFG (explained in Section 2).

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **city road map**. You want to find **every possible route** from your **Home (Input)** to your **Office (Output)**, and you also want to know about **roundabouts (loops)** that might cause traffic to circulate without progressing toward the office.

- Each road segment has a **travel time** (analogous to a branch gain).
- Each complete route from Home to Office is a **forward path** with a **total travel time** (forward path gain $P_k$).
- Each roundabout is a **loop** with its own circulation cost (loop gain $L_i$).
- Some roundabouts are so far apart that **cars on one cannot reach the other** (non-touching loops).
- **Mason's formula** simply adds up all "useful" forward path times, but **discounts** them based on how much "traffic" gets trapped in loops and roundabouts. The discount factor is exactly $\Delta / \Delta_k$.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important fact for KTU: **Mason's formula gives the OVERALL gain in ONE step** without graphical reduction. This is precisely why it is a board-exam favourite.

### Key Terminology You MUST Memorize

- **Node**: A point representing a variable (signal).
- **Branch**: A directed line segment representing a functional dependence (gain).
- **Forward Path**: A path from input to output that does **not cross any node more than once**.
- **Loop**: A closed path that starts and ends at the same node, crossing no other node twice.
- **Non-Touching Loops**: Two (or more) loops that share **no common node**.
- **Path Gain ($P_k$)**: Product of all branch gains along a forward path.
- **Loop Gain ($L_i$)**: Product of all branch gains around a loop.
- **Determinant ($\Delta$)**: $1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$ (sums over all *individual, pairwise non-touching, and triplet non-touching* loop combinations respectively).
- **Cofactor ($\Delta_k$)**: The determinant $\Delta$ evaluated after **removing all loops that touch the $k^{th}$ forward path**.

### Physical / Engineering Relevance

MGF is the **backbone of modern control engineering CAD tools** (MATLAB, Simulink S-functions, VisSim). Whenever a control engineer simulates a multi-loop system with feedback, the solver internally uses Mason's logic. It is also the foundation for **sensitivity analysis**, **stability studies** (via characteristic equation from $\Delta = 0$), and **bond-graph modelling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Signal Flow Graph ŌĆö Building Blocks

A Signal Flow Graph (SFG) is a **directed network of nodes connected by weighted branches**, in which the value at any node equals the **sum of incoming branch gains multiplied by their source-node values**.

For every branch connecting node $x_j \rightarrow x_i$ with transmittance $a_{ij}$:

$$
x_i \;=\; \sum_{j} a_{ij}\, x_j \quad \text{(superposition at each node)}
$$

This compact linear-algebraic representation is exactly what makes MGF possible.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of MGF

> [!NOTE]
> **Always work through these 6 steps in order** ŌĆö examiners award method marks generously when these steps are visibly listed.

1. **Draw (or recognise) the SFG** corresponding to the given block diagram.
2. **Identify ALL forward paths** $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N$ from input $R(s)$ to output $C(s)$.
3. **Identify ALL individual loops** $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_M$ in the SFG.
4. **Identify ALL pairs of non-touching loops** $(L_i, L_j)$ ŌĆö compute each product $L_i L_j$.
5. **Identify ALL triplets of non-touching loops** ŌĆö compute each product $L_i L_j L_k$ (rare in KTU problems but examined).
6. **For every forward path $P_k$**, find $\Delta_k$ by setting to **zero every loop that touches $P_k$** and recomputing $\Delta$.
7. Apply the master formula $T = \frac{\sum P_k \Delta_k}{\Delta}$.

### 2.3 Why Each Term Appears ŌĆö The "Why"

- The **numerator** $\sum P_k \Delta_k$ sums the contribution of each forward path, **discounted by the loops that DO NOT touch it** (since loops touching the path are already "absorbed" into the path's journey).
- The **denominator** $\Delta$ captures the global loop structure and is **independent of the path** ŌĆö it represents the closed-loop characteristic determinant.
- The **alternating signs** $+,-,+,-$ come from the geometric-series-like expansion of feedback paths ŌĆö algebraically derived using Cramer's rule on the SFG node equations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Computational Rule | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_k$ | Forward path gain | Product of branch gains along $k^{th}$ path from input to output | Do not include loops inside a path |
| $L_i$ | Individual loop gain | Product of branch gains around loop $i$ | Sign depends on number of negative feedbacks |
| $L_i L_j$ | Non-touching loop pair product | Product of gains of two loops sharing NO node | Most often missed in KTU scripts |
| $L_i L_j L_k$ | Non-touching triplet product | Product of three mutually non-touching loop gains | Only in 14-mark problems |
| $\Delta$ | Graph determinant | $1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$ | Every term has a sign |
| $\Delta_k$ | Cofactor of $k^{th}$ forward path | $\Delta$ with all loops touching $P_k$ set to **zero** | Common error: forgetting to delete them |
| $T$ | Overall transfer function | $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$ | Always simplify to $T = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention**: A loop with an **odd number of negative-sign branches** contributes **negative** $L_i$. Two such negative loops interacting give **positive** $L_i L_j$. Always count carefully.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Analog/RF circuit analysis**: Topological feedback analysis of two-port networks.
- **Digital signal processing**: Lattice and lattice-ladder filters use SFG representations.
- **State-space modelling**: Transition from state matrices to SFG for visual insight.
- **Control system design**: Root-locus, Nyquist, and Bode plots all implicitly use the $\Delta$ polynomial extracted via MGF.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Algebraic Justification (Cramer's Rule Path)

Consider an SFG with $n$ nodes. The node equations can be written compactly as:

$$
\mathbf{x} \;=\; \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x} \;+\; \mathbf{B}\,u
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})\,\mathbf{x} \;=\; \mathbf{B}\,u
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{x} \;=\; (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\,\mathbf{B}\,u
$$

Applying Cramer's rule to extract any output node $x_j$:

$$
x_j \;=\; \dfrac{\det(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})_j}{\det(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})}\cdot (\text{forcing term})
$$

The numerator determinant **equals** $\sum P_k \Delta_k$ (sum of forward path gains each multiplied by the loops not touching them), and the denominator **equals** $\Delta$. This is precisely Mason's formula ŌĆö derived, not assumed.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example ŌĆö The "Classic KTU Problem"

**Problem.** For the SFG shown in the diagram of Section 4, determine the transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ using Mason's Gain Formula.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Forward Paths

Scanning every distinct path from $R(s)$ to $C(s)$ that does not repeat a node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= G_1 \, G_2 \, G_3 \, G_4 \quad \text{(top route through the cascade)} \\[2pt]
P_2 &= G_5 \, G_6 \, G_7 \quad \text{(bottom route through the cascade)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Identify Individual Loops

Three feedback loops exist:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= -G_2 \, H_1 \\[2pt]
L_2 &= -G_6 \, H_2 \\[2pt]
L_3 &= -G_2 \, G_3 \, G_4 \, G_5 \, H_3 \quad \text{(outer feedback loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Non-Touching Loop Pairs

A pair is non-touching if **no node is common** between the two loops. Visual inspection of the SFG shows that loops $L_1$ and $L_2$ do **not** share any node:

$$
L_1 L_2 \;=\; (-G_2 H_1)(-G_6 H_2) \;=\; G_2 G_6 H_1 H_2
$$

All other loop pairs share at least one node, hence are touching and must be ignored.

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Construct the Graph Determinant $\Delta$

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 \;-\; (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) \;+\; L_1 L_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 \;-\; \bigl(-G_2 H_1 - G_6 H_2 - G_2 G_3 G_4 G_5 H_3\bigr) \;+\; G_2 G_6 H_1 H_2
$$

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 \;+\; G_2 H_1 \;+\; G_6 H_2 \;+\; G_2 G_3 G_4 G_5 H_3 \;+\; G_2 G_6 H_1 H_2
$$

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Cofactors $\Delta_1$ and $\Delta_2$

**Cofactor of $P_1$**: Which loops touch $P_1$? Path $P_1$ traverses nodes through which $L_1$ and $L_3$ pass. So $L_1$ and $L_3$ must be deleted. Only $L_2$ survives:

$$
\Delta_1 \;=\; 1 \;-\; L_2 \;=\; 1 \;+\; G_6 H_2
$$

**Cofactor of $P_2$**: Path $P_2$ touches $L_2$ and $L_3$. Only $L_1$ survives:

$$
\Delta_2 \;=\; 1 \;-\; L_1 \;=\; 1 \;+\; G_2 H_1
$$

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Assemble the Final Transfer Function

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{P_1 \Delta_1 \;+\; P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta}
$$

Substituting each term:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4\,(1 + G_6 H_2) \;+\; G_5 G_6 G_7\,(1 + G_2 H_1)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_6 H_2 + G_2 G_3 G_4 G_5 H_3 + G_2 G_6 H_1 H_2}
$$

This is the **complete analytical answer** expected by KTU examiners.

### 3.3 Quick Sanity Check

If all feedback gains are zero ($H_1 = H_2 = H_3 = 0$), the formula collapses to:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 + G_5 G_6 G_7}{1} \;=\; G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 + G_5 G_6 G_7
$$

This matches the physical intuition of two parallel cascades, confirming correctness.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reference Block-Level Functional Architecture (SFG for the Worked Example)

The Mermaid graph below reproduces the **exact SFG** used in the derivation of Section 3. It contains **8 nodes** and the same forward paths / loops identified above.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR((R)) --> node1((X1))
    node1 --> node2((X2))
    node2 --> node3((X3))
    node3 --> node4((X4))
    node4 --> nodeC((C))

    nodeR --> node5((X5))
    node5 --> node6((X6))
    node6 --> nodeC

    node2 --> nodeH1((H1))
    nodeH1 --> node1

    node6 --> nodeH2((H2))
    nodeH2 --> node5

    node4 --> nodeH3((H3))
    nodeH3 --> node5
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram

- **Forward path P1**: R ŌåÆ X1 ŌåÆ X2 ŌåÆ X3 ŌåÆ X4 ŌåÆ C, with branch gains $G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4$.
- **Forward path P2**: R ŌåÆ X5 ŌåÆ X6 ŌåÆ C, with branch gains $G_5 G_6 G_7$.
- **Loop L1**: X2 ŌåÆ H1 ŌåÆ X1 ŌåÆ X2 (note: this is the inner feedback around $G_2$).
- **Loop L2**: X6 ŌåÆ H2 ŌåÆ X5 ŌåÆ X6 (inner feedback around $G_6$).
- **Loop L3**: X4 ŌåÆ H3 ŌåÆ X5 ŌåÆ ... ŌåÆ X2 ŌåÆ X3 ŌåÆ X4 (large outer loop through $G_3, G_4, G_5, H_3$).
- The loops $L_1$ (uses X1, X2, H1) and $L_2$ (uses X5, X6, H2) **share no common node** ŌĆö hence they are non-touching and contribute the $L_1 L_2$ term in $\Delta$.

### 4.3 Block-Level Conceptual Flow of Mason's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Draw SFG from block diagram] --> stepB[Step 2: List all forward paths Pk]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: List all individual loops Li]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Identify non-touching loop sets]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Compute determinant Delta]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Compute each cofactor Delta k]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Apply T equals sum Pk Delta k over Delta]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**State Mason's Gain Formula. Define all terms involved in it with a neat sketch of a signal flow graph having at least two forward paths and two loops.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Mason's Gain Formula gives the overall transfer function between an input node and an output node of a signal flow graph.
>
> $$
> T \;=\; \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \, \Delta_k}{\Delta}
> $$
>
> Where:
> - $P_k$ is the gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path from input to output.
> - $\Delta$ is the graph determinant: $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$
> - $\Delta_k$ is the cofactor obtained by setting to zero all loops that touch the $k^{th}$ forward path.
> - $L_i, L_j, L_k$ are individual loop gains and non-touching products.
>
> An SFG with two forward paths $G_1 G_2$ and $G_3$, with two loops $L_1 = -G_1 H_1$ and $L_2 = -G_2 H_2$, yields:
>
> $$
> T \;=\; \dfrac{G_1 G_2 \,(1 + G_2 H_2) + G_3 \,(1 + G_1 H_1)}{1 + G_1 H_1 + G_2 H_2 + G_1 G_2 H_1 H_2}
> $$

**Marks Distribution:** Formula 1, Definitions 1, Sketch + application 1.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**What is a non-touching loop? Explain its significance in Mason's Gain Formula with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-touching loops** are two or more loops in a signal flow graph that do **not share any common node**.
>
> **Significance**: They produce the **higher-order correction terms** $\sum L_i L_j$ and $\sum L_i L_j L_k$ inside the determinant $\Delta$. These terms represent the **combined effect** of independent circulating signals, which is **additive** (because each independent loop contributes its own feedback).
>
> **Example**: In an SFG with $L_1 = -G_1 H_1$ (loop around block 1) and $L_2 = -G_2 H_2$ (loop around block 2), if the loops use completely separate nodes, they are non-touching and contribute $+G_1 G_2 H_1 H_2$ inside $\Delta$.
>
> $$
> \Delta \;=\; 1 + G_1 H_1 + G_2 H_2 + G_1 G_2 H_1 H_2
> $$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition 1, Significance 1, Example 1.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Missing non-touching pairs** is the #1 cause of mark loss. Always check pairwise AFTER listing individual loops.
> 2. **Wrong sign** in loop gains ŌĆö every negative feedback branch flips the sign.
> 3. **Forgetting to delete touching loops** when computing $\Delta_k$ ŌĆö leads to inflated denominators.
> 4. **Skipping algebraic simplification** ŌĆö KTU awards a method mark only if the final simplified $T = C(s)/R(s)$ is clearly presented.
> 5. **Treating a forward path with a repeated node as valid** ŌĆö invalid; KTU explicitly penalises this.

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** For the block diagram shown below, draw the equivalent signal flow graph and identify all forward paths and loops.
*System blocks: forward gains $G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4$ in cascade; parallel path $G_5$; feedback gains $H_1$ around $G_1G_2$, $H_2$ around $G_3G_4$, $H_3$ around the parallel path.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence, using Mason's Gain Formula, determine the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

1. **Forward paths** (from input $R(s)$ to output $C(s)$):

$$
P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4, \qquad P_2 = G_5
$$

2. **Individual loops**:

$$
L_1 = -G_1 G_2 H_1, \qquad L_2 = -G_3 G_4 H_2, \qquad L_3 = -G_5 H_3
$$

3. **Non-touching pairs**: $L_1$ and $L_2$ share no node, $L_1$ and $L_3$ share no node, $L_2$ and $L_3$ share no node.

$$
L_1 L_2 = G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 H_1 H_2, \quad L_1 L_3 = G_1 G_2 G_5 H_1 H_3, \quad L_2 L_3 = G_3 G_4 G_5 H_2 H_3
$$

4. **Non-touching triplet**: All three loops are mutually non-touching.

$$
L_1 L_2 L_3 = -G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 G_5 H_1 H_2 H_3
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Forward paths 2 marks, individual loops 2 marks, non-touching pairs 2 marks, triplet 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Construct $\Delta$**:

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 \;-\; (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) \;+\; (L_1 L_2 + L_1 L_3 + L_2 L_3) \;-\; L_1 L_2 L_3
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_3 G_4 H_2 + G_5 H_3 + G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 H_1 H_2 + G_1 G_2 G_5 H_1 H_3 + G_3 G_4 G_5 H_2 H_3 + G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 G_5 H_1 H_2 H_3
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute cofactors**:

- $\Delta_1$: $P_1$ touches ALL three loops ŌåÆ all must be deleted:

$$
\Delta_1 \;=\; 1
$$

- $\Delta_2$: $P_2$ touches only $L_3$ ŌåÆ $L_1$ and $L_2$ survive as a non-touching pair:

$$
\Delta_2 \;=\; 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + L_1 L_2 \;=\; 1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_3 G_4 H_2 + G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 H_1 H_2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Mason's formula**:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 \;+\; G_5 \bigl(1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_3 G_4 H_2 + G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4 H_1 H_2\bigr)}{\Delta}
$$

with $\Delta$ as expanded above.

**Marks Distribution:** $\Delta$ expansion 2 marks, both cofactors 2 marks, substitution + final $T$ 3 marks.

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** A signal flow graph has the following topological data. Determine $\Delta$:
- Forward path: $P_1 = abcd$
- Loops: $L_1 = -abp$, $L_2 = -bcq$, $L_3 = -crs$, $L_4 = -ds$
- Non-touching pairs: $(L_1, L_3)$ and $(L_2, L_4)$.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the cofactor $\Delta_1$ and the overall transfer function $T = P_1 \Delta_1 / \Delta$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sum of individual loops**:

$$
\sum L_i \;=\; L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 \;=\; -abp - bcq - crs - ds
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum of non-touching pairs**:

$$
\sum L_i L_j \;=\; L_1 L_3 + L_2 L_4 \;=\; (abp)(crs) + (bcq)(ds) \;=\; abcrps + bcqds
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö No non-touching triplets** (given) and higher-order terms vanish.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble $\Delta$**:

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j
$$

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1 + abp + bcq + crs + ds + abcrps + bcqds
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Individual loop sum 2 marks, non-touching pairs 2 marks, final $\Delta$ assembly 3 marks.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify loops touching $P_1$**: $P_1 = abcd$ traverses nodes of $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ **all four**.

Therefore, **every loop touches** $P_1$, and all must be set to zero in the cofactor:

$$
\Delta_1 \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into Mason's formula**:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} \;=\; \dfrac{abcd}{1 + abp + bcq + crs + ds + abcrps + bcqds}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification via physical intuition**: When all feedback gains $p, q, r, s \to 0$, $T \to abcd$, confirming correctness.

**Marks Distribution:** Identification of touching loops 2 marks, $\Delta_1$ evaluation 2 marks, final $T$ 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Mason's Gain Formula**

- Ō£ģ Mason's Gain Formula: $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$.
- Ō£ģ **Forward path** = input-to-output, no node repeated.
- Ō£ģ **Loop** = closed path, no node repeated except start/end.
- Ō£ģ **Non-touching** = NO common node between two loops.
- Ō£ģ $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$ (signs alternate).
- Ō£ģ $\Delta_k$ = $\Delta$ with all loops touching $P_k$ set to **zero**.
- Ō£ģ Sign of each $L_i$ is **negative** for negative feedback (odd number of negative branches).
- Ō£ģ Path gain $P_k$ is the **product** of branch gains along that path.
- Ō£ģ Always simplify to $T = C(s)/R(s)$ at the end ŌĆö KTU awards the final simplification mark.
- Ō£ģ Triple non-touching loops are **rare but possible** ŌĆö check pairwise first.
- Ō£ģ Verify by setting all feedback gains to zero ŌĆö the answer must reduce to the open-loop gain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Tip**: In a 14-mark problem, expect **2 forward paths, 3ŌĆō4 individual loops, and 1ŌĆō2 non-touching pairs**. Practice at least **five numerical problems** before the ESE. The formula itself is one line ŌĆö the marks lie in **correct identification of topology**, not in algebraic manipulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routh-Hurwitz Method for Stability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Routh-Hurwitz Method for Stability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a **necessary and sufficient** mathematical procedure that determines the **absolute stability** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system by examining the coefficients of its characteristic equation. It counts the number of roots of the characteristic polynomial that lie in the **right half of the s-plane (RHP)** without requiring the explicit evaluation of those roots.

For a closed-loop system with characteristic equation:

$$\Delta(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

The Routh-Hurwitz method constructs a tabular array (the **Routh Array**) using these coefficients. The number of **sign changes** in the **first column** of this array equals the number of poles in the right-half s-plane.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Mountain Pass" Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Stability = Keeping the Ball in the Valley**
>
> Imagine a ball resting inside a deep valley (the left-half s-plane, where stable poles live). The **characteristic equation** is like the equation that defines the *shape of the terrain*. The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** is the surveyor's rule that checks: *"Are there any hidden bumps or escape ramps on the right side that would let the ball roll out of the valley?"*
>
> - If **all the terrain slopes down toward the valley** ŌåÆ System is **STABLE** (zero sign changes).
> - If there are **upward slopes on the right** ŌåÆ The ball will escape ŌåÆ System is **UNSTABLE** (sign changes present).
> - The number of sign changes tells you **how many escape routes** exist.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Routh-Hurwitz method is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) under the 2024 Scheme for Module 2. Mastery of:
> 1. Routh Array construction
> 2. Special cases (zero first column, row of zeros)
> 3. Finding the **range of K for stability**
>
> ŌĆ”directly fetches full marks in Part B questions.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Poles in LHP (Left Half Plane):** Real part **Žā < 0** ŌåÆ System is **STABLE**.
- **Poles on jŽē-axis:** Marginal / **Critically Stable** (oscillatory).
- **Poles in RHP (Right Half Plane):** Real part **Žā > 0** ŌåÆ System is **UNSTABLE**.
- **Stability Margin (s-domain):** The distance of dominant poles from the jŽē-axis (not directly given by Routh, but conceptually linked).
- **Order of the system (n):** The degree of the characteristic polynomial.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Part Stability Test

The Routh-Hurwitz test is applied in **two stages**:

### Stage 1: Necessary Condition (Hurwitz / Routh's First Condition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Necessary (but not sufficient) Condition for Stability:**
> All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real, non-zero, and have the SAME SIGN** (all positive or all negative). If even one coefficient is **zero** or has an **opposite sign**, the system is **automatically unstable** (or marginally stable).

**Example:** $s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s + 5 = 0$ ŌåÆ The coefficient of $s$ is **$-3$** (negative) ŌåÆ System is **UNSTABLE** without even constructing the array.

### Stage 2: Sufficient Condition (Routh Array Construction)

If the necessary condition is satisfied, construct the **Routh Array** and examine its first column for sign changes.

## 2.2 Routh Array Construction ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

For a general characteristic equation of order $n$:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + a_3 s^{n-3} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

| Row | Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | ... |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s^n$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-1}$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $a_5$ | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | $\dots$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |  |
| $s^0$ | $a_n$ |  |  |  |

### Element Computation Formulas

**Row $s^{n-2}$:**
$$b_1 = \frac{(a_1 \cdot a_2) - (a_0 \cdot a_3)}{a_1}$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(a_1 \cdot a_4) - (a_0 \cdot a_5)}{a_1}$$

**Row $s^{n-3}$:**
$$c_1 = \frac{(b_1 \cdot a_3) - (a_1 \cdot b_2)}{b_1}$$

$$c_2 = \frac{(b_1 \cdot a_5) - (a_1 \cdot b_3)}{b_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Every element in the new row is computed as a **determinant of a 2├Ś2 matrix** divided by the **first element of the row above it**.
>
> General formula:
> $$\text{New Element} = \frac{(\text{Row Above Col 1} \times \text{Row Above-1 Col } i+1) - (\text{Row Above-1 Col 1} \times \text{Row Above Col } i+1)}{\text{Row Above Col 1}}$$

## 2.3 The Stability Conclusion

- **For stability:** All elements in the **first column** must be **strictly positive** (or all strictly negative).
- **Number of RHP poles** = Number of **sign changes** in the first column.
- **If a first-column element is zero:** ŌåÆ Marginal stability or conditional stability (use **╬Ą-method**).
- **If an entire row is zero:** ŌåÆ Symmetric roots about origin (use **auxiliary polynomial**).

## 2.4 Special Cases ŌĆö KTU Examiner's Favourite Traps

### Special Case 1: Zero in the First Column (╬Ą-Method)

> [!WARNING]
> **Procedure:**
> 1. Replace the zero with a small positive number **$\varepsilon$** where $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
> 2. Complete the Routh array.
> 3. Examine signs as $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
> 4. This is the **most common 7-mark sub-part** in KTU ESE.

### Special Case 2: Entire Row of Zeros

> [!NOTE]
> This indicates **symmetric roots about the origin** (e.g., $\pm j\omega$, $\pm \sigma$, $\pm \sigma \pm j\omega$ pairs).
>
> **Procedure:**
> 1. Form the **Auxiliary Polynomial** $A(s)$ from the row **ABOVE** the zero row (use even powers of $s$).
> 2. Differentiate: $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
> 3. Replace the zero row with the coefficients of $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
> 4. Continue the array.

### Special Case 3: Range of K for Stability

A standard KTU problem format: *"Find the range of K for which the system is stable."*

> The characteristic equation is written with K as a parameter. The Routh array is built treating K as a variable. Stability inequalities are then written using the first column conditions: $b_1 > 0, c_1 > 0$, etc. Solving these inequalities gives the **stability range**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Necessary condition | All $a_i > 0$ (or all $< 0$) | Quick elimination check |
| 2 | Routh array first row | $s^n$: $a_0, a_2, a_4, \dots$ | Powers of $s$ |
| 3 | Routh array second row | $s^{n-1}$: $a_1, a_3, a_5, \dots$ | Odd powers of $s$ |
| 4 | Generic element | $b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}$ | Determinant form |
| 5 | Stability condition | First column **all same sign** | Direct conclusion |
| 6 | # RHP poles | # **sign changes** in first column | Key result |
| 7 | ╬Ą-method | Replace $0$ with $\varepsilon \to 0^+$ | Zero in first column |
| 8 | Aux. polynomial | Use row above zero row | Zero row encountered |
| 9 | Derivative rule | $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$ | Replaces zero row |
| 10 | Critical K | Solve first column inequalities | Range of K problem |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Determining safe gain ranges for autopilot control loops.
- **Process Control:** Tuning PID controllers in chemical plants (avoiding instability).
- **Robotics:** Stability of manipulator arm feedback loops.
- **Power Systems:** Generator excitation control stability analysis.
- **Filter Design:** Ensuring active filter circuits do not self-oscillate.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example ŌĆö Building the Routh Array (Order 4)

**Problem:** Determine the stability of a system whose characteristic equation is:

$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$

### Step 1: Verify the Necessary Condition

The coefficients are: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 2, a_2 = 3, a_3 = 4, a_4 = 5$.

All coefficients are **real, non-zero, and positive** Ō£ō ŌåÆ Necessary condition is satisfied. Proceed to construct the Routh array.

### Step 2: Construct the First Two Rows

Since $n = 4$ (even powers of $s$ start the first row):

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |

*(Note: A zero is added to the $s^3$ row at the end for completeness; it does not affect computations.)*

### Step 3: Compute the $s^2$ Row

$$b_1 = \frac{(2 \cdot 3) - (1 \cdot 4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(2 \cdot 5) - (1 \cdot 0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $0$ |

### Step 4: Compute the $s^1$ Row

$$c_1 = \frac{(1 \cdot 4) - (2 \cdot 5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6$$

$$c_2 = \frac{(1 \cdot 0) - (2 \cdot 0)}{1} = \frac{0 - 0}{1} = 0$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $-6$ | $0$ | |

### Step 5: Compute the $s^0$ Row

$$d_1 = \frac{(-6 \cdot 5) - (1 \cdot 0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $-6$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $5$ | | |

### Step 6: Examine the First Column

First column entries: $\{+1, +2, +1, -6, +5\}$

Sign changes:
- $+1 \to +2$ (no change)
- $+2 \to +1$ (no change)
- $+1 \to -6$ (**change 1**)
- $-6 \to +5$ (**change 2**)

**Number of sign changes = 2** ŌåÆ **Two poles in the RHP** ŌåÆ **System is UNSTABLE**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Example ŌĆö Range of K for Stability (with ╬Ą-method)

**Problem:** For the characteristic equation:

$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + K = 0$$

Find the range of $K$ for stability.

### Step 1: Set up the first two rows

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $K$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |

### Step 2: Compute the $s^2$ row

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = 1$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(2)(K) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{2K}{2} = K$$

### Step 3: Compute the $s^1$ row

$$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(K)}{1} = 4 - 2K$$

The $s^1$ row has only one non-zero element (since the row above has only two entries: $1$ and $K$).

### Step 4: Compute the $s^0$ row

$$d_1 = \frac{(4 - 2K)(K) - (1)(0)}{4 - 2K} = K$$

### Step 5: Complete Routh Array

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $K$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $K$ | |
| $s^1$ | $4 - 2K$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

### Step 6: Apply stability conditions (first column must be positive)

From the first column:
1. $1 > 0$ Ō£ō (always)
2. $2 > 0$ Ō£ō (always)
3. $1 > 0$ Ō£ō (always)
4. $4 - 2K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 2$
5. $K > 0$

### Step 7: Combine inequalities

$$0 < K < 2$$

### Step 8: Handle boundary cases (╬Ą-method)

At $K = 0$: characteristic equation becomes $s(s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 4) = 0$ ŌåÆ pole at origin ŌåÆ **marginally stable**.

At $K = 2$: $4 - 2K = 0$ ŌåÆ first column has a **zero** ŌåÆ use ╬Ą-method:

Replace $0$ with $\varepsilon$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $2$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $2$ | |
| $s^1$ | $\varepsilon$ | | |
| $s^0$ | $2$ | | |

First column: $\{+1, +2, +1, +\varepsilon, +2\}$ ŌåÆ all positive ŌåÆ **marginally stable** at $K = 2$ (poles on $j\omega$ axis).

### Final Answer:

$$0 < K < 2 \implies \text{Asymptotically stable}$$
$$K = 0 \text{ or } K = 2 \implies \text{Marginally stable (oscillatory)}$$
$$K < 0 \text{ or } K > 2 \implies \text{Unstable}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Example ŌĆö Special Case: Row of Zeros

**Problem:** Determine the stability of:

$$s^5 + 2s^4 + 24s^3 + 48s^2 - 25s - 50 = 0$$

### Step 1: Set up first two rows

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^5$ | $1$ | $24$ | $-25$ |
| $s^4$ | $2$ | $48$ | $-50$ |

### Step 2: Compute the $s^3$ row

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(24) - (1)(48)}{2} = \frac{48 - 48}{2} = 0$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(2)(-25) - (1)(-50)}{2} = \frac{-50 + 50}{2} = 0$$

**Entire $s^3$ row is zero!**

### Step 3: Form the Auxiliary Polynomial

Use the row above ($s^4$): $2s^4 + 48s^2 - 50$

$$A(s) = 2s^4 + 48s^2 - 50$$

### Step 4: Differentiate

$$\frac{dA(s)}{ds} = 8s^3 + 96s$$

### Step 5: Replace the zero row with coefficients of $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^5$ | $1$ | $24$ | $-25$ |
| $s^4$ | $2$ | $48$ | $-50$ |
| $s^3$ | $8$ | $96$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $24$ | $-50$ | |
| $s^1$ | $112.67$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $-50$ | | |

### Step 6: Count sign changes in first column

$\{+1, +2, +8, +24, +112.67, -50\}$ ŌåÆ **one sign change** ŌåÆ **1 pole in RHP** ŌåÆ **System is UNSTABLE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The row of zeros indicated the presence of **symmetric roots about the origin** (e.g., $\pm j\omega$). Solving $A(s) = 0$ gives $s = \pm j\sqrt{5}$ and $s = \pm j\sqrt{5}$ ŌĆö confirming imaginary axis poles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Case | Symptom in Array | Remedy | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Negative coefficient | First column immediately breaks | System unstable by inspection | Unstable root exists |
| Zero in first column | Row continuation fails | Replace with $\varepsilon$ | Root on $j\omega$ axis |
| Entire row of zeros | Computation stops | Auxiliary polynomial | Symmetric roots about origin |
| All positive first column | No sign changes | System **STABLE** | All poles in LHP |
| $k$ sign changes | $k$ RHP poles | System **UNSTABLE** | $k$ poles in right half |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart ŌĆö The Routh-Hurwitz Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Characteristic Equation delta s] --> B{All coefficients positive and non-zero?}
    B -- No --> C[System is UNSTABLE by inspection]
    B -- Yes --> D[Construct Routh Array Row s^n and Row s to the n-1]
    D --> E[Compute subsequent rows using determinant formula]
    E --> F{Encounter zero in first column?}
    F -- No --> G{Encounter entire row of zeros?}
    F -- Yes --> H[Apply epsilon method: replace 0 with epsilon approaching 0 plus]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Form Auxiliary Polynomial from row above]
    I --> J[Differentiate Auxiliary Polynomial]
    J --> K[Substitute coefficients in place of zero row]
    K --> E
    G -- No --> L[Examine first column of Routh Array]
    L --> M{All first-column entries have same sign?}
    M -- Yes --> N[System is STABLE]
    M -- No --> O[Count number of sign changes in first column]
    O --> P[That many poles lie in RHP: System is UNSTABLE]
    P --> Q[End]
    N --> Q
    C --> Q
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Stability Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Closed Loop Transfer Function] --> A2[Extract Characteristic Equation delta s]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Analysis Stage]
        A2 --> B1[Check Necessary Condition: all coefficients positive]
        B1 --> B2[Build Routh Array s^n, s to n-1]
        B2 --> B3[Compute lower rows using 2x2 determinant rule]
        B3 --> B4{Detect special cases}
    end

    subgraph SPECIAL[Special Case Handler]
        B4 -- Zero first column --> C1[epsilon substitution]
        B4 -- Entire row of zeros --> C2[Auxiliary polynomial and derivative]
        C1 --> B3
        C2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Stage]
        B4 -- None --> D1[Read first column]
        D1 --> D2{Same sign throughout?}
        D2 -- Yes --> D3[Output: STABLE]
        D2 -- No --> D4[Output: UNSTABLE with k RHP poles]
    end

    INPUT --> ANALYSIS
    ANALYSIS --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Routh Array Geometric Layout (3rd Order Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    row4["s^4 row:  a0,    a2,    a4"] --> row3["s^3 row:  a1,    a3,    a5"]
    row3 --> row2["s^2 row:  b1,    b2,    b3"]
    row2 --> row1["s^1 row:  c1,    c2,    c3"]
    row1 --> row0["s^0 row:  d1"]
    row0 --> fc["FIRST COLUMN READ HERE"]
    fc --> decide{Same sign?}
    decide -- Yes --> stable[STABLE]
    decide -- No --> unstable[UNSTABLE - count sign changes]
```
