# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

> **Q:** State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What is the necessary condition for stability of a system using this criterion?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion:** It is a mathematical test that determines the **absolute stability** of an LTI system by checking the coefficients of the characteristic equation. The number of sign changes in the **first column of the Routh Array** gives the number of poles in the **right half of the s-plane (RHP)**.
>
> **Necessary Condition:** All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real, non-zero, and have the same sign** (all positive or all negative). If this is violated, the system is **unstable**.

*Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):*
- Definition: 1.5 marks
- Necessary condition: 1.5 marks

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** What is meant by the "auxiliary polynomial" in the Routh array? When is it used?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ is constructed from the row **immediately above** an entire row of zeros in the Routh array. It contains only the **even powers of $s$** with coefficients from that row. Its derivative $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$ is then used to replace the zero row so that the array can be continued.
>
> **When used:** When a **complete row of zeros** appears in the Routh array, which indicates the existence of **symmetric roots about the origin** (poles on the $j\omega$ axis, or in mirror-image pairs across the origin).

*Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):*
- Definition of aux polynomial: 1.5 marks
- Condition for use: 1.5 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 2 Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7 marks** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Two alternatives are provided; students answer **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the Routh array for the system whose characteristic equation is given by:
> $$s^4 + 3s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s + 4 = 0$$
> Comment on the stability. If unstable, find the number of poles in the RHP.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** For the system with characteristic equation:
> $$s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + K = 0$$
> Determine the range of $K$ for which the system is **asymptotically stable**. Use the $\varepsilon$-method to handle boundary cases.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Verify necessary condition.**
Coefficients: $1, 3, 5, 6, 4$ ŌĆö all positive Ō£ō

**Step 2: Build first two rows.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $5$ | $4$ |
| $s^3$ | $3$ | $6$ | $0$ |

**Step 3: Compute $s^2$ row.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(3)(5) - (1)(6)}{3} = \frac{15 - 6}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(3)(4) - (1)(0)}{3} = \frac{12}{3} = 4$$

**Step 4: Compute $s^1$ row.**

$$c_1 = \frac{(3)(6) - (3)(4)}{3} = \frac{18 - 12}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2$$

**Step 5: Compute $s^0$ row.**

$$d_1 = \frac{(2)(4) - (3)(0)}{2} = 4$$

**Step 6: Complete Routh Array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $5$ | $4$ |
| $s^3$ | $3$ | $6$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $3$ | $4$ | |
| $s^1$ | $2$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $4$ | | |

**Step 7: Examine first column.**

$\{+1, +3, +3, +2, +4\}$ ŌĆö **all positive, no sign changes.**

**Conclusion:** System is **STABLE** (all 4 poles are in the LHP).

*(Allocation: 2 marks array construction, 2 marks element-wise computation, 1 mark conclusion, 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: First two rows.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $3$ | |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $K$ | |

**Step 2: Compute $s^1$ row.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(K)}{2} = \frac{6 - K}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute $s^0$ row.**

$$c_1 = \frac{\left(\frac{6 - K}{2}\right)(K) - (2)(0)}{\frac{6 - K}{2}} = K$$

**Step 4: Apply stability conditions.**

First column: $1, 2, \frac{6 - K}{2}, K$ ŌĆö all must be **positive**.

$$\frac{6 - K}{2} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 6$$

$$K > 0$$

**Step 5: Handle boundary cases with $\varepsilon$-method.**

**At $K = 0$:** $s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s = 0 \Rightarrow s(s^2 + 2s + 3) = 0$ ŌåÆ one pole at origin ŌåÆ **marginally stable**.

**At $K = 6$:** First column becomes $1, 2, 0, 6$. Replace $0$ with $\varepsilon$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $3$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $6$ |
| $s^1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $6$ | |

First column: $\{+1, +2, +\varepsilon, +6\}$ ŌåÆ all positive as $\varepsilon \to 0^+$ ŌåÆ **marginally stable** (poles on $j\omega$ axis).

**Step 6: Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 6 \implies \text{Asymptotically Stable}}$$
$$K = 0 \text{ or } K = 6 \implies \text{Marginally Stable}$$

*(Allocation: 2 marks array, 2 marks inequalities, 2 marks ╬Ą-method, 1 mark final range.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, investigate the stability of a system with characteristic equation:
> $$s^4 + s^3 + 2s^2 + 2s + 3 = 0$$
> Identify any special case encountered and handle it.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:
> $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$
> Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: First two rows.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ |

**Step 2: Compute $s^2$ row.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(1)(2) - (1)(2)}{1} = 0 \quad \text{(ZERO!)}$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(1)(3) - (1)(0)}{1} = 3$$

**Special Case Triggered: Zero in first column!**

**Step 3: Apply $\varepsilon$-method.**

Replace $0$ with $\varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon \to 0^+$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\varepsilon$ | $3$ | |
| $s^1$ | $c_1$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $3$ | | |

**Step 4: Compute $s^1$ row.**

$$c_1 = \frac{(\varepsilon)(2) - (1)(3)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2\varepsilon - 3}{\varepsilon} = 2 - \frac{3}{\varepsilon}$$

As $\varepsilon \to 0^+$: $c_1 \to -\infty$ (negative).

**Step 5: Examine first column.**

$\{+1, +1, +\varepsilon, \text{negative}, +3\}$

Sign changes: $\varepsilon \to (\text{negative})$ ŌåÆ 1 sign change; (negative) ŌåÆ $+3$ ŌåÆ another sign change.

**Total sign changes = 2 ŌåÆ 2 poles in RHP.**

**Conclusion:** System is **UNSTABLE** with **2 poles in the right half of the s-plane**.

*(Allocation: 2 marks array, 2 marks ╬Ą-method application, 2 marks sign analysis, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Form closed-loop characteristic equation.**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

$$s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 2: Necessary condition.**

Coefficients $1, 6, 8, K$ ŌåÆ $K > 0$ required.

**Step 3: Routh Array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

**Step 4: Compute $s^1$ row element.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6}$$

**Step 5: Stability conditions from first column.**

1. $1 > 0$ Ō£ō
2. $6 > 0$ Ō£ō
3. $\frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 48$
4. $K > 0$

**Step 6: Final range.**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48 \text{ for closed-loop stability}}$$

At $K = 0$: pole at origin ŌåÆ marginally stable.
At $K = 48$: first column has a zero ŌåÆ use ╬Ą-method ŌåÆ marginally stable.

*(Allocation: 2 marks char. eqn, 2 marks array, 2 marks inequalities, 1 mark range.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the necessary condition check** (ŌłÆ2 marks): Always verify all coefficients are positive first.
> 2. **Sign errors in the determinant formula** (ŌłÆ3 marks): Remember the order: `(row_above ├Ś row_aboveŌłÆ1+1) ŌłÆ (row_aboveŌłÆ1 ├Ś row_above+1)` divided by `row_above first element`.
> 3. **Not handling zero first column** (ŌłÆ3 marks): When $b_1 = 0$ or any first column entry is 0, MUST use $\varepsilon$-method.
> 4. **Skipping the ╬Ą-method for boundary K** (ŌłÆ2 marks): At $K = K_{critical}$, show the $\varepsilon$ substitution to prove marginal stability.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the physical interpretation** (ŌłÆ1 mark): End with "system is stable/unstable" with reason.
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in $b_1, c_1$ computations**: Use a calculator in the exam hall; one slip cascades.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember (Module 2 ŌĆö Routh-Hurwitz):**
>
> - [x] **Necessary condition:** ALL coefficients must be **real, non-zero, and SAME sign**.
> - [x] **Sufficient condition:** ALL first-column entries of Routh array have **same sign**.
> - [x] **# sign changes in 1st column = # RHP poles.**
> - [x] **Routh array layout:** First row starts with **$s^n$** (even-power coefficients), second row with **$s^{n-1}$** (odd-power coefficients).
> - [x] **Generic element formula:** $\frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}$ (determinant form).
> - [x] **Zero in 1st column** ŌåÆ Use **$\varepsilon$-method**, replace with $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
> - [x] **Row of zeros** ŌåÆ Form **Auxiliary Polynomial** $A(s)$, differentiate, substitute.
> - [x] **Range of K problems:** Stability inequalities from first column ŌåÆ solve simultaneously.
> - [x] **Boundary K values:** Use ╬Ą-method to determine **marginal stability** (poles on $j\omega$-axis).
> - [x] **Marginal stability** = pure imaginary roots (sustained oscillation).
> - [x] **Asymptotic stability** = all roots strictly in LHP.
> - [x] **KTU pattern:** Always end with **"number of sign changes = RHP poles"** and a clear stability statement.
> - [x] **Key takeaway:** Routh-Hurwitz is a **frequency-domain stability test** ŌĆö it never requires finding the roots explicitly.
> - [x] **Limitation:** Only answers *stability* (yes/no/how many RHP), not exact root locations.
> - [x] **Real-world link:** Direct application in **gain selection** for industrial PID controllers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Analysis in Time Domain - Root Locus: Construction of Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability Analysis in Time Domain - Root Locus: Construction of Root Locus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Root Locus** is a graphical method in the complex *s*-plane that depicts the *closed-loop pole trajectories* of a feedback control system as a *single system parameter* (typically the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from $0$ to $\infty$. It was introduced by **Walter R. Evans in 1948** and is the single most important qualitative tool taught under KTU Module 3 for transient and stability analysis in the time domain.

For a standard negative-feedback system, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 + K \cdot \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = 0$$

The root locus is defined as the set of all points in the *s*-plane where the **angle criterion** is satisfied:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

and the corresponding **magnitude criterion** gives the value of $K$ at any point on the locus:

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = \frac{\vert D(s) \vert}{\vert N(s) \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The root locus technique is restricted to single-parameter variation. Although $K$ is the standard varied parameter, the same procedure applies if we vary *any* other system parameter (e.g., a pole-zero location) by proper rearrangement of the characteristic equation into the form $1 + K \cdot F(s) = 0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are driving a car along a winding road, and the steering wheel sensitivity represents the gain $K$ of a cruise-control system.

- At **low steering sensitivity** (small $K$), the car tracks the road gently ŌĆö the *closed-loop poles* lie deep in the *Left-Half Plane (LHP)*, so the response is well-damped and sluggish.
- As you increase the sensitivity, the car starts responding faster ŌĆö poles migrate towards the imaginary axis.
- At some critical sensitivity, the car becomes *marginally stable* ŌĆö poles touch the $j\omega$ axis and you feel oscillations.
- Push the sensitivity further and the car becomes unstable ŌĆö poles cross into the **Right-Half Plane (RHP)**, meaning the response blows up to infinity.

> [!NOTE]
> The **root locus** is essentially the *map of every road the closed-loop poles can take* as $K$ sweeps from $0$ to $\infty$. Stability is simply the question: *"Does any portion of this road ever enter the RHP?"* If no, the system is *closed-loop stable* for that entire range of $K$.

### 1.3 Why Root Locus Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

Root locus is treated as a **high-weightage design and analysis tool** in Module 3 because it:
1. Provides a *qualitative* picture of stability without solving the closed-loop characteristic equation repeatedly.
2. Helps in **gain tuning** ŌĆö finding the maximum allowable $K$ for stability.
3. Allows *damping ratio* $(\zeta)$ and *natural frequency* $(\omega_n)$ estimation by direct geometric measurement on the plot.
4. Serves as the foundation for **lead, lag, and lead-lag compensator design** taught later in the course.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Notation Used in Board Valuation:**
> - Open-loop transfer function: $G(s)H(s)$
> - Variable gain: $K$ (sometimes $K^*$ when factorised in Bode/Evans form)
> - Number of open-loop poles: $P$; Number of open-loop zeros: $Z$
> - Number of root-locus branches: equal to $P$ (the order of the characteristic equation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Principle ŌĆö Angle and Magnitude Conditions

When $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, a point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ lies on the root locus **if and only if** $G(s)H(s)$ evaluated at that point is a *real negative number*. This yields the two **fundamental conditions**:

| Criterion | Equation | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Angle Condition** | $\sum \angle(\text{zeros}) - \sum \angle(\text{poles}) = \pm (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$ | Determines *geometry* of the locus |
| **Magnitude Condition** | $K = \dfrac{\prod \vert s - p_i \vert}{\prod \vert s - z_j \vert}$ | Determines *gain* $K$ at a chosen point |

> [!NOTE]
> The **angle condition** alone is sufficient to draw the locus. The magnitude condition is used *after* the locus is drawn to find the gain at any specific point.

### 2.2 The Eleven Rules of Root Locus Construction (Evans' Procedure)

The KTU board paper typically tests the application of these rules in a **7-mark sub-question**. Memorising the rules in order is the fastest path to full marks.

1. **Number of branches** = number of open-loop poles $P$ (not zeros $Z$).
2. **Symmetry** ŌĆö Always about the real axis (complex roots occur in conjugate pairs).
3. **Starting and ending points** ŌĆö Branches *start* ($K=0$) at the open-loop poles and *end* ($K=\infty$) at the open-loop zeros. $P - Z$ branches go to infinity.
4. **Segments on the real axis** ŌĆö A point on the real axis is part of the locus if the total number of real poles *and* zeros to its **right** is **odd**.
5. **Asymptotes** ŌĆö For the $P - Z$ branches going to infinity, asymptotes are straight lines at angles:
   $$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{P - Z}, \quad q = 0, 1, \dots, (P-Z-1)$$
6. **Centroid (Intersection of Asymptotes)** ŌĆö All asymptotes intersect the real axis at:
   $$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum \text{Real parts of poles} - \sum \text{Real parts of zeros}}{P - Z}$$
7. **Breakaway and Break-in Points** ŌĆö Points on the real axis where multiple locus branches meet, then depart. Found by solving $\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{D(s)}{N(s)}$.
8. **$j\omega$ Crossings** ŌĆö Imaginary axis crossing point found by applying **Routh-Hurwitz** to the characteristic polynomial and finding the value of $K$ that causes a row of zeros.
9. **Angle of Departure (from a complex pole $p_k$)**:
   $$\phi_{d,k} = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(p_k - z_j) - \sum_{i \neq k} \angle(p_k - p_i)$$
10. **Angle of Arrival (at a complex zero $z_k$)**:
    $$\phi_{a,k} = 180^\circ - \sum \angle(z_k - z_j) + \sum \angle(z_k - p_i)$
11. **Total Angle of Locus at a Point** ŌĆö The locus leaves a pole and enters a zero always at $90^\circ$ angles to the asymptote / next branch (for a simple pole/zero).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Angle condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ (2q+1)$ | Used to test if a trial point lies on locus |
| 2 | Magnitude condition | $K = \dfrac{\vert D(s) \vert}{\vert N(s) \vert}$ | $K$ is **always positive** for standard $1+KG(s)H(s)$ |
| 3 | Asymptote angle | $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{P-Z}$ | $q = 0, 1, \dots, P-Z-1$ |
| 4 | Centroid | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{P-Z}$ | Real-valued scalar on real axis |
| 5 | Breakaway point | $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{D(s)}{N(s)}$ | Valid root = lies on real-axis segment |
| 6 | Angle of departure | $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(p_k - z_j) - \sum_{i \neq k} \angle(p_k - p_i)$ | All angles measured *from* the pole |
| 7 | Marginal $K$ | Found from Routh array aux. equation | Row of zeros ŌćÆ $j\omega$ crossing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions in KTU Papers:**
> - $K$ is **dimensionless** unless stated otherwise.
> - Pole-zero locations are given in *rad/s* (or *s$^{-1}$*).
> - All angles must be expressed in **degrees** in the final answer (radians are penalised by ┬Į mark).
> - When the characteristic equation is in the form $1 - K G(s) H(s) = 0$ (positive feedback), the angle condition changes to $\pm 360^\circ \cdot q$ ŌĆö this is the **Complementary Root Locus** (not commonly asked in KTU Module 3 but appears in Part A).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Gain Scheduling** ŌĆö Industrial process controllers (PID loops) are tuned by locating the operating point on the root locus and reading off $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$.
- **Stability Margins** ŌĆö The closest approach of the root locus to the imaginary axis gives the *critical gain $K_{cr}$* and the *critical frequency $\omega_{cr}$*.
- **Lead Compensator Design** ŌĆö Adds a *zero-pole pair* that *pulls* the locus leftward, increasing damping.
- **Robotics & Aerospace** ŌĆö Autopilot design and robotic manipulator controllers rely on Evans' technique to verify gain ranges before deployment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Angle and Magnitude Conditions

Consider the standard unity negative-feedback characteristic equation:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad G(s)H(s) = -1$$

The right-hand side $-1$ is a *real negative number* with magnitude $1$ and angle $\pm 180^\circ, \pm 540^\circ, \dots$, i.e., $(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$. Equating complex quantities:

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \cdot e^{j \angle G(s)H(s)} = 1 \cdot e^{j(2q+1)180^\circ}$$

Separating modulus and phase:

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(Magnitude Condition)}$$

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ \quad \text{(Angle Condition)}$$

Now write $G(s)H(s)$ in pole-zero form with $K$ factored out:

$$G(s)H(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{j=1}^{Z}(s - z_j)}{\prod_{i=1}^{P}(s - p_i)}$$

Substituting into the angle condition:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{Z} \angle(s - z_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{P} \angle(s - p_i) = (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$$

This is the **fundamental angle equation** the student must compute (using all angles in degrees) for any test point $s$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Centroid of Asymptotes

For large $\vert s \vert$, the polynomial ratio approaches the asymptotic behaviour. Using the long-division expansion:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s^Z + a_{Z-1}s^{Z-1} + \dots)}{s^P + b_{P-1}s^{P-1} + \dots}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $s^P$ (assuming $P > Z$):

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s^{P-Z}} \cdot \frac{1 + a_{Z-1}/s + \dots}{1 + b_{P-1}/s + \dots}$$

For $s \to \infty$, only the leading powers matter, and the locus asymptotically approaches the angles where the *phase* of $G(s)H(s)$ is $(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$. This yields:

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{P - Z}$$

The **centroid** comes from considering the next-order correction. The characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ expanded for large $s$ gives:

$$s^{P-Z} + (b_{P-1} - a_{Z-1}) s^{P-Z-1} + \dots = 0$$

Let $m = P - Z$. The roots of the asymptotic polynomial sum to $-(b_{P-1} - a_{Z-1})$ (Vieta's formula). Since all $m$ asymptotic roots are equidistant from the centroid $\sigma_A$ along their respective asymptote directions, the centroid is:

$$\sigma_A = -\frac{\text{sum of coefficients of } s^{m-1} \text{ in char. eq.}}{\text{coefficient of } s^m} = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{P - Z}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Breakaway Point equation $\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$

From the characteristic equation:

$$1 + K \cdot \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = -\frac{D(s)}{N(s)}$$

A breakaway point is a real-axis location where *two poles collide* ŌĆö the function $K(s)$ has a local extremum (a double root of the char. equation). Hence:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{d}{ds}\left[-\frac{D(s)}{N(s)}\right] = 0$$

Applying the quotient rule:

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{D(s)}{N(s)}\right] = \frac{D'(s) N(s) - D(s) N'(s)}{N^2(s)} = 0$$

The denominator is non-zero at the breakaway point, so:

$$D'(s) N(s) - D(s) N'(s) = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **master breakaway equation**. In KTU board exams, students often forget that they must plug each real-root candidate *back* into $K = -D(s)/N(s)$ to confirm $K > 0$. A negative $K$ candidate is *rejected* even if it solves the derivative equation.

### 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Full Root Locus Construction

**Problem (typical KTU 7-mark style):** Sketch the root locus of the system whose open-loop transfer function is:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify poles and zeros.**
Poles: $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = -2$, $p_3 = -4$ ŌåÆ $P = 3$, $Z = 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Number of branches and symmetry.**
Three branches; symmetric about the real axis.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Real-axis segments.**
Count real poles+zeros to the right of any test point:

- Right of $0$: 3 (odd) ŌåÆ locus exists on $(0, \infty)$? No, we test between poles.
- Between $0$ and $-2$: 2 (even) ŌåÆ no locus.
- Between $-2$ and $-4$: 1 (odd) ŌåÆ locus **exists** on $[-4, -2]$.
- Left of $-4$: 3 (odd) ŌåÆ locus **exists** on $(-\infty, -4]$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Asymptotes.**
$P - Z = 3$ asymptotes.

$$\phi_{A,0} = \frac{180^\circ}{3} = 60^\circ, \quad \phi_{A,1} = \frac{3 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 180^\circ, \quad \phi_{A,2} = \frac{5 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 300^\circ$$

Centroid:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4)}{3} = -\frac{6}{3} = -2$$

So the three asymptotes meet at $\sigma = -2$ and go off at $60^\circ$, $180^\circ$, and $300^\circ$ (or $-60^\circ$).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Breakaway point on $[-4, -2]$.**

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$ (expanded from $1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$).

So $K = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)$.

Set $\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

$$3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6}$$

$$s = \frac{-12 \pm 6.928}{6} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s_1 = -0.845, \quad s_2 = -3.155$$

Test $s_1 = -0.845$: lies between $0$ and $-2$ ŌĆö this segment is **not** on the locus, so reject.

Test $s_2 = -3.155$: lies on $[-4, -2]$ ŌĆö **valid breakaway point**. 

Compute $K$ at this point:

$$K = -((-3.155)^3 + 6(-3.155)^2 + 8(-3.155)) = -(-31.42 + 59.73 - 25.24) = -3.07 \approx 3.07$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö $j\omega$ crossing (Routh-Hurwitz).**

Characteristic polynomial: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$.

Routh array:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{48 - K}{6} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}
$$

Marginal stability when the $s^1$ row becomes zero: $K = 48$.

Auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row: $6s^2 + K = 0 \Rightarrow 6s^2 + 48 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -8 \Rightarrow s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$.

So the locus crosses the imaginary axis at $\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ rad/s at $K = 48$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range of $K$ for stability:** $0 < K < 48$. The system is *unstable* beyond $K = 48$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Summary for the plot.**

- Three branches starting at $0$, $-2$, $-4$.
- Branches from $-2$ and $-4$ break away at $s \approx -3.155$, curve into the complex plane, and cross $j\omega$ at $\pm j 2.83$ when $K = 48$.
- The branch from $0$ moves left along the real axis to $-\infty$ along the $180^\circ$ asymptote.
- Two branches go to infinity at $\pm 60^\circ$ from the centroid $\sigma = -2$.

### 3.5 Angle-of-Departure Example (3-Pole System with Complex Pole)

For a system with poles at $-1$, and $-2 \pm j 2$ (typical 7-mark KTU problem), the angle of departure from the complex pole $p_2 = -2 + j 2$ is computed as:

$$\phi_{d,2} = 180^\circ + \sum \angle(p_2 - z_j) - \sum_{i \neq 2} \angle(p_2 - p_i)$$

With no zeros ($Z=0$) and other pole $p_1 = -1$:

$$\angle(p_2 - p_1) = \angle((-2+j2) - (-1)) = \angle(-1 + j2) = \arctan\left(\frac{2}{-1}\right) = 180^\circ - 63.43^\circ = 116.57^\circ$$

(Using the convention that angle is measured in standard position; the point lies in Q2.)

The angle contribution from the *conjugate* pole $p_3 = -2 - j2$ is *automatically* $180^\circ$ (a horizontal line to the right of $p_2$).

$$\phi_{d,2} = 180^\circ + 0^\circ - 116.57^\circ - 180^\circ = -116.57^\circ$$

Equivalently, the locus leaves $p_2$ heading at $\phi \approx 116.57^\circ$ below the negative real axis (or $360^\circ - 116.57^\circ = 243.43^\circ$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the conjugate pole contributes exactly $180^\circ$:** The vector from $p_2$ to $p_3$ is a purely *vertical* (downward) line ŌĆö the angle of any purely vertical line is $\pm 90^\circ$ or $180^\circ$ from the horizontal. The vector from $-2 + j2$ to $-2 - j2$ points straight *down*, so its angle is $-90^\circ$ or equivalently $270^\circ$ ŌĆö but as part of the *denominator* sum, the convention yields $180^\circ$. This shortcut is a common KTU valuation trap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Closed-Loop Control System Showing Root Locus Context

```mermaid
graph TD
    refA["Reference Input R s"]
    sumA["Summing Junction"]
    ctrlA["Controller Gc s"]
    plantA["Plant Gp s"]
    fbA["Feedback Sensor H s"]
    outA["Output Y s"]
    gainA["Variable Gain K"]
    sPlane["s plane Root Locus"]
    stabilityA["Stability Check"]
    refA --> sumA
    sumA --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> gainA
    gainA --> plantA
    plantA --> outA
    outA --> fbA
    fbA --> sumA
    plantA --> sPlane
    sPlane --> stabilityA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Evans' 11-Step Construction Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["Step 1 Identify P poles and Z zeros"]
    step2["Step 2 Count P branches on real axis symmetry"]
    step3["Step 3 Mark real axis segments by odd count rule"]
    step4["Step 4 Compute asymptote angles phiA"]
    step5["Step 5 Compute centroid sigmaA on real axis"]
    step6["Step 6 Solve dK by ds equals 0 for breakaway points"]
    step7["Step 7 Build Routh array for j omega crossings"]
    step8["Step 8 Compute angle of departure for complex poles"]
    step9["Step 9 Compute angle of arrival for complex zeros"]
    step10["Step 10 Plot branches in s plane"]
    step11["Step 11 Mark critical K and stability region"]
    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11
```

### 4.3 Mermaid s-Plane Schematic ŌĆö Root Locus for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["Open Loop Pole at s equals 0"]
    p2["Open Loop Pole at s equals minus 2"]
    p3["Open Loop Pole at s equals minus 4"]
    brk["Breakaway Point at s equals minus 3.155"]
    cent["Centroid sigma equals minus 2"]
    asymp1["Asymptote at 60 degrees"]
    asymp2["Asymptote at 180 degrees"]
    asymp3["Asymptote at 300 degrees"]
    jw["j omega crossing at plus minus j 2.83"]
    p1 --> asymp2
    p2 --> brk
    p3 --> brk
    cent --> asymp1
    cent --> asymp3
    brk --> jw
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block is a **block-level functional representation** of the $s$-plane layout. For the *actual* root locus sketch required in KTU answer scripts, students must draw the figure manually on **graph paper** with proper axes ($\sigma$ and $j\omega$), arrowheads indicating the direction of increasing $K$, and all critical points labelled. Hand-drawn plots get full credit; computer-printed plots are usually *not* accepted unless the question specifically allows CAD tools.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Block-Level Architecture of a Root-Locus-Based Design Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    specA["Performance Spec zeta and omega_n"]
    locA["Desired Pole Location in s plane"]
    locusA["Existing Root Locus"]
    compA["Compensator Required if locus misses"]
    addA["Add zero pole pair to reshape locus"]
    verifyA["Re-draw and verify new locus passes through target"]
    specA --> locA
    locA --> locusA
    locusA --> compA
    compA --> addA
    addA --> verifyA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This compensator-design flow is a *preview* of Module 4/5 content but reinforces *why* we study root locus: it is not an end in itself but a **design map** on which compensator zeros and poles are placed to "pull" the locus toward the desired closed-loop pole location.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Type)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, 3 Marks]**
*Define root locus. State any four rules for constructing the root locus on the s-plane.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The root locus is the locus (path) traced by the closed-loop poles of a feedback control system in the *s*-plane as a *single system parameter* (usually gain $K$) varies from $0$ to $\infty$, while all other parameters remain fixed.

**Four Construction Rules:**

1. **Number of branches** = number of open-loop poles $P$.
2. **Symmetry** about the real axis (complex roots occur in conjugate pairs).
3. **Real-axis segments** are part of the locus if the total number of real poles and zeros to the *right* of the test segment is **odd**.
4. **Asymptotes:** For the $P - Z$ branches going to infinity, the asymptote angles are $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{P - Z}$, intersecting the real axis at the centroid $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{P - Z}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, 3 Marks]**
*What is the angle criterion for the root locus? Why is the magnitude condition not required while sketching the locus?*

**Model Answer:**

**Angle Criterion:** A point $s$ in the *s*-plane lies on the root locus if and only if the angle of $G(s)H(s)$ evaluated at $s$ is an odd multiple of $180^\circ$:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ, \pm 540^\circ, \dots$$

Mathematically:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{Z} \angle(s - z_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{P} \angle(s - p_i) = (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$$

**Why magnitude is not required to sketch:** The angle condition alone determines the *geometric shape* (path) of the locus ŌĆö for every test point we can verify if the angle equation is satisfied. The magnitude condition gives the *value of $K$* at a chosen point, which is computed *after* the locus is drawn, not while constructing it. Hence the locus shape is independent of $K$ and depends only on pole-zero geometry.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Full Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Sample Paper*

For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**a)** Sketch the complete root locus. Identify the asymptote angles, centroid, breakaway/break-in points, and $j\omega$ crossing. (7 Marks)

**b)** Find the range of $K$ for which the system is stable. Also determine $K$ such that the damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify poles and zeros.**
Poles: $p_1 = 0, p_2 = -2, p_3 = -3$. Zero: $z_1 = -1$. So $P = 3$, $Z = 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Real-axis segments.**
Test point to the right of $0$: 3 poles + 1 zero = 4 (even) ŌåÆ no.
Between $0$ and $-1$: 3 poles + 1 zero = 4 (even) ŌåÆ no.
Between $-1$ and $-2$: 3 poles (odd) ŌåÆ **yes, locus exists**.
Between $-2$ and $-3$: 2 poles (even) ŌåÆ no.
Left of $-3$: 3 poles (odd) ŌåÆ **yes, locus exists**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Asymptotes.**
$P - Z = 2$ asymptotes.

$$\phi_{A,0} = \frac{180^\circ}{2} = 90^\circ, \quad \phi_{A,1} = \frac{540^\circ}{2} = 270^\circ \equiv -90^\circ$$

**Centroid:**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-3) - (-1)}{2} = \frac{-5 + 1}{2} = -2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Breakaway/break-in points.**
Characteristic equation: $s(s+2)(s+3) + K(s+1) = 0$
$\Rightarrow s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s + K(s+1) = 0$
$\Rightarrow s^3 + 5s^2 + (6+K)s + K = 0$

So $K = -\dfrac{s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s}{s + 1}$.

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(3s^2 + 10s + 6)(s+1) - (s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s)(1)}{(s+1)^2} = 0$$

Numerator simplification:

$$3s^3 + 3s^2 + 10s^2 + 10s + 6s + 6 - s^3 - 5s^2 - 6s = 0$$

$$2s^3 + 8s^2 + 10s + 6 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 3 = 0$$

Trial: $s = -1$: $-1 + 4 - 5 + 3 = 1 \neq 0$. Trial: $s = -3$: $-27 + 36 - 15 + 3 = -3 \neq 0$.

This cubic has one real root approximately $s \approx -2.32$ (between $-2$ and $-3$ ŌĆö not on the locus since this segment has *even* count ŌåÆ reject) and a pair of complex conjugates (also rejected since breakaway must occur on the real axis).

Hence **no valid breakaway point on the real axis**. The two real-axis branches (between $-1$ and $-2$, and left of $-3$) simply *meet the asymptotes* smoothly.

**Step 5 ŌĆö $j\omega$ crossing.**
Routh array on $s^3 + 5s^2 + (6+K)s + K = 0$:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & (6+K) \\
s^2 & 5 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{5(6+K) - K}{5} = \dfrac{30 + 4K}{5} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}
$$

For marginal stability, $s^1$ row = 0: $30 + 4K = 0 \Rightarrow K = -7.5$.

Since $K = -7.5 < 0$, the system is **stable for all $K > 0$** ŌĆö the locus never crosses the $j\omega$ axis for positive $K$. The asymptotic branches depart at $\pm 90^\circ$ and stay in the LHP.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Stability range:** $K > 0$ (no upper bound; system is stable for all positive gains).

**Damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$:** A line of constant $\zeta$ in the *s*-plane is a straight line through the origin at angle:

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^\circ$$

So the desired pole lies on a line in the LHP at $60^\circ$ below the negative real axis. Let the pole be $s = -\sigma + j\omega$ with $\omega/\sigma = \tan(60^\circ) = \sqrt{3}$, so $\omega = \sqrt{3} \sigma$.

The pole must lie on the root locus, so it must satisfy the characteristic equation:

$$s^3 + 5s^2 + (6+K)s + K = 0$$

Substitute $s = -\sigma + j\sqrt{3}\sigma$ and separate real and imaginary parts. For the imaginary part to vanish, we get a relation between $K$ and $\sigma$, and the real part gives the second equation. Solving simultaneously yields $K \approx 1.0$ and $\sigma \approx 0.4$, giving a desired pole at $s \approx -0.4 + j0.69$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $K > 0$** when solving $\frac{dK}{ds} = 0$. A negative $K$ candidate means the "breakaway" is on the *complementary* root locus (positive feedback), and full credit is denied.
> 2. **Mixing up $\phi_A$ formula**: Some students use $\phi_A = \frac{360^\circ q}{P-Z}$ (Bode plot formula) instead of the root-locus $(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$. This is the single most common error.
> 3. **Conjugate-pole angle contribution**: Many students forget the contribution of the *conjugate* pole when computing angle of departure. Always include the vector from the departure pole to *every* other pole and zero.
> 4. **No graph paper plot**: A 7-mark construction sub-question with *only* numerical answers (no diagram) typically gets at most 4ŌĆō5 marks. The plot is worth at least 2ŌĆō3 marks by itself.
> 5. **Centroid on wrong side of the poles**: If your computed centroid is positive when all poles are negative, you have a sign error. Recheck the sum.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Style*

For a unity feedback control system with:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s^2 + 4s + 13)}$$

**a)** Sketch the complete root locus for $K \geq 0$. Show the angle of departure from the complex poles, asymptotes, centroid, and the $j\omega$-axis crossing. (7 Marks)

**b)** Determine the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability and the marginal frequency of oscillation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify poles and zeros.**
$s(s^2 + 4s + 13) = 0$ gives $p_1 = 0$ and $p_{2,3} = -2 \pm j3$. So $P = 3$, $Z = 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Real-axis segment.**
Right of $p_1 = 0$: 3 poles (odd) ŌåÆ locus exists on $(-\infty, 0]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Asymptotes.**
$P - Z = 3$ asymptotes at $60^\circ$, $180^\circ$, $300^\circ$. Centroid:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{0 + (-2) + (-2)}{3} = -\frac{4}{3} \approx -1.33$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Angle of departure from $p_2 = -2 + j3$.**

Conjugate contribution from $p_3 = -2 - j3$: vector is purely vertical (downward), giving $180^\circ$ to the sum.

Contribution from $p_1 = 0$:
$\angle(p_2 - p_1) = \angle(-2 + j3) = 180^\circ - \arctan(3/2) = 180^\circ - 56.31^\circ = 123.69^\circ$.

Sum of pole contributions (excluding $p_2$): $180^\circ + 123.69^\circ = 303.69^\circ$.

No zeros, so:

$$\phi_d = 180^\circ - 303.69^\circ = -123.69^\circ \equiv 236.31^\circ$$

(Equivalently, $360^\circ - 123.69^\circ = 236.31^\circ$, meaning the locus leaves $p_2$ heading *down-right*, eventually curving back into the RHP because of the asymptote direction.)

**Step 5 ŌĆö $j\omega$ crossing.**
Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 4s^2 + 13s + K = 0$.

Routh array:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 13 \\
s^2 & 4 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{52 - K}{4} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}
$$

Marginal stability: $K = 52$. Auxiliary equation: $4s^2 + 52 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -13 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{13} \approx \pm j 3.61$ rad/s.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Stability range:** $0 < K < 52$. For $K = 52$ the system is marginally stable, oscillating at $\omega = \sqrt{13}$ rad/s. For $K > 52$, two complex poles migrate into the RHP, causing instability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] Root locus is the *path* of closed-loop poles as **one parameter** varies.
- [ ] Angle condition is the *only* requirement for sketching; magnitude gives the gain value.
- [ ] **Branches** = number of open-loop poles (not zeros).
- [ ] **Real-axis rule**: locus exists where the count of poles + zeros to the right is **odd**.
- [ ] **Asymptote angle**: $\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{P-Z}$ (not $360^\circ q$ ŌĆö that's a Bode formula).
- [ ] **Centroid**: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{P-Z}$.
- [ ] **Breakaway point**: solve $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{D(s)}{N(s)}$; *verify $K > 0$* and *verify the point lies on the real-axis locus segment*.
- [ ] **$j\omega$ crossing**: always use the **Routh-Hurwitz** array on the characteristic polynomial; the auxiliary equation gives the crossing frequency.
- [ ] **Angle of departure/arrival** must include the contribution of *all* other poles and zeros (including the conjugate).
- [ ] **Stability from root locus**: the system is stable for $K$ in the range for which the entire locus lies in the LHP.
- [ ] **Common mark-loss traps**: missing graph-paper plot, wrong sign in $K$, forgetting conjugate-pole contribution, mixing up $\phi_A$ and centroid formulas.
- [ ] **Plot direction**: branches move from poles ($K=0$) to zeros ($K=\infty$); arrowheads on the plot point *away* from the poles.
- [ ] **Damping ratio** is read directly from a line through the origin at angle $\cos^{-1}(\zeta)$.
- [ ] **Natural frequency** $\omega_n$ equals the *modulus* of the pole distance from the origin: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega^2}$.
- [ ] For positive feedback or "complementary root locus", the angle condition changes to $\pm 360^\circ q$ ŌĆö explicitly check which case the question demands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway:** *Root locus converts a tedious algebraic problem of solving the characteristic equation for every $K$ into a single elegant picture ŌĆö drawing it correctly is half the battle won.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Addition of Poles and Zeros - Frequency Domain Analysis: Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Addition of Poles and Zeros - Frequency Domain Analysis: Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** is the steady-state response of a system to a sinusoidal input signal of varying frequency. It is characterized by the **magnitude ratio** $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ and the **phase angle** $\angle G(j\omega)$ as functions of the input frequency $\omega$.

In control system theory, the frequency response of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ into the transfer function $G(s)$, yielding $G(j\omega)$. The resulting complex-valued function describes how the system amplifies (or attenuates) and phase-shifts sinusoidal inputs at each frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ŌĆö Effect of Pole/Zero Addition:**
> - **Adding a pole** to an existing transfer function $G(s)$ results in a new transfer function $G_1(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{(1 + s/a)}$, where $1/a$ is the **time constant** and $a$ is the **corner frequency** (in rad/s).
> - **Adding a zero** to an existing transfer function $G(s)$ results in a new transfer function $G_1(s) = (1 + s/a) \cdot G(s)$, where $a$ is again the **corner frequency**.
> - These additions fundamentally reshape the asymptotic Bode magnitude and phase plots, especially in the vicinity of the corner frequency $a$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Audio Equalizer:**
> Think of a transfer function as an **audio equalizer** in a music system:
> - The **baseline equalizer settings** represent the original transfer function $G(s)$, shaping the sound (system response) at different frequencies.
> - **Adding a Pole** is like installing a **low-pass filter knob** ŌĆö it **reduces (attenuates) the gain** at higher frequencies, making the sound "softer" or "duller" at the high end. The corner frequency $a$ determines *where* the attenuation begins.
> - **Adding a Zero** is like installing a **high-boost or treble knob** ŌĆö it **increases (amplifies) the gain** at higher frequencies, making the sound "brighter" or "sharper" at the high end.
> - The **phase shift** corresponds to the **timing delay** introduced ŌĆö poles add phase lag (delay), zeros add phase lead (advance).

This analogy maps directly to the Bode plot, where poles "roll off" the magnitude at $-20$ dB/decade past the corner frequency and contribute $-90┬░$ of phase lag, while zeros "roll up" the magnitude at $+20$ dB/decade past the corner frequency and contribute $+90┬░$ of phase lead.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Corner Frequency ($a$ or $\omega_c$):** The frequency at which the asymptotic Bode plot changes slope. Measured in **rad/s** (or Hz, where $f_c = a / 2\pi$).
- **Magnitude Slope:** A simple pole contributes $-20$ dB/decade; a simple zero contributes $+20$ dB/decade.
- **Phase Contribution:** A simple pole contributes $0┬░ \to -90┬░$ (centered at corner); a simple zero contributes $0┬░ \to +90┬░$ (centered at corner).
- **Maximum Phase Deviation:** At the corner frequency $a$, the actual magnitude deviates from the asymptotic plot by $\pm 3$ dB, and the phase is exactly at half its final value ($\pm 45┬░$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Breakdown ŌĆö Effect of Adding a Pole

When a pole is added to the open-loop transfer function:

$$G_1(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + s/a}$$

**Why this happens (Physical Reasoning):**
- A pole in the left-half plane (LHP) corresponds to a **natural mode** of the system that decays over time.
- Adding a pole introduces a new energy-storage or dissipative element (e.g., a capacitor in an RC circuit).
- This new mode **slows down** the system, reduces high-frequency gain, and adds phase lag.

**How it manifests in the Bode plot:**

| Frequency Range | Magnitude Change | Phase Change |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------|
| $\omega \ll a$ (one decade below) | $0$ dB (negligible) | $\approx 0┬░$ |
| $\omega = a$ (at corner) | $-3$ dB (deviation) | $-45┬░$ |
| $\omega \gg a$ (one decade above) | $-20$ dB/decade slope | $\approx -90┬░$ |

### 2.2 Systematic Breakdown ŌĆö Effect of Adding a Zero

When a zero is added to the open-loop transfer function:

$$G_1(s) = (1 + s/a) \cdot G(s)$$

**Why this happens (Physical Reasoning):**
- A zero in the LHP corresponds to a **feedforward** or **derivative-like** action.
- Adding a zero introduces a "preview" or anticipatory response ŌĆö the system responds *before* the input fully changes.
- This **speeds up** the transient response, boosts high-frequency gain, and adds phase lead.

**How it manifests in the Bode plot:**

| Frequency Range | Magnitude Change | Phase Change |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------|
| $\omega \ll a$ (one decade below) | $0$ dB (negligible) | $\approx 0┬░$ |
| $\omega = a$ (at corner) | $+3$ dB (deviation) | $+45┬░$ |
| $\omega \gg a$ (one decade above) | $+20$ dB/decade slope | $\approx +90┬░$ |

### 2.3 Composite Effects (Multiple Poles and Zeros)

> [!NOTE]
> **Superposition Principle:** When multiple poles and zeros are added, the net effect on the Bode plot is the **algebraic sum** of individual contributions. The net magnitude slope at any frequency equals the sum of individual slopes, and the net phase is the sum of individual phase contributions.

For a transfer function of the general form:

$$G(s) = \frac{K \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{m}(1 + s/z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(1 + s/p_j)}$$

The asymptotic high-frequency slope is:

$$\text{Slope} = 20(n - m) \text{ dB/decade}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Pole factor | $1/(1 + s/a)$ | $a$ = corner frequency in **rad/s** |
| 2 | Zero factor | $(1 + s/a)$ | $a$ = corner frequency in **rad/s** |
| 3 | Magnitude of pole factor | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Magnitude of zero factor | $\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Phase of pole factor | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$ | In **degrees** or **radians** |
| 6 | Phase of zero factor | $+\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$ | In **degrees** or **radians** |
| 7 | Slope per pole | $-20$ **dB/decade** | Beyond corner frequency |
| 8 | Slope per zero | $+20$ **dB/decade** | Beyond corner frequency |
| 9 | Phase per pole | $-90┬░$ total (asymptotic) | Distributed over 2 decades around $a$ |
| 10 | Phase per zero | $+90┬░$ total (asymptotic) | Distributed over 2 decades around $a$ |
| 11 | Error at corner (pole) | $-3$ **dB** | Actual vs. asymptotic magnitude |
| 12 | Error at corner (zero) | $+3$ **dB** | Actual vs. asymptotic magnitude |
| 13 | Phase at corner (pole) | $-45┬░$ | Exact value of actual phase |
| 14 | Phase at corner (zero) | $+45┬░$ | Exact value of actual phase |
| 15 | Decade conversion | $1$ decade $= 20\log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB | Logarithmic frequency scale |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The effect-of-poles-and-zeros analysis is critical in:
> - **Compensator design** (lead, lag, lead-lag networks) in feedback control.
> - **Filter design** in signal processing (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass).
> - **Stability analysis** via Bode plots (gain/phase margins).
> - **Audio engineering** (tone shaping, equalization).
> - **Antenna and RF system design** (bandwidth shaping).

### 2.5 Stability Implications

> [!WARNING]
> Adding poles generally **degrades stability margins** (reduces phase margin, may reduce gain crossover frequency). Adding zeros in strategic locations can **restore or improve stability**. This trade-off is the foundation of **compensator design** in classical control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Magnitude Response of a Simple Pole Factor

**Starting Point:** The pole factor is given by:

$$H_p(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega/a}$$

**Step 1:** Express the complex number in polar form. The denominator has:
- Real part: $1$
- Imaginary part: $\omega/a$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude of the denominator using the Pythagorean theorem for complex numbers:

$$\vert 1 + j\omega/a \vert = \sqrt{(1)^2 + (\omega/a)^2} = \sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the magnitude of the pole factor is:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to decibels:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using logarithm properties:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = -20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the power rule for logarithms:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/a)^2\right]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Asymptotic Approximation for $\omega \ll a$:**
When $\omega \ll a$, the term $(\omega/a)^2 \ll 1$, so:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert_{dB} \approx -10 \log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Asymptotic Approximation for $\omega \gg a$:**
When $\omega \gg a$, the term $(\omega/a)^2 \gg 1$, so:

$$\vert H_p(j\omega) \vert_{dB} \approx -10 \log_{10}\left[(\omega/a)^2\right] = -20 \log_{10}(\omega/a)$$

This is a straight line with slope $-20$ dB/decade, intersecting $0$ dB at $\omega = a$.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Exact Value at $\omega = a$:**

$$\vert H_p(ja) \vert_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}(1 + 1) = -10 \log_{10}(2) = -3.01 \text{ dB}$$

This confirms the $-3$ dB deviation at the corner frequency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Phase Response of a Simple Pole Factor

**Starting Point:**

$$H_p(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega/a}$$

**Step 1:** The phase of a complex ratio is the phase of the numerator minus the phase of the denominator:

$$\angle H_p(j\omega) = \angle(1) - \angle(1 + j\omega/a)$$

**Step 2:** The phase of the real constant $1$ is $0┬░$. The phase of $(1 + j\omega/a)$ is:

$$\angle(1 + j\omega/a) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega/a}{1}\right) = \tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\angle H_p(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Asymptotic Behavior:**
- For $\omega \ll a$ (by factor of 10 or more): $\tan^{-1}(\omega/a) \approx 0┬░$, so $\angle H_p \approx 0┬░$
- For $\omega \gg a$ (by factor of 10 or more): $\tan^{-1}(\omega/a) \approx 90┬░$, so $\angle H_p \approx -90┬░$
- At $\omega = a$: $\angle H_p = -\tan^{-1}(1) = -45┬░$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Linear Asymptotic Approximation:**
The phase transitions linearly (in log-frequency) from $0┬░$ at $\omega = a/10$ to $-90┬░$ at $\omega = 10a$, passing through $-45┬░$ at $\omega = a$.

The slope of this transition is:

$$\text{Phase slope} = \frac{-90┬░ - 0┬░}{\log_{10}(10a) - \log_{10}(a/10)} = \frac{-90┬░}{2 \text{ decades}} = -45┬░/\text{decade}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Magnitude Response of a Simple Zero Factor

**Starting Point:**

$$H_z(j\omega) = 1 + j\omega/a$$

**Step 1:** Magnitude in polar form:

$$\vert H_z(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + (\omega/a)^2} = \sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels:

$$\vert H_z(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (\omega/a)^2} = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/a)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Asymptotic Behavior:**
- For $\omega \ll a$: $\vert H_z \vert_{dB} \approx 0$ dB
- For $\omega \gg a$: $\vert H_z \vert_{dB} \approx 20 \log_{10}(\omega/a)$ ŌĆö slope of $+20$ dB/decade
- At $\omega = a$: $\vert H_z \vert_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(2) = +3.01$ dB

---

### 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö Phase Response of a Simple Zero Factor

**Step 1:** By analogous reasoning to the pole case:

$$\angle H_z(j\omega) = +\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Asymptotic Behavior:**
- For $\omega \ll a$: $\angle H_z \approx 0┬░$
- For $\omega \gg a$: $\angle H_z \approx +90┬░$
- At $\omega = a$: $\angle H_z = +45┬░$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Summary of Effects

| Action | On Magnitude Plot (Low $\omega$) | On Magnitude Plot (At $a$) | On Magnitude Plot (High $\omega$) | On Phase Plot (Total Swing) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Add Pole at $a$ | $0$ dB (no change) | $-3$ dB | Slope $\to -20$ dB/dec | $0┬░ \to -90┬░$ (lag) |
| Add Zero at $a$ | $0$ dB (no change) | $+3$ dB | Slope $\to +20$ dB/dec | $0┬░ \to +90┬░$ (lead) |
| Add Pole at $b$ ($b \ll a$) | Starts rolling off early | $-3$ dB at $b$ | Slope becomes more negative | Adds $-90┬░$ lag centered at $b$ |
| Add Zero at $b$ ($b \gg a$) | No change | No change | Boosts magnitude | Adds $+90┬░$ lead centered at $b$ |

---

### 3.6 Derivation ŌĆö General Transfer Function with $n$ Poles and $m$ Zeros

**Starting Point:** Consider a general transfer function with $n$ poles at $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ and $m$ zeros at $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_m$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{m}(1 + j\omega/z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(1 + j\omega/p_j)}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total Magnitude in dB:**

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert K \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{m} 10\log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/z_i)^2\right] - \sum_{j=1}^{n} 10\log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/p_j)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total Phase:**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \tan^{-1}(\omega/z_i) - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \tan^{-1}(\omega/p_j)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö High-Frequency Asymptotic Slope:**

For $\omega \gg \max(p_j, z_i)$, all terms $(\omega/p_j)^2 \gg 1$ and $(\omega/z_i)^2 \gg 1$:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} \approx 20\log_{10}\vert K \vert + 20m \log_{10}(\omega) - 20n \log_{10}(\omega) + \text{constant}$$

The slope in dB/decade is:

$$\text{Slope}_{HF} = 20(m - n) \text{ dB/decade}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Low-Frequency Asymptotic Slope:**
For $\omega \ll \min(p_j, z_i)$:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} \approx 20\log_{10}\vert K \vert$$

This is a constant (flat line) in the low-frequency region.

---

### 3.7 Worked Example ŌĆö Combined Effect

**Problem:** Given $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine the asymptotic Bode plot characteristics.

**Step 1:** Convert to time-constant form. Divide numerator and denominator to get corner frequencies:

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s \cdot 2(1 + s/2) \cdot 5(1 + s/5)} = \frac{10}{10s(1 + s/2)(1 + s/5)} = \frac{1}{s(1 + s/2)(1 + s/5)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify poles and corner frequencies:
- Pole at origin (integrator): contributes $-20$ dB/dec slope starting from $\omega = 0$
- Pole at $p_1 = 2$ rad/s: additional $-20$ dB/dec slope for $\omega > 2$
- Pole at $p_2 = 5$ rad/s: additional $-20$ dB/dec slope for $\omega > 5$

**Step 3:** Initial slope (low frequency):
The integrator $1/s$ gives a slope of $-20$ dB/dec, passing through $0$ dB at $\omega = 1$ rad/s.

**Step 4:** Magnitude expression:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(\omega) - 10\log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/2)^2\right] - 10\log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega/5)^2\right]$$

**Step 5:** Verify at $\omega = 1$ rad/s (low frequency):

$$\vert G(j1) \vert_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(1) - 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ dB} \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Slope changes:
- $0 < \omega < 2$: Slope = $-20$ dB/dec
- $2 < \omega < 5$: Slope = $-40$ dB/dec
- $\omega > 5$: Slope = $-60$ dB/dec

**Step 7:** Total phase at $\omega = 1$ rad/s:

$$\angle G(j1) = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(1/2) - \tan^{-1}(1/5)$$
$$= -90┬░ - 26.57┬░ - 11.31┬░ = -127.88┬░$$

    B -->|Add Zero at corner frequency a| D[New G1 of s equals 1 plus s over a times G of s]
    B -->|Add Both| E[Composite System with N poles and M zeros]
    C --> F[Analyze Magnitude Bode Plot]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Analyze Phase Bode Plot]
