# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** State the Nyquist stability criterion. Explain the significance of the critical point $(-1, j0)$.

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist stability criterion states that the number of closed-loop poles $Z$ in the right-half $s$-plane is given by

$$Z = N + P$$

where $N$ is the number of clockwise encirclements of the point $(-1, j0)$ by the Nyquist plot of $G(s)H(s)$, and $P$ is the number of open-loop poles in the RHP. For stability, $Z = 0$, hence $N = -P$. The critical point $(-1, j0)$ corresponds to the condition $G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -1$, i.e., magnitude $= 1$ and phase $= -180┬░$. If the Nyquist plot passes through this point, the closed-loop system exhibits sustained oscillations (marginally stable). **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** Define gain margin and phase margin. What are the typical minimum values for a well-designed system?

**Model Answer:**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** The factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Mathematically,

$$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}, \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$$

- **Phase Margin (PM, $\gamma$):** The additional phase lag that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before instability:

$$\gamma = 180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

Typical minimum design values: **$GM \geq 6$ dB** and **$\gamma \geq 30┬░$** for adequate relative stability and acceptable transient response. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024]
> **(a)** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

> draw the Nyquist plot and find:
> 1. The range of $K$ for closed-loop stability. **(4 marks)**
> 2. The value of $K$ for which the system is marginally stable. **(3 marks)**

> **(b)** For the same system with $K = 20$, determine the **gain margin** and **phase margin** using the Bode plot method. Comment on the stability. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

#### Part (a)(1) ŌĆö Range of $K$ for Stability

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{pc}$** (phase $= -180┬░$):

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{5}\right) = -180┬░$$

$$\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{5}\right) = 90┬░$$

Use identity: $\tan^{-1}(A) + \tan^{-1}(B) = 90┬░$ when $AB = 1$ (and both positive):

$$\dfrac{\omega}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega}{5} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega^2 = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+4}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2+25}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{14}\sqrt{35}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{10 \cdot 14 \cdot 35}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{4900}} = \dfrac{K}{70}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Stability Condition:** $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert < 1$:

$$\dfrac{K}{70} < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{K < 70}$$

#### Part (a)(2) ŌĆö Marginal Stability

At $K = 70$, $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$, so the Nyquist plot passes through $(-1, j0)$ ŌĆö the system is **marginally stable** with sustained oscillation at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$ rad/s. **(3 marks)**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö GM and PM for $K = 20$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{pc}$ (same as before):** $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute GM:**

$$GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \dfrac{70}{20} = 3.5$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3.5) \approx 10.88 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{gc}$** (where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$):

$$\dfrac{20}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+4}\sqrt{\omega^2+25}} = 1$$

$$\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+4}\sqrt{\omega^2+25} = 20$$

Solve iteratively:

- $\omega = 1$: $1 \cdot \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{26} \approx 11.40$ ŌĆö too small
- $\omega = 1.5$: $1.5 \cdot \sqrt{6.25} \cdot \sqrt{27.25} \approx 1.5 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 5.22 \approx 19.57$ ŌĆö too small
- $\omega = 1.55$: $1.55 \cdot \sqrt{6.4025} \cdot \sqrt{27.40} \approx 1.55 \cdot 2.53 \cdot 5.235 \approx 20.53$ Ō£ō

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.55$ rad/s.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**

$$\phi_{gc} = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.55}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.55}{5}\right) = -90┬░ - 37.78┬░ - 17.22┬░ = -145┬░$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute PM:**

$$\gamma = 180┬░ + (-145┬░) = 35┬░$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Comment:** Both $GM_{dB} \approx 10.88$ dB $> 0$ and $\gamma \approx 35┬░ > 0┬░$, so the system is **stable** with **good relative stability**. **(7 marks)**

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
> **(a)** Explain the relationship between the Nyquist plot and the Bode plot. How are gain margin and phase margin obtained from each? **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A unity feedback system has

$$G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.05s)}$$

> Determine using the Bode stability criterion:
> 1. Gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$. **(3 marks)**
> 2. Phase margin. **(4 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Nyquist vs. Bode Relationship

The Nyquist plot and Bode plot are two representations of the **same** open-loop frequency response $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$:

| **Nyquist Plot** | **Bode Plot** |
|---|---|
| Single plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ in the complex plane as $\omega: 0 \to \infty$ | Two separate plots: $\vert GH \vert_{dB}$ vs. $\log\omega$ and $\angle GH$ vs. $\log\omega$ |
| Stability: count encirclements of $(-1, j0)$ | Stability: $\omega_{gc} < \omega_{pc}$ (magnitude crosses 0 dB before phase crosses $-180┬░$) |
| $GM = 1/\vert GH \vert$ at point where plot crosses negative real axis | $GM_{dB} = -\vert GH \vert_{dB}$ at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| $PM = 180┬░ + \angle GH$ at unit magnitude radius | $PM = 180┬░ + \angle GH$ at $\omega_{gc}$ (0 dB crossover) |
| Applicable to non-minimum-phase and time-delay systems | Easier to construct by asymptotic approximations |

**Key Relationship:** For any frequency, the distance of the Nyquist plot from the origin equals the magnitude $\vert GH \vert$, and the angle from the positive real axis equals the phase $\angle GH$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Bode Stability Criterion for Given System

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(1+0.5j\omega)(1+0.05j\omega)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct Bode Magnitude:**

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(10) - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 20\log_{10}\sqrt{1+(0.5\omega)^2} - 20\log_{10}\sqrt{1+(0.05\omega)^2}$$

- Low frequency ($\omega \ll 1$): $-20\log_{10}\omega$ (slope $-20$ dB/dec)
- At $\omega = 2$ rad/s (corner from $0.5s$): slope changes to $-40$ dB/dec
- At $\omega = 20$ rad/s (corner from $0.05s$): slope changes to $-60$ dB/dec

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $\omega_{gc}$** (where $\vert G \vert = 1$, i.e., 0 dB):

Asymptotic magnitude near $\omega_{gc}$ (between corner frequencies 2 and 20 rad/s) follows slope $-40$ dB/dec through the point $\omega = 2$, $\vert G \vert_{dB} \approx 20 - 20\log_{10}(2) = 20 - 6 = 14$ dB.

To drop from 14 dB at $\omega = 2$ to 0 dB at slope $-40$ dB/dec:

$$\Delta_{dB} = 14, \quad \text{ratio of frequencies} = 10^{14/40} = 10^{0.35} \approx 2.24$$

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 2 \times 2.24 = 4.47 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Refined answer:** $\omega_{gc} \approx 4.47$ rad/s. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \cdot 4.47) - \tan^{-1}(0.05 \cdot 4.47)$$

$$= -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(2.235) - \tan^{-1}(0.2235)$$

$$= -90┬░ - 65.9┬░ - 12.6┬░ = -168.5┬░$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Phase Margin:**

$$\gamma = 180┬░ + (-168.5┬░) = 11.5┬░$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Stability Comment:** $\gamma \approx 11.5┬░ > 0$, so the system is **stable but poorly damped** with low relative stability. Compensator design is recommended. **(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $N$:** Many students treat clockwise encirclements as positive, but in the standard Ogata/KTU convention, **CW = negative**. Reversing this single sign flips the entire stability verdict.
> 2. **$P$ counting:** Only count open-loop poles in the RHP. Poles at the origin (on the imaginary axis) require special **indented contour treatment** and are NOT counted in $P$ directly.
> 3. **Phase formula:** At $\omega_{gc}$, the phase margin is $180┬░ + \phi_{gc}$ where $\phi_{gc}$ is **already negative** ŌĆö adding 180┬░ gives the remaining margin, not the full angle.
> 4. **Bode asymptotic vs. exact:** At corners and near crossover, the asymptotic approximation deviates by up to 3 dB. For KTU numerical problems, **always verify with exact magnitude** at $\omega_{gc}$ and adjust if needed.
> 5. **Gain margin units:** Report $GM$ as both a **dimensionless ratio** and in **dB** unless the question specifies otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Stability in the Frequency Domain**

- Ō£ģ **Nyquist Criterion** is the only test that works for **open-loop unstable systems** ($P \neq 0$) and **time-delay systems**.
- Ō£ģ **Formula:** $Z = N + P$; for stability, $Z = 0 \Rightarrow N = -P$.
- Ō£ģ **Critical point** is $(-1, j0)$; encirclements of **origin** by $1 + GH$ equal encirclements of $(-1, j0)$ by $GH$.
- Ō£ģ **Gain Margin (GM)** measured at **phase crossover $\omega_{pc}$** where $\angle GH = -180┬░$.
- Ō£ģ **Phase Margin (PM, $\gamma$)** measured at **gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$** where $\vert GH \vert = 1$ (0 dB).
- Ō£ģ **Bode stability** for minimum-phase systems: magnitude crosses 0 dB **before** phase crosses $-180┬░$.
- Ō£ģ **Both GM and PM must be positive** for a stable system (necessary but not sufficient for non-minimum-phase systems).
- Ō£ģ **Conditional stability** occurs when the Nyquist plot encircles $(-1, j0)$ multiple times ŌĆö system may go unstable for *both* gain increase and decrease.
- Ō£ģ **$N$ convention:** Clockwise = negative, Counter-clockwise = positive (Ogata convention).
- Ō£ģ **Indentation rule:** Always indent Nyquist contour around OL poles on the imaginary axis.
- Ō£ģ **Typical design targets:** $GM \geq 6$ dB, $\gamma \geq 30┬░$ to $60┬░$ for good relative stability and acceptable overshoot.
- Ō£ģ **PM and overshoot correlation:** Small PM ŌćÆ large overshoot; $\gamma \approx 45┬░$ gives roughly $25\%$ overshoot.
- Ō£ģ **Frequency-domain methods work on experimental data** ŌĆö no mathematical model of plant required (used heavily in practice via spectrum analyzers and network analyzers).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nyquist Stability Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Stability Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nyquist Stability Criterion** is a frequency-domain graphical technique used in classical control theory to determine the stability of a closed-loop control system by examining the open-loop frequency response $G(s)H(s)$ plotted on the complex $s$-plane (specifically the Nyquist plot). The criterion is formally stated as:

> **Cauchy's Principle of Argument Theorem Application:** The number of closed-loop poles in the right-half of the $s$-plane ($Z$) equals the number of open-loop poles in the right-half $s$-plane ($P$) plus the number of clockwise encirclements of the critical point $-1 + j0$ by the Nyquist plot ($N$).

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$Z = N + P$$

where $N$ is the number of **clockwise encirclements** of the point $-1 + j0$ by the complete Nyquist plot of $G(s)H(s)$, and $Z$ is the number of zeros of the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ in the right-half plane (which equals the number of closed-loop unstable poles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Nyquist criterion is the only frequency-domain method that works for **open-loop unstable systems** and systems with **pure time delays (transport lag)**, making it indispensable for process control applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box - Characteristic Equation:** The closed-loop characteristic equation of a unity feedback system is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. The roots of this equation determine the closed-loop stability. The Nyquist criterion establishes a relationship between the open-loop transfer function and the closed-loop poles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **hiker standing at the origin (0,0)** of a complex plane, and a meandering river (the Nyquist plot) flows around the landscape. There is a dangerous cliff at the point $-1 + j0$ (the critical point). The river's path is plotted as the frequency $\omega$ sweeps from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

- If the river (plot) **winds clockwise** around the cliff $-1 + j0$ a certain number of times, you can deduce how many closed-loop poles have been pushed into the dangerous (unstable) right-half plane.
- If the plot **does not encircle** $-1 + j0$, and the open-loop system is already stable, the closed-loop system remains stable.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a thermostat controlling room temperature. The Nyquist plot tells the engineer whether, under all operating frequencies (slow drift to fast oscillation), the feedback loop will cause the temperature to **oscillate uncontrollably (instability)** or **settle smoothly to the desired setpoint (stability)**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following bolded metrics are standard in Nyquist analysis:

- **Critical Point:** $-1 + j0$ (the point to be encircled, derived from the characteristic equation $1 + G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = 0$).
- **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$):** The frequency at which the magnitude $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ (i.e., $0\text{ dB}$).
- **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$):** The frequency at which the phase angle $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$ (i.e., $-180^\circ$ crossing).
- **Gain Margin (GM):** $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$.
- **Phase Margin (PM):** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Foundation

The Nyquist criterion is rooted in **Cauchy's Principle of Argument** from complex analysis, which states:

> If a contour $\Gamma_s$ in the $s$-plane encircles $Z$ zeros and $P$ poles of a function $F(s)$, then the corresponding contour $\Gamma_F = F(s)$ in the $F(s)$-plane will encircle the origin $N = Z - P$ times in the **clockwise** direction.

For the closed-loop characteristic equation $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$:
- Zeros of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ = Closed-loop poles
- Poles of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ = Open-loop poles (since poles of $G(s)H(s)$ are also poles of $1 + G(s)H(s)$)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Nyquist Criterion

1. **Construct the open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$ and identify its right-half plane poles ($P$).
2. **Draw the Nyquist contour** in the $s$-plane:
   - The imaginary axis from $-j\infty$ to $+j\infty$
   - A semicircle of infinite radius in the right-half plane
3. **Map this contour** through $G(s)H(s)$ to obtain the **Nyquist plot** in the $GH$-plane.
4. **Count encirclements** $N$ of the critical point $-1 + j0$:
   - **Clockwise encirclements:** Positive ($+$)
   - **Counter-clockwise encirclements:** Negative ($-$)
5. **Apply the formula** $Z = N + P$ to determine the number of closed-loop poles in the right-half plane.
6. **Conclude stability:** The system is stable if and only if $Z = 0$.

### 2.3 Variations of the Encirclement Formula

| Condition | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| General case | $Z = N + P$ | $N$ = CW encirclements of $-1 + j0$ |
| Open-loop stable ($P = 0$) | $Z = N$ | No encirclements $\Rightarrow$ closed-loop stable |
| Open-loop unstable ($P \neq 0$) | $Z = N + P$ | $N$ must be $-P$ for stability |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Term** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Closed-loop characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Determines closed-loop stability |
| Nyquist Stability Equation | $Z = N + P$ | $Z$ = closed-loop RHP poles, $N$ = CW encirclements, $P$ = open-loop RHP poles |
| Stability Condition | $Z = 0$ | System is stable |
| Gain Margin (in dB) | $GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \right)$ | Measured in **dB** |
| Phase Margin | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Measured in **degrees** |
| Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ | **rad/s** |
| Gain Crossover Frequency | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ (i.e., $0$ dB) | **rad/s** |
| Mapping Equation | $G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \text{Re} + j\text{Im}$ | Frequency substitution $s = j\omega$ |
| Minimum distance to $-1 + j0$ | $\vert 1 + G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ | Vector from $-1 + j0$ to plot point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention for KTU Exams:** The convention $Z = N + P$ uses $N$ as **clockwise encirclements (positive)**. If your textbook uses the opposite convention (CCW positive), the formula becomes $Z = P - N$. **Always check the sign convention** explicitly in your answer script.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Nyquist Stability Criterion is extensively used in:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Analyzing flight control systems where open-loop dynamics may be unstable (e.g., high-performance aircraft pitch dynamics).
- **Process Control:** Chemical reactors with significant **time delays** (transport lag $e^{-T_ds}$) that cannot be analyzed using Routh-Hurwitz.
- **Power Systems:** Determining stability margins of grid-connected inverters and synchronous generators.
- **Servo Systems:** Designing position control systems for robotics and CNC machines.
- **Active Suspension Systems:** Vehicle dynamics where the open-loop plant is marginally stable or unstable.

The criterion is **uniquely powerful** because it provides both **absolute stability** (yes/no) and **relative stability** (how far from instability, via GM and PM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Stability Criterion from Cauchy's Argument Principle

Let $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$ be the closed-loop characteristic polynomial. By definition:
- **Zeros of $F(s)$** are the **closed-loop poles**
- **Poles of $F(s)$** are the **open-loop poles** of $G(s)H(s)$ (since the added constant $1$ does not introduce new poles)

Let the Nyquist contour $\Gamma_s$ enclose the entire right-half $s$-plane. By Cauchy's Argument Principle:

$$N = Z - P$$

where $N$ is the number of **counter-clockwise** encirclements of the origin by the mapped contour $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$.

The origin of the $F(s)$-plane corresponds to the point $-1 + j0$ in the $G(s)H(s)$-plane (since $F(s) = 0 \Rightarrow G(s)H(s) = -1$). Therefore, the encirclements of the origin by $F(s)$ equal the encirclements of $-1 + j0$ by $G(s)H(s)$.

Rearranging for clockwise encirclements (KTU convention):

$$Z = N + P$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Gain Margin and Phase Margin

#### Gain Margin (GM) Derivation:

At the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$, the phase angle equals $-180^\circ$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

This means the vector $G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})$ lies on the **negative real axis**. Its distance from the origin is $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.

For the plot to just touch the critical point $-1 + j0$, the magnitude must equal $1$. The factor by which the gain can be increased before instability is the **Gain Margin**:

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

In decibels:

$$GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

#### Phase Margin (PM) Derivation:

At the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$, the magnitude equals $1$ (i.e., $0$ dB):

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

The point on the plot at $\omega_{gc}$ lies on the **unit circle**. The phase margin is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated before the phase reaches $-180^\circ$:

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

> **Note:** $PM > 0$ indicates phase lag tolerance; $PM < 0$ indicates instability.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix - Example Application

**Problem:** Determine the stability of a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}, \quad K = 10$$

#### Step 1: Identify Open-Loop RHP Poles ($P$)

The poles of $G(s)$ are at $s = 0$, $s = -1$, $s = -2$. All poles are in the left-half plane or on the imaginary axis.

$$P = 0$$

#### Step 2: Substitute $s = j\omega$ and Find Frequency Response

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$$

#### Step 3: Separate into Real and Imaginary Parts

Expanding the denominator:

$$(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2) = -\omega^2 + 3j\omega + 2$$

Multiplying by $j\omega$:

$$j\omega(-\omega^2 + 3j\omega + 2) = -3\omega^2 + j(2\omega - \omega^3)$$

Therefore:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{(-3\omega^2) + j(2\omega - \omega^3)}$$

Rationalizing by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10[(-3\omega^2) - j(2\omega - \omega^3)]}{(-3\omega^2)^2 + (2\omega - \omega^3)^2}$$

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-30\omega^2 - j(20\omega - 10\omega^3)}{9\omega^4 + 4\omega^2 - 4\omega^4 + \omega^6}$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$9\omega^4 + 4\omega^2 - 4\omega^4 + \omega^6 = \omega^6 + 5\omega^4 + 4\omega^2 = \omega^2(\omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4)$$

Factoring $\omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4 = (\omega^2 + 1)(\omega^2 + 4)$.

Thus:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-30\omega^2 - j(20\omega - 10\omega^3)}{\omega^2(\omega^2 + 1)(\omega^2 + 4)}$$

#### Step 4: Find Phase Crossover Frequency

The imaginary part equals zero when $20\omega - 10\omega^3 = 0$:

$$\omega(20 - 10\omega^2) = 0 \Rightarrow \omega^2 = 2 \Rightarrow \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 5: Find Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2}$

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{2}) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{4}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{24}} = \frac{10}{2\sqrt{6}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{6}}$$

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{2}) \vert \approx 2.041$$

#### Step 6: Calculate Gain Margin

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \frac{1}{2.041} \approx 0.49$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.49) \approx -6.2 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 7: Stability Conclusion

Since $GM_{dB} < 0$ dB (or equivalently, the Nyquist plot encircles $-1 + j0$ **clockwise** at least once), we have $N = 2$ (two clockwise encirclements for this Type-1 system). Thus:

$$Z = N + P = 2 + 0 = 2$$

The closed-loop system has **2 poles in the right-half plane** and is **unstable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Nyquist Stability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open-Loop TF GsHs] --> B[Identify Open-Loop RHP Poles: P]
    B --> C[Substitute s equals j omega]
    C --> D[Compute GH as Function of omega]
    D --> E[Plot Nyquist Diagram: -infinity to +infinity]
    E --> F[Mirror Image: omega less than 0]
    F --> G[Count Encirclements N of -1 plus j0]
    G --> H[Apply Formula: Z equals N plus P]
    H --> I{Z equals 0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[System is STABLE]
    I -->|No| K[System is UNSTABLE with Z RHP Poles]
    J --> L[Compute GM and PM for Relative Stability]
    K --> M[Redesign Controller or Reduce Gain]
```

### 4.2 Nyquist Plot Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin 0,0] --> B[Unit Circle radius 1]
    B --> C[Critical Point: -1 + j0]
    C --> D{Nyquist Plot Path}
    D -->|Does NOT encircle| E[Stable Region]
    D -->|Encircles CW| F[Unstable Region: Z greater than 0]
    D -->|Touches exactly| G[Marginally Stable: Z equals 0 at boundary]
    E --> H[GM greater than 0 dB, PM greater than 0 deg]
    F --> I[GM less than 0 dB, PM less than 0 deg]
    G --> J[GM equals 0 dB, PM equals 0 deg]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Nyquist Analysis

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Domain: s-plane]
        A1[Open-Loop Transfer Function GsHs] --> A2[Identify Poles and Zeros]
        A2 --> A3[Determine Open-Loop Stability: P]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Mapping Process]
        B1[Substitute s equals j omega] --> B2[Compute Real Part Re GH]
        B2 --> B3[Compute Imaginary Part Im GH]
        B3 --> B4[Plot Polar Curve]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Domain: GH-plane]
        C1[Complete Nyquist Plot] --> C2[Mirror for Negative Frequencies]
        C2 --> C3[Count Encirclements N around -1 + j0]
        C3 --> C4[Apply Z equals N plus P]
        C4 --> C5{Stability Decision}
    end
    INPUT --> PROCESS
    PROCESS --> OUTPUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the Nyquist Stability Criterion. What is the significance of the critical point $-1 + j0$?*

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist Stability Criterion states that the number of closed-loop poles of the system in the right-half of the $s$-plane is given by $Z = N + P$, where $N$ is the number of **clockwise encirclements** of the critical point $-1 + j0$ by the complete Nyquist plot of $G(s)H(s)$, and $P$ is the number of open-loop poles in the right-half $s$-plane. The system is stable if and only if $Z = 0$.

The critical point $-1 + j0$ is significant because it represents the point where the closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ is satisfied. The proximity of the Nyquist plot to this point indicates the relative stability of the system.

**(3 marks: 1 for stating formula, 1 for stability condition, 1 for significance of critical point)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. What do negative values of GM and PM indicate?*

**Model Answer:**
**Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the system becomes unstable. Mathematically, $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$, measured in dB as $GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.

**Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency before instability. $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

**Negative GM (in dB)** indicates that the system is already unstable (gain is too high), and the gain must be reduced to stabilize. **Negative PM** indicates that the phase lag has already exceeded the critical $-180^\circ$ at unity gain, making the closed-loop system unstable.

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for GM, PM, and significance of negative values)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Consider a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s-1)}, \quad K > 0$$

Using the Nyquist stability criterion, determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the above system, compute the Gain Margin and Phase Margin when $K = 1$, and comment on the relative stability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Range of $K$ for Stability:**

**Step 1: Identify open-loop poles.**
The open-loop poles are at $s = 0$ and $s = +1$. Since $s = +1$ is in the right-half plane:

$$P = 1$$

**Step 2: Find the characteristic equation.**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s-1)} = 0$$

$$s(s-1) + K(s+3) = 0$$

$$s^2 - s + Ks + 3K = 0$$

$$s^2 + (K-1)s + 3K = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply Routh-Hurwitz to verify.**

| $s^2$ | $1$ | $3K$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s^1$ | $K-1$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $3K$ | |

For stability: $K - 1 > 0$ and $3K > 0$, so $K > 1$.

**Step 4: Verify using Nyquist criterion.**

The frequency response:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(j\omega+3)}{j\omega(j\omega-1)} = \frac{K(j\omega+3)}{-\omega^2 - j\omega}$$

Rationalizing:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(j\omega+3)(-\omega^2 + j\omega)}{\omega^4 + \omega^2}$$

The imaginary part equals zero (phase crossover) when the phase of $G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$:

$$\angle(j\omega+3) - \angle(j\omega) - \angle(j\omega-1) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right) - 90^\circ - (180^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega)) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right) + \tan^{-1}(\omega) = 90^\circ$$

Using the identity: $\tan(A + B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}$

$$\tan(90^\circ) = \infty \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{\omega^2}{3} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{3} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$.**

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{3}) \vert = \frac{K \cdot \sqrt{3+3}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3+1}} = \frac{K\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 2} = \frac{K\sqrt{2}}{2}$$

**Step 6: Apply $N = -P$ for stability (i.e., $N = -1$, one counter-clockwise encirclement).**

For no encirclement of $-1 + j0$ (when the plot magnitude is less than 1 at $\omega_{pc}$):

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert < 1 \Rightarrow \frac{K\sqrt{2}}{2} < 1 \Rightarrow K < \sqrt{2}$$

Combining with $K > 0$:

$$\boxed{0 < K < \sqrt{2}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for identifying $P$, 2 for characteristic equation, 3 for stability range)**

---

**(b) GM and PM at $K = 1$:**

**Step 1: Compute Gain Margin.**

At $K = 1$, $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{3}$ rad/s:

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{3}) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\sqrt{3}) \vert} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{2}) = 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Find Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$.**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

$$\frac{\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2 + 9}}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2 + 1}} = 1$$

$$\omega_{gc}^2 + 9 = \omega_{gc}^2(\omega_{gc}^2 + 1)$$

$$\omega_{gc}^4 + \omega_{gc}^2 - \omega_{gc}^2 - 9 = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{gc}^4 = 9$$

$$\omega_{gc} = \sqrt{3} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Phase at $\omega_{gc}$.**

$$\angle G(j\sqrt{3}) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) - 90^\circ - (180^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3}))$$

$$= 30^\circ - 90^\circ - 180^\circ + 60^\circ = -180^\circ$$

**Step 4: Compute Phase Margin.**

$$PM = 180^\circ + (-180^\circ) = 0^\circ$$

**Step 5: Comment on Relative Stability.**

The Gain Margin of $3.01$ dB is **positive but small**, indicating the system can tolerate only a small gain increase. The Phase Margin of $0^\circ$ indicates the system is **marginally stable** (on the verge of instability). The system exhibits sustained oscillations when $K = 1$.

**(7 marks: 2 for GM, 2 for PM, 3 for stability commentary)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{50}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$$

Draw the Nyquist plot and determine whether the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system in part (a), calculate the Gain Margin and Phase Margin. If the gain is increased to $K = 100$, determine the new stability status. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nyquist Plot Construction and Stability:**

**Step 1: Identify open-loop poles.**

Poles are at $s = -1, -2, -3$, all in the left-half plane. Therefore:

$$P = 0$$

**Step 2: Magnitude and Phase Calculation.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{50}{(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)(j\omega+3)}$$

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{50}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+4}\sqrt{\omega^2+9}}$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right)$$

**Step 3: Key frequency points.**

| $\omega$ | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | $\angle G(j\omega)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0$ | $50 / 6 \approx 8.33$ | $0^\circ$ |
| $0.5$ | $5.18$ | $-67.5^\circ$ |
| $1.0$ | $2.96$ | $-108.4^\circ$ |
| $1.5$ | $1.69$ | $-138.4^\circ$ |
| $\infty$ | $0$ | $-270^\circ$ |

**Step 4: Phase crossover frequency.**

Setting $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$:

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right) = 180^\circ$$

Iteratively solving: $\omega_{pc} \approx 1.85$ rad/s.

At $\omega_{pc}$:

$$\vert G(j1.85) \vert = \frac{50}{\sqrt{4.42}\sqrt{7.42}\sqrt{12.42}} = \frac{50}{2.10 \times 2.72 \times 3.52} \approx \frac{50}{20.13} \approx 2.48$$

**Step 5: Stability Conclusion.**

The Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis at $-2.48$ (magnitude $> 1$). Since $P = 0$ and the plot does not encircle $-1 + j0$ (it crosses to the left of it, meaning the critical point is on the right side of the crossing, hence no encirclement), we have $N = 0$:

$$Z = N + P = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**The closed-loop system is STABLE.**

**(7 marks: 2 for $P$ identification, 3 for Nyquist plot construction, 2 for stability conclusion)**

---

**(b) GM, PM, and New Gain Analysis:**

**Step 1: Gain Margin at $K = 50$.**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} = \frac{1}{2.48} \approx 0.403$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.403) \approx -7.89 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Gain Crossover Frequency.**

Setting $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2+1}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2+4}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2+9} = 50$$

Iterating: $\omega_{gc} \approx 2.27$ rad/s.

**Step 3: Phase Margin.**

At $\omega_{gc} = 2.27$:

$$\angle G(j2.27) = -\tan^{-1}(2.27) - \tan^{-1}(1.135) - \tan^{-1}(0.757)$$

$$= -66.2^\circ - 48.6^\circ - 37.1^\circ = -151.9^\circ$$

$$PM = 180^\circ - 151.9^\circ = 28.1^\circ$$

**Step 4: Analysis at $K = 100$.**

At $K = 100$, the magnitude scales by a factor of $2$. The new phase crossover magnitude is:

$$\vert G_{new}(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 2 \times 2.48 = 4.96$$

Since this is much greater than $1$, the critical point $-1 + j0$ is now **encircled** by the Nyquist plot. Specifically:

$$N = 2 \text{ (two clockwise encirclements)}$$

$$Z = N + P = 2 + 0 = 2$$

**The closed-loop system becomes UNSTABLE with 2 RHP poles** when $K = 100$.

The maximum stable gain occurs when $\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$, giving:

$$K_{max} = 50 \times \frac{1}{2.48} \approx 20.16$$

**(7 marks: 2 for GM, 2 for PM, 3 for K=100 stability analysis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap (Most Common Mistake):** KTU uses $Z = N + P$ with $N$ = clockwise encirclements. Students often write $Z = P - N$ from textbooks using the opposite convention, leading to **wrong stability conclusions and loss of 4-5 marks**.
> 2. **Mirror Image Omission:** The Nyquist plot for negative frequencies is the mirror image (about the real axis) of the positive frequency plot. Forgetting to consider this mirror image leads to **incorrect encirclement counting (lose 3 marks)**.
> 3. **Phase Calculation Errors:** When finding the phase crossover, students often forget the principal value conventions for $\tan^{-1}$ (especially for the third quadrant). Always verify by substituting back.
> 4. **Missing $P$ Identification:** Many students forget to count the open-loop RHP poles ($P$), particularly for systems with poles on the imaginary axis (marginally stable open-loop), leading to **incorrect $Z$ calculation**.
> 5. **Units Confusion:** Phase margin is in **degrees**, gain margin is in **dB** (not linear). Writing GM = 0.403 without converting to dB costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Formula:** $Z = N + P$ (KTU 2024 Scheme convention: $N$ = clockwise encirclements of $-1 + j0$).
- **Stability Condition:** Closed-loop system is **stable if and only if $Z = 0$**.
- **Critical Point:** $-1 + j0$ (encirclements around this point determine stability).
- **Gain Margin (GM):** $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$, in dB: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- **Phase Margin (PM):** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
- **Phase Crossover ($\omega_{pc}$):** Frequency where $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$.
- **Gain Crossover ($\omega_{gc}$):** Frequency where $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ ($0$ dB).
- **Positive GM and PM** indicate relative stability; **negative values** indicate instability.
- **Type-0 system:** Nyquist plot starts at finite positive real axis and ends at origin from $-90^\circ$ direction.
- **Type-1 system:** Nyquist plot starts at infinity along $-90^\circ$ and ends at origin from $-90^\circ(n-m)$ direction.
- **Cauchy's Argument Principle** is the mathematical foundation.
- **Always mirror** the positive frequency plot about the real axis for negative frequencies.
- **Nyquist works for time-delay systems** $e^{-T_ds}$, which Routh-Hurwitz cannot handle.
- **Typical KTU exam weightage:** 7-14 marks per question; commonly appears in Part B of ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Analysis Using Polar and Bode Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability Analysis Using Polar and Bode Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stability? (The Heart of Control Engineering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability (KTU 2024 Definition):** A linear time-invariant (LTI) control system is said to be **absolutely stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output (BIBO stable). It is **marginally stable** if the system oscillates with constant amplitude for a bounded input, and **unstable** if the output grows without bound.

In Module 3, we are less interested in the "yes/no" answer (which comes from the Routh-Hurwitz criterion in Module 2) and more interested in the **"how stable?"** question. This measure of *how stable* a system is, expressed as a number in dB or degrees, is called **Relative Stability**.

## 1.2 The Two Graphical Arenas: Polar vs. Bode

| Tool | Graph Style | Information Read | Mathematical Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polar Plot** (Nyquist) | $G(j\omega)$ traced on the complex plane ($\text{Re}$ vs. $\text{Im}$) | Absolute stability, encirclements, conditional stability | Harder to sketch by hand |
| **Bode Plot** | Two semi-log curves: $\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB}$ and $\angle G(j\omega)$ | Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM) directly as distances | Very easy; uses asymptotes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCAET501 Module 3):** Students must be able to draw the polar plot and Bode plot of a given open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ and determine the stability of the closed-loop system using **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)**.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: Riding a Bicycle ­¤Ü▓

Imagine riding a bicycle:

- **Unstable System:** You are a complete novice, the handlebars are loose, and a tiny push sends you crashing. The system has **no margin of safety** (GM and PM are negative).
- **Marginally Stable System:** You balance perfectly, but the slightest pebble throws you off. The system just barely keeps going (GM = 0 dB or PM = 0┬░).
- **Stable System with Large Margins:** You are an expert cyclist; even with a strong gust of wind, you stay upright comfortably. The system has **large Gain and Phase Margins**, meaning it can tolerate big disturbances before failing.
- **Overly Stable / Sluggish System:** You over-correct every tiny wobble ŌĆö you don't fall, but you waste enormous energy and react slowly. This is an **over-damped** system with excessive margins.

**Gain Margin (GM)** represents how much extra gain you can tolerate before instability sets in (like how much the wind resistance can increase). **Phase Margin (PM)** represents how much extra phase lag (delay) the system can absorb before instability occurs (like how much reaction time you can spare).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polar Plot ŌĆö The Geometric Stability Test

The polar plot is the locus of the tip of the vector $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ as $\omega$ varies from **$0$ to $\infty$**. It is plotted on a complex plane with the real axis ($\sigma$) and imaginary axis ($j\omega$).

### 2.1.1 Rules for Sketching a Polar Plot

1. Substitute $s = j\omega$ into $G(s)H(s)$ and separate it into real and imaginary parts: $G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \text{Re}(\omega) + j\,\text{Im}(\omega)$.
2. Find the starting magnitude: $\lim_{\omega \to 0} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$.
3. Find the ending magnitude: $\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$.
4. Find the points where the plot crosses the real or imaginary axis (set $\text{Im} = 0$ for real-axis crossing; set $\text{Re} = 0$ for imaginary-axis crossing).
5. Determine the angle of departure/asymptote in the high-frequency region.

### 2.1.2 The Nyquist Stability Criterion (Bridging Concept)

Although the full Nyquist contour method belongs to advanced stability analysis, the **consequence** of Nyquist's criterion in the frequency domain is what we use in Module 3:

> **Nyquist Consequence (KTU Module 3 Form):** For a **minimum-phase** open-loop system with **no open-loop poles in the right half-plane ($P = 0$)**, the closed-loop system is stable **if and only if the critical point $-1 + j0$ lies to the *left* of the polar plot as $\omega$ increases from $0$ to $\infty$**.

This single fact lets us read **Gain Margin** directly off the polar plot.

## 2.2 Bode Plot ŌĆö The Engineer's Graph Paper

A Bode plot consists of two curves on a semi-log graph (x-axis: $\log_{10}\omega$):

- **Magnitude plot:** $20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Phase plot:** $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ in **degrees**.

### 2.2.1 The Two Critical Frequencies

| Frequency | Definition | What happens at this point? |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** | Frequency where magnitude $= 1$ (i.e., $0\,\text{dB}$) | Energy of signal is preserved |
| **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)** | Frequency where phase $= -180┬░$ | Signal becomes maximally inverted |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are tested almost every semester. Memorize the *form*, not just the symbol.

| Quantity | Mathematical Definition | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gain Margin (GM)** | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) or **dB** | Extra gain tolerable |
| **GM in dB** | $\text{GM}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\right)$ | **dB** | Standard textbook form |
| **Phase Margin (PM)** | $\text{PM} = 180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | **Degrees (┬░)** | Extra phase lag tolerable |
| **Magnitude (dB)** | $M_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | dB | Logarithmic amplitude |
| **Stability Condition** | $\text{GM}_{dB} > 0$ **AND** $\text{PM} > 0┬░$ | ŌĆö | Closed-loop stable |
| **Marginal Stability** | $\text{GM}_{dB} = 0$ **OR** $\text{PM} = 0┬░$ | ŌĆö | Sustained oscillation |
| **Instability** | $\text{GM}_{dB} < 0$ **OR** $\text{PM} < 0┬░$ | ŌĆö | Output grows unbounded |
| **Minimum Phase System** | All poles & zeros in **LHP**; $\text{PM} \approx 0$ iff $\text{GM} \approx 0$ | ŌĆö | Unique magnitudeŌĆōphase relationship |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** GM and PM are the universally accepted design benchmarks in industries such as aerospace autopilot tuning, robotic arm controller design, and process control in chemical plants. Typical design targets: $\text{PM} \ge 45┬░$ and $\text{GM} \ge 6\,\text{dB}$ (Bode's classic design rule).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gain Margin Formula

### Step 1: The Physics of Instability
A closed-loop system becomes unstable when the feedback signal re-enters the system with a phase shift of $-180┬░$ (i.e., inverted) **AND** with a magnitude $\ge 1$. In such a case, the system becomes a *positive feedback* loop with gain $\ge 1$, leading to self-reinforcing oscillation.

### Step 2: Identify the Phase Crossover Frequency
At $\omega = \omega_{pc}$, the total phase shift is exactly $-180┬░$. So:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180┬░$$

### Step 3: Read the Magnitude at That Point
At this same $\omega_{pc}$, the open-loop magnitude is $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$. We can safely *ignore* the angle because it is already $-180┬░$ ŌĆö what matters now is: *how large is the magnitude?*

### Step 4: Define the Largest Tolerable Gain
For the closed-loop to *just barely* stay stable, we need the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ to be scaled down to exactly $1$. The factor by which the current magnitude can be amplified before hitting $1$ is:

$$K_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Decibels
Since Bode plots use dB, we take $20\log_{10}$:

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\right) = -20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

This is why on a Bode magnitude plot, the GM is simply the **vertical distance from the $0\,\text{dB}$ axis down to the magnitude curve at $\omega_{pc}$**, read as a *positive* number if the curve is below $0\,\text{dB}$ at that point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Phase Margin Formula

### Step 1: The Physics of Resonance
When the open-loop magnitude equals $1$ (i.e., $0\,\text{dB}$), the loop is no longer attenuating the signal. If, at this same frequency, the phase has already slipped to $-180┬░$, the system is critically unstable.

### Step 2: Identify the Gain Crossover Frequency
At $\omega = \omega_{gc}$, the magnitude equals $1$:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 20\log_{10}\vert \cdot \vert = 0\,\text{dB}$$

### Step 3: Read the Phase at That Point
At $\omega_{gc}$, the phase is $\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$. We measure how far this phase is from the catastrophic value of $-180┬░$:

$$\text{PM} = -180┬░ - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \quad \text{(when angles are negative)}$$

Equivalently, since Bode phase plots are typically drawn with negative angles going down:

$$\text{PM} = 180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

This is the **vertical distance on the phase plot, measured upwards from the $-180┬░$ line to the phase curve at $\omega_{gc}$**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(1 + s)(1 + 0.5s)}$

> A classic KTU problem. We will find $\omega_{gc}$, $\omega_{pc}$, GM, and PM step-by-step.

### Step A: Substitute $s = j\omega$

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(1 + j\omega)(1 + 0.5j\omega)}$$

### Step B: Find $\omega_{pc}$ (Phase Crossover)

The phase is the sum of the angles of numerator and denominator:

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = 0┬░ - 90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega)$$

Set this equal to $-180┬░$:

$$-90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) = -180┬░$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) = 90┬░$$

Taking tangent of both sides and using the identity $\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}$:

$$\dfrac{\omega + 0.5\omega}{1 - (0.5)\omega^2} = \tan(90┬░) = \infty$$

The denominator must equal zero:

$$1 - 0.5\,\omega^2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega^2 = 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 1.414\,\text{rad/s}}$$

### Step C: Find Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \omega_{pc}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.25\,\omega_{pc}^2}}$$

Substitute $\omega_{pc}^2 = 2$:

$$= \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1+2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.5}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{1.5}}$$

$$= \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.5}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{9}} = \dfrac{K}{3}$$

### Step D: Compute GM

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{K/3}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{K}\right)$$

For $K = 1$: $\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) \approx 9.54\,\text{dB}$ (stable).

For $K = 4$: $\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3/4) \approx -2.5\,\text{dB}$ (unstable).

### Step E: Find $\omega_{gc}$ (Gain Crossover)

Set $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$:

$$\dfrac{K}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.25\omega^2}} = 1$$

For $K = 1$, squaring both sides:

$$\omega^2 (1 + \omega^2)(1 + 0.25\omega^2) = 1$$

This is a cubic in $\omega^2$. Let $x = \omega^2$:

$$x(1 + x)(1 + 0.25x) = 1$$
$$x(1 + 1.25x + 0.25x^2) = 1$$
$$0.25x^3 + 1.25x^2 + x - 1 = 0$$

Solving numerically yields $x \approx 0.745$, hence $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.863\,\text{rad/s}$.

### Step F: Compute PM

$$\text{PM} = 180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$
$$= 180┬░ - 90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(0.863) - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \times 0.863)$$
$$= 90┬░ - 40.78┬░ - 23.34┬░$$
$$\boxed{\text{PM} \approx 25.88┬░}$$

This is positive, confirming stability for $K = 1$, though the margin is modest.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Input Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{pc}$ | $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180┬░$ | Open-loop TF |
| 2 | $\omega_{gc}$ | $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ | Open-loop TF |
| 3 | GM (dB) | $-20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Step 1 result |
| 4 | PM (deg) | $180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Step 2 result |
| 5 | Verdict | $\text{GM}>0 \ \&\ \text{PM}>0 \Rightarrow \text{Stable}$ | Steps 3 & 4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stability Analysis Master Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the entire decision logic a student must follow when given an open-loop transfer function in a KTU examination:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given G(s)H(s)] --> B[Substitute s = j omega]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    C -->|Polar Plot| D[Plot on Re vs Im plane for omega from 0 to infinity]
    C -->|Bode Plot| E[Plot 20 log sub 10 of G sub dB and phase in degrees]
    D --> F[Locate -1 plus j 0 critical point relative to plot]
    E --> G[Find omega sub pc from phase plot at -180 deg]
    E --> H[Find omega sub gc from magnitude plot at 0 dB]
    G --> I[Compute GM sub dB equals -20 log of magnitude at omega sub pc]
    H --> J[Compute PM equals 180 deg plus phase at omega sub gc]
    I --> K{GM sub dB greater than 0}
    J --> L{PM greater than 0 deg}
    K -->|Yes| M[Stable]
    K -->|No| N[Unstable]
    L -->|Yes| M
    L -->|No| N
    M --> O[Report GM and PM with units]
    N --> P[Suggest gain reduction or lead compensation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Phase and Gain Margins on a Typical Bode Plot

The following block schematic shows the *anatomical* locations of $\omega_{gc}$, $\omega_{pc}$, GM, and PM on a Bode plot for a typical second-order-like underdamped system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAG["MAGNITUDE PLOT  G sub dB vs log omega"]
        M0[0 dB horizontal line] --- M1[Slope changes at corner frequencies]
        M1 --> M2[Curve crosses 0 dB at omega sub gc]
    end
    subgraph PHASE["PHASE PLOT  angle in deg vs log omega"]
        P0[0 deg reference] --- P1[Phase drops with increasing omega]
        P1 --> P2[Phase reaches -180 deg at omega sub pc]
    end
    M2 -.->|omega sub gc| GC[Gain Crossover Frequency]
    P2 -.->|omega sub pc| PC[Phase Crossover Frequency]
    GC --> PMcalc[Phase Margin equals 180 deg plus phase at omega sub gc]
    PC --> GMcalc[Gain Margin sub dB equals -20 log of magnitude at omega sub pc]
    PMcalc --> OUT1[PM in degrees]
    GMcalc --> OUT2[GM in dB]
```

## 4.3 Stability Regions in the GMŌĆōPM Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[GM greater than 0 dB AND PM greater than 0 deg] --> S1[ABSOLUTELY STABLE]
    R2[GM equals 0 dB OR PM equals 0 deg] --> S2[MARGINALLY STABLE  sustained oscillation]
    R3[GM less than 0 dB OR PM less than 0 deg] --> S3[UNSTABLE  output diverges]
    R4[GM positive but very small AND PM less than 30 deg] --> S4[STABLE BUT POORLY DAMPED  high overshoot]
    R5[PM greater than 70 deg] --> S5[OVERDAMPED  sluggish response]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Heuristic (Bode):** Aim for $\text{PM} \approx 45┬░\text{ŌĆō}60┬░$ and $\text{GM} \ge 6\,\text{dB}$ for a good balance between speed of response and damping.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Answer in **2ŌĆō3 sentences** with a key formula. Each carries **3 marks** (2 for content, 1 for presentation).

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. State the conditions for stability of a closed-loop system in terms of these margins.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability, measured at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$. Mathematically,
  $$\text{GM}_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$
- **Phase Margin (PM):** It is defined as the additional phase lag that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before instability, given by
  $$\text{PM} = 180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$
- **Stability conditions:** The closed-loop system is stable if and only if $\text{GM}_{dB} > 0$ **and** $\text{PM} > 0┬░$. Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**What is the significance of the critical point $(-1 + j0)$ in the polar plot? What does the polar plot crossing this point imply?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The point $(-1, j0)$ on the polar plot is called the **critical point** because the closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ reduces to $G(s)H(s) = -1$ at the threshold of instability.
- If the polar plot **encircles** $(-1, j0)$ ŌåÆ closed-loop system is **unstable**.
- If the polar plot **passes through** $(-1, j0)$ ŌåÆ closed-loop system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillations).
- If the polar plot **does not encircle** $(-1, j0)$ ŌåÆ closed-loop system is **stable**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). The student answers **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Part B ŌĆō Option A** [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Question]**

For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \dfrac{50}{(1 + 0.1s)(1 + 2s)}$$

**Draw the polar plot and determine:**

**(a)** The gain margin and phase margin.
**(b)** Comment on the closed-loop stability.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$ and split into real/imaginary parts.**

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{50}{(1 + 0.1j\omega)(1 + 2j\omega)} = \dfrac{50}{(1 - 0.2\omega^2) + j(2.1\omega)}$$

Rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate:

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{50\left[(1 - 0.2\omega^2) - j(2.1\omega)\right]}{(1 - 0.2\omega^2)^2 + (2.1\omega)^2}$$

**Step 2: Find Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$.**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2.1\omega}{1 - 0.2\omega^2}\right) = -180┬░$$

This requires $1 - 0.2\omega^2 = 0$ (i.e., the denominator of the arctan is positive only for the arctan to reach $\pm 90┬░$, but for the angle to reach $-180┬░$ we need the real part of the original denominator to be zero, leading to pure imaginary denominator):

$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\,\text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 3: Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$.**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{50}{2.1 \cdot \sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{50}{2.1 \times 2.236} = \dfrac{50}{4.696} \approx 10.65$$

**Step 4: Gain Margin.**

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{10.65}\right) = 20\log_{10}(0.0939) \approx \boxed{-20.55\,\text{dB}}$$

**Step 5: Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$.**

Set $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\dfrac{50}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2)^2 + (2.1\omega_{gc})^2}} = 1$$

Squaring:

$$(1 - 0.2\omega^2)^2 + 4.41\omega^2 = 2500$$

$$1 - 0.4\omega^2 + 0.04\omega^4 + 4.41\omega^2 = 2500$$

$$0.04\omega^4 + 4.01\omega^2 - 2499 = 0$$

Let $x = \omega^2$:

$$0.04x^2 + 4.01x - 2499 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$x = \dfrac{-4.01 \pm \sqrt{16.08 + 399.84}}{0.08} = \dfrac{-4.01 \pm 20.40}{0.08}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$x = \dfrac{16.39}{0.08} \approx 204.9 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{gc} \approx 14.31\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6: Phase Margin.**

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2.1 \times 14.31}{1 - 0.2 \times 204.9}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30.05}{-39.98}\right)$$

Since the real part is negative and imaginary part is positive, the angle lies in the second quadrant:

$$= -\tan^{-1}(-0.7516) = -(180┬░ - 36.9┬░) = -143.1┬░$$

$$\text{PM} = 180┬░ + (-143.1┬░) = \boxed{36.9┬░}$$

##### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Stability Comment:**

- $\text{GM}_{dB} \approx -20.55\,\text{dB} < 0$ ŌĆö **negative GM**
- $\text{PM} \approx 36.9┬░ > 0$ ŌĆö **positive PM**

**Verdict:** This is a **conflict case**! Although GM is negative, PM is positive. The system is **stable** in the sense that small disturbances decay, but it is **conditionally stable** with respect to gain. If the gain is increased by a factor of $\approx 10.65$ (the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$), the system will become unstable. Therefore, the gain $K = 50$ is too high, and the system is **sensitive to gain variations**. Recommended $K_{\max} \approx 50/10.65 \approx 4.7$ for safe operation.

---

### **Part B ŌĆō Option B** [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]**

For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(1 + s)(1 + 0.2s)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the Bode plot for $K = 1$ and determine the gain margin and phase margin.
**(b)** Find the value of $K$ for which the system becomes marginally stable. Find the corresponding oscillation frequency.

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$ and express in Bode form.**

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{j\omega(1 + j\omega)(1 + 0.2j\omega)}$$

**Step 2: Magnitude (dB) expression for $K = 1$.**

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.04\omega^2}}\right)$$

- Initial slope: $-20\,\text{dB/dec}$ (due to $1/s$).
- At $\omega = 1$: slope changes to $-40\,\text{dB/dec}$ (corner at $\omega = 1$).
- At $\omega = 5$: slope changes to $-60\,\text{dB/dec}$ (corner at $\omega = 1/0.2 = 5$).

**Step 3: Phase expression.**

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)$$

- Starts at $-90┬░$ for low $\omega$.
- Goes to $-270┬░$ for high $\omega$.

**Step 4: Find $\omega_{pc}$ (Phase Crossover).**

Set phase = $-180┬░$:

$$-90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = -180┬░$$
$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = 90┬░$$

Using the addition formula:

$$\dfrac{\omega + 0.2\omega}{1 - 0.2\omega^2} = \infty \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 - 0.2\omega^2 = 0$$
$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\,\text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$.**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{1.2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{36}} = \dfrac{1}{6}$$

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(6) \approx 15.56\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 6: Find $\omega_{gc}$ (Gain Crossover).**

Set $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\omega\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.04\omega^2} = 1$$

For small $\omega \ll 1$, the expression simplifies to approximately $\omega \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = \omega = 1$. So:

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 1\,\text{rad/s}$$ (more precisely $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.945\,\text{rad/s}$)

**Step 7: Phase Margin.**

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90┬░ - \tan^{-1}(0.945) - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \times 0.945)$$
$$= -90┬░ - 43.4┬░ - 10.7┬░ = -144.1┬░$$

$$\boxed{\text{PM} = 180┬░ + (-144.1┬░) = 35.9┬░}$$

##### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1: Marginal Stability Condition.**

The system is marginally stable when $\text{GM}_{dB} = 0$, i.e., when $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$. From Step 5 of Part (a), at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$:

$$\left\vert \dfrac{K}{j\omega_{pc}(1 + j\omega_{pc})(1 + 0.2j\omega_{pc})} \right\vert = 1$$

$$\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{1.2}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{K}{6} = 1$$

$$\boxed{K_{\max} = 6}$$

**Step 2: Oscillation Frequency.**

The frequency of sustained oscillation at marginal stability is precisely $\omega_{pc}$:

$$\boxed{\omega_{osc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\,\text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 3: Verification Using Routh-Hurwitz (Optional Cross-Check).**

Characteristic equation: $s(1 + s)(1 + 0.2s) + K = 0$
$\Rightarrow s(0.2s^2 + 1.2s + 1) + K = 0$
$\Rightarrow 0.2s^3 + 1.2s^2 + s + K = 0$

Routh array:
- $s^3$: $0.2$, $1$
- $s^2$: $1.2$, $K$
- $s^1$: $\dfrac{1.2 - 0.2K}{1.2}$
- $s^0$: $K$

For marginal stability, $1.2 - 0.2K = 0 \Rightarrow K = 6$ Ō£ō (matches).

The auxiliary equation from $s^2$ row: $1.2s^2 + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -5 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{5}$ Ō£ō.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Module 3 KTU Papers):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in PM:** A phase angle of $-150┬░$ gives PM = $+30┬░$, **not** $-30┬░$. Many students write PM = $180┬░ - \text{angle}$ (wrong) instead of $180┬░ + \text{angle}$ (correct, because the angle is already negative).
>
> 2. **Confusing GM and PM Frequencies:** GM is read at $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase is $-180┬░$). PM is read at $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude is $0\,\text{dB}$). Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose full marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** GM must be reported in **dB**; PM must be reported in **degrees**. Writing "GM = 6" without "dB" loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **DC Gain Mistake:** For Type-1 systems (one pole at origin), the polar plot starts at $\infty$ on the negative real axis. Students often incorrectly start it at the origin.
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting Negative GM:** A negative $\text{GM}_{dB}$ does **not** automatically mean the system is unstable ŌĆö check the **PM** as well! A positive PM overrides a negative GM in some conditional stability cases (see Part B Option A above).
>
> 6. **Marginal vs. Absolute Stability:** In Bode plots, $\text{GM}_{dB} = 0$ and $\text{PM} = 0$ are *equivalent* conditions for marginal stability in minimum-phase systems. Always show the calculation of **both** in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick-Recall (Print this section):**

- ŌśÉ **Gain Margin (GM)** = additional gain tolerable, measured at $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180┬░$).
- ŌśÉ **Phase Margin (PM)** = additional phase lag tolerable, measured at $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = $0\,\text{dB}$).
- ŌśÉ **Stability Test:** $\text{GM}_{dB} > 0$ **AND** $\text{PM} > 0┬░$ Ō¤╣ **Stable**; either negative Ō¤╣ unstable; either zero Ō¤╣ marginal.
- ŌśÉ **Polar Plot:** Locus of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ in the complex plane as $\omega: 0 \to \infty$.
- ŌśÉ **Critical Point:** $(-1, j0)$ ŌĆö the closed-loop system is unstable if the polar plot encircles this point (for $P = 0$).
- ŌśÉ **Bode Plot:** Two semi-log curves; magnitude in dB and phase in degrees.
- ŌśÉ **GM in dB** = $-20 \log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ (note the *negative* sign because we want a *positive* number when stable).
- ŌśÉ **PM in degrees** = $180┬░ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
- ŌśÉ **Design Targets (Bode's Rule):** $\text{PM} \approx 45┬░\text{ŌĆō}60┬░$, $\text{GM} \ge 6\,\text{dB}$.
- ŌśÉ **Minimum-phase system:** All poles and zeros in LHP. For these, $\text{GM} \approx 0 \Leftrightarrow \text{PM} \approx 0$.
- ŌśÉ **Type-1 systems** (one pole at origin) start polar plot at $\infty$ on negative real axis at $\omega = 0^+$.
- ŌśÉ **Marginal stability oscillation frequency** = $\omega_{pc}$ (the frequency where phase is $-180┬░$).
- ŌśÉ **Maximum allowable gain** for stability = $\text{GM}$ (in linear ratio, not dB).
- ŌśÉ **Corner frequencies** of Bode plot: $\omega_c = 1/T$ for each $(1 + Ts)$ term; for $(1 + s/a)$ form, corner is at $a$.
- ŌśÉ **Slope changes:** Each pole adds $-20\,\text{dB/dec}$ past its corner; each zero adds $+20\,\text{dB/dec}$ past its corner.
- ŌśÉ **Initial slope of Bode magnitude plot** = $-20 \times N$, where $N$ = system type (number of poles at origin).
- ŌśÉ **All formulas in this topic are dimensionless in the linear sense; only the dB and degree units make the numbers human-readable.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relative Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Relative Stability** is a quantitative measure of *how close* a closed-loop control system is to the boundary of instability. Unlike **absolute stability** (which only answers the binary question *"Is the system stable or not?"*), relative stability specifies the *degree* or *margin* of safety by which a stable system remains stable.

In the frequency domain, this margin is quantified by two principal indices defined on the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ evaluated along the $j\omega$ axis:

- **Phase Margin (PM or $\gamma$)** ŌĆö measured at the *Gain Crossover Frequency* ($\omega_{gc}$).
- **Gain Margin (GM)** ŌĆö measured at the *Phase Crossover Frequency* ($\omega_{pc}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Relative Stability is a Module 3 topic and is **directly assessable** in Part A (3 marks: definitions) and Part B (14 marks: Bode/Nyquist-based computation of GM & PM). Mastery of this concept is essential for solving higher-order stability problems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Cliff Edge Walk:** Imagine a blindfolded person walking along a narrow ridge. *Absolute stability* tells you only whether they are currently on the ridge (stable) or have already fallen (unstable). *Relative stability* tells you *how far* they are from the edge of the cliff. A wider margin means they are safely in the middle of the ridge; a small margin means one wrong step and they fall.
>
> In control systems, the "cliff" is the stability boundary ($j\omega$-axis in the $s$-plane or the $(-1, j0)$ point in the Nyquist plane). **GM** and **PM** are the perpendicular distances from the open-loop frequency response to this critical point.

### 1.3 Physical Significance

- A system with **large GM and PM** is **robustly stable** ŌĆö it tolerates parameter variations, component aging, and noise without becoming unstable.
- A system with **small GM and PM** is **fragile** ŌĆö small disturbances or gain changes can push it into instability.
- A system with **negative GM or PM** is **unstable** under closed-loop conditions.

### 1.4 The Two Key Frequency Points

| Symbol | Name | Defining Condition on $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\omega_{pc}$ | **Phase Crossover Frequency** | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ |
| $\omega_{gc}$ | **Gain Crossover Frequency** | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \;\; (0\,\text{dB})$ |

These are the two critical points on the frequency response where the relative stability metrics are evaluated.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Gain Margin (GM) ŌĆö Theory

The **Gain Margin** is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain may be increased *before* the closed-loop system becomes marginally stable (i.e., before the Nyquist plot crosses the critical point $-1 + j0$).

**Mechanism:**
1. At $\omega = \omega_{pc}$, the phase of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ is exactly $-180^\circ$. This means the loop signal is inverted, so positive feedback behaviour emerges.
2. The magnitude at this frequency, $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$, indicates how strongly the system is being "pushed" towards instability.
3. If this magnitude is already $\geq 1$, the system is unstable; if it is $< 1$, the system has *gain reserve*.

**Formal Definition:**
$$GM = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$$

In decibels (the form most commonly used in Bode plots):
$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(GM) = -20 \log_{10}\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$$

**Stability Rule based on GM (for minimum-phase systems):**
- $GM_{dB} > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable
- $GM_{dB} = 0 \Rightarrow$ Marginally stable (sustained oscillations)
- $GM_{dB} < 0 \Rightarrow$ Unstable

### 2.2 Phase Margin (PM) ŌĆö Theory

The **Phase Margin** is the additional phase lag (measured in degrees) that can be introduced into the open-loop transfer function at $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system reaches the instability threshold.

**Mechanism:**
1. At $\omega = \omega_{gc}$, the magnitude is unity ($0\,\text{dB}$). Any additional phase lag will rotate the Nyquist plot further towards the $-1$ point.
2. The current phase at $\omega_{gc}$ is $\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.
3. The "available room" before the phase reaches $-180^\circ$ is the Phase Margin.

**Formal Definition:**
$$\gamma = PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Stability Rule based on PM (for minimum-phase systems):**
- $PM > 0^\circ \Rightarrow$ Stable
- $PM = 0^\circ \Rightarrow$ Marginally stable
- $PM < 0^\circ \Rightarrow$ Unstable

### 2.3 Engineering Benchmarks (Design Heuristics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer-Standard Acceptable Ranges:**
> - **Phase Margin:** $30^\circ \leq PM \leq 60^\circ$ (the "sweet spot" is around $PM \approx 45^\circ - 60^\circ$).
> - **Gain Margin:** $GM_{dB} \geq 6\,\text{dB}$ (typical), with $GM_{dB} \geq 10\,\text{dB}$ considered very robust.
> - For a **second-order system** with damping ratio $\zeta$, the empirical relation is:
> $$\zeta \approx \frac{PM(\text{in degrees})}{100}$$
> Hence $PM = 60^\circ \implies \zeta \approx 0.6$ (a near-optimum damping for minimal overshoot and fast settling).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Evaluated At | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ | ŌĆö | rad/s |
| 2 | Gain Crossover Frequency | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ | ŌĆö | rad/s |
| 3 | Gain Margin (linear) | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | $\omega = \omega_{pc}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Gain Margin (dB) | $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | $\omega = \omega_{pc}$ | dB |
| 5 | Phase Margin | $\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | $\omega = \omega_{gc}$ | degrees |
| 6 | PM vs Damping Ratio (2nd order) | $\zeta \approx PM/100$ | ŌĆö | dimensionless |
| 7 | Resonant Peak $M_r$ relation | $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for small $\zeta$ | $\omega_r$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | BandwidthŌĆōPM relation | Higher PM $\Rightarrow$ Lower overshoot, longer settling | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Autopilots:** Relative stability margins are mandated by aviation authorities (e.g., MIL-F-8785C requires $PM \geq 45^\circ$ and $GM \geq 6$ dB gain margin; $45^\circ$ phase margin is a typical design target for flight control laws).
- **Power Electronics Converters:** SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply) feedback loops are tuned using PM measured on the Bode plot of the converter's control-to-output transfer function.
- **Active Suspension Systems in Automobiles:** Adequate PM ensures passenger comfort without sustained oscillation.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Joint controllers rely on PM to suppress vibrations and ensure smooth trajectory tracking.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gain Margin from the Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

The closed-loop characteristic equation of a unity-feedback system is:
$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

For the system to be on the verge of instability, a pair of closed-loop poles must lie exactly on the imaginary axis, i.e., $s = j\omega_{pc}$. Substituting:
$$1 + G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = 0$$

Rearranging:
$$G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -1$$

This is the **critical point** in the Nyquist plot. In polar form, $-1$ has magnitude $1$ and phase $-180^\circ$ (or $+180^\circ$). Thus, the critical condition simultaneously demands:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

This defines $\omega_{pc}$ as the frequency where the phase is exactly $-180^\circ$. At a stable operating point, the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is *less* than $1$. The factor by which the gain can be multiplied (scaling $K \to K_{crit}$) to push the system onto the critical point is:
$$K_{crit} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

Hence:
$$\boxed{GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}}$$

In decibels:
$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\right) = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Phase Margin

At the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$, the magnitude of the open-loop transfer function is unity:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$$

The phase at this frequency is some value $\phi = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$. If we add an additional phase lag of $\theta$ such that the total phase becomes $-180^\circ$, we have:
$$\phi - \theta = -180^\circ$$

Solving for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \phi + 180^\circ = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

This $\theta$ is precisely the additional phase lag that destabilizes the system ŌĆö and hence, by definition, the Phase Margin:
$$\boxed{\gamma = PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement:**
For the open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}, \quad K = 10$$

Compute the Gain Margin and Phase Margin.

---

#### Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{(j\omega)(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 5)}$$

#### Step 2: Expand the Denominator

$$(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 5) = (j\omega)^2 + 7(j\omega) + 10 = -\omega^2 + 7j\omega + 10$$

So:
$$(j\omega)(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 5) = j\omega \cdot (10 - \omega^2 + 7j\omega)$$

Multiplying:
$$= 10j\omega - j\omega^3 - 7\omega^2 = -\omega^3 - 7\omega^2 + 10j\omega$$

Factor out $j\omega$:
$$= j\omega(-\omega^2 - 7\omega \cdot \frac{1}{j} \cdot j + 10) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{reorganize:}$$

Let us re-expand cleanly:
$$(j\omega)(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 5) = (j\omega)\bigl[(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)\bigr]$$

$$(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5) = j^2\omega^2 + 7j\omega + 10 = -\omega^2 + 7j\omega + 10$$

Multiplying by $j\omega$:
$$(j\omega)(-\omega^2 + 7j\omega + 10) = -j\omega^3 + 7j^2\omega^2 + 10j\omega = -j\omega^3 - 7\omega^2 + 10j\omega$$

Grouping into real and imaginary parts:
$$= -7\omega^2 + j(10\omega - \omega^3)$$

So the real part is $-7\omega^2$ and the imaginary part is $\omega(10 - \omega^2)$.

#### Step 3: Phase of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$

Since the numerator $10$ is real and positive, the phase of the entire function equals the negative of the phase of the denominator:
$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\text{Im(Den)}}{\text{Re(Den)}}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega(10-\omega^2)}{-7\omega^2}\right)$$

$$= -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10 - \omega^2}{-7\omega}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega^2 - 10}{7\omega}\right)$$

#### Step 4: Find $\omega_{pc}$ by setting phase $= -180^\circ$

$$-\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}^2 - 10}{7\omega_{pc}}\right) = -180^\circ$$

The arctangent reaches $-180^\circ$ when its argument is $0$ (taking the principal branch) or when the denominator is $0$ yielding a vertical asymptote. The argument becomes $0$ when:
$$\omega_{pc}^2 - 10 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 5: Compute Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{10}{\vert j\omega_{pc} \vert \cdot \vert j\omega_{pc} + 2 \vert \cdot \vert j\omega_{pc} + 5 \vert}$$

Compute each factor:
$$\vert j\omega_{pc} \vert = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$
$$\vert j\sqrt{10} + 2 \vert = \sqrt{4 + 10} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742$$
$$\vert j\sqrt{10} + 5 \vert = \sqrt{25 + 10} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.916$$

Denominator product:
$$\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{35} = \sqrt{10 \cdot 14 \cdot 35} = \sqrt{4900} = 70$$

Therefore:
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{10}{70} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$

#### Step 6: Compute Gain Margin

$$GM = \frac{1}{1/7} = 7 \quad \text{(linear)}$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(7) = 20 \times 0.8451 = 16.90 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 7: Find $\omega_{gc}$ by setting magnitude $= 1$

$$\frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2 + 4}\sqrt{\omega^2 + 25}} = 1$$

Squaring:
$$\frac{100}{\omega^2(\omega^2 + 4)(\omega^2 + 25)} = 1$$

$$\omega^2(\omega^2 + 4)(\omega^2 + 25) = 100$$

Let $x = \omega^2$:
$$x(x+4)(x+25) = 100$$
$$x(x^2 + 29x + 100) = 100$$
$$x^3 + 29x^2 + 100x - 100 = 0$$

Solving numerically (e.g., Newton-Raphson) yields $x \approx 0.9026$, so:
$$\omega_{gc} \approx \sqrt{0.9026} \approx 0.950 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 8: Compute Phase at $\omega_{gc}$ and Phase Margin

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}^2 - 10}{7\omega_{gc}}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.9026 - 10}{7 \times 0.950}\right)$$

$$= -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-9.097}{6.65}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(-1.368) = -53.85^\circ$$

Therefore:
$$PM = 180^\circ + (-53.85^\circ) = +126.15^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $PM = 126.15^\circ$ is *unusually large* (well above the typical $60^\circ$ design target), suggesting the system is **overdamped** and **sluggish**. $GM_{dB} = 16.90$ dB is healthy, so gain can be increased by a factor of $7$ before instability.

### 3.4 Result Tabulation

| Parameter | Value | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\omega_{pc}$ | $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s | Frequency at which phase $=-180^\circ$ |
| $\omega_{gc}$ | $\approx 0.950$ rad/s | Frequency at which magnitude $= 0$ dB |
| $GM$ (linear) | $7$ | Gain can be increased $7\times$ |
| $GM_{dB}$ | $+16.90$ dB | Positive $\Rightarrow$ stable |
| $PM$ | $+126.15^\circ$ | Positive but excessive $\Rightarrow$ sluggish |
