## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bode Plot Architecture ŌĆö Visualising PM and GM

The Bode plot is the primary engineering tool for measuring relative stability. Below is a structural flow showing how the open-loop frequency response is interpreted to extract margins.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Open Loop Transfer Function G of s times H of s"] --> B["Substitute s = j omega"]
    B --> C["Compute Magnitude M of omega = 20 log G of j omega H of j omega"]
    B --> D["Compute Phase phi of omega = angle G of j omega H of j omega"]
    C --> E["Locate Gain Crossover omega gc where M of omega = 0 dB"]
    D --> F["Locate Phase Crossover omega pc where phi of omega = minus 180 degrees"]
    F --> G["Read Gain Margin GM dB = minus M of omega pc"]
    E --> H["Read Phase Margin PM = 180 degrees plus phi of omega gc"]
    G --> I["Stability Verdict and Design Action"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Nyquist Plot Architecture ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation of PM and GM

The Nyquist plot visualizes the same data in the complex $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ plane. Margins correspond to *distances* to the critical $-1+j0$ point.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Open Loop Frequency Response G of j omega H of j omega"] --> B["Plot locus in Complex Plane"]
    B --> C["Critical Point P at minus 1 plus j0"]
    B --> D["Find Intersection with Negative Real Axis gives omega pc"]
    D --> E["Distance from Origin to Intersection = Magnitude at omega pc"]
    E --> F["GM linear = 1 divided by that magnitude"]
    B --> G["Find Intersection with Unit Circle gives omega gc"]
    G --> H["Angle of Locus at this point = phi of omega gc"]
    H --> I["PM = 180 degrees plus phi of omega gc"]
    F --> J["Relative Stability Indices"]
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Conditional Stability Warning ŌĆö A Special Case

A **conditionally stable system** has multiple $\omega_{pc}$ and $\omega_{gc}$ crossings. In such cases, a *decrease* in gain (not an increase) can destabilize the system, which violates the usual GM interpretation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Conditional Stability Condition"] --> B["Multiple Phase Crossings of minus 180 degrees"]
    B --> C["Open Loop Plot Encircles minus 1 plus j0 multiple times"]
    C --> D["Increasing Gain = May Stabilize System"]
    C --> E["Decreasing Gain = May Destabilize System"]
    D --> F["Conventional GM Interpretation is Invalid"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Use Full Nyquist Criterion and Count Encirclements"]
```

### 4.4 Standard Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Compute GM dB and PM"] --> Q1{"GM dB greater than 0 dB?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"PM greater than 0 degrees?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> Unstable["Closed Loop Unstable"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"Conditional Stability?"}
    Q2 -- "No" --> Unstable
    Q3 -- "No" --> Stable["Closed Loop Stable with Positive Margins"]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> Conditional["Margins Valid only in Operating Range"]
```

> **Mermaid Compilation Notes (per protocol):**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and use **uppercase labels without markdown formatting** inside quotes to ensure clean rendering.
> - Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` are *not* used as node identifiers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define *Phase Margin* and *Gain Margin* as used in the frequency-domain analysis of control systems. State the stability conditions based on these margins for a minimum-phase system.

**Model Answer:**

*Phase Margin (PM, $\gamma$):* It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes marginally stable.

$$\gamma = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

*Gain Margin (GM):* It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ before the system becomes marginally stable.

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}, \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$$

*Stability conditions (minimum-phase system):* The closed-loop system is stable if and only if $GM_{dB} > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^\circ$.

---

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **absolute stability** and **relative stability** in control systems. Why is relative stability more informative for design engineers?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Absolute Stability | Relative Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Indicates only whether the system is stable or unstable | Quantifies *how stable* a stable system actually is |
| Information | Binary (yes/no) | Continuous metric (GM, PM) |
| Tools | Routh-Hurwitz, Jury, root locus | Bode plot, Nyquist plot |
| Engineering Utility | Basic pass/fail check | Design tuning of controllers |

*Engineering relevance:* A system can be absolutely stable yet have a phase margin of only $5^\circ$, making it practically unusable (large overshoot, ringing, fragile to noise). Relative stability metrics allow engineers to **quantify** robustness and **design** compensators (lead/lag/PI/PID) to achieve target margins (e.g., $PM \approx 45^\circ - 60^\circ$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** For the unity-feedback system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+10)}, \quad K = 40$$
compute the **Gain Margin** in dB and the **Phase Margin** in degrees. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the gain $K$ is increased by a factor of $5$, determine the new stability status of the system. Comment on the relative stability. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1: Express $G(j\omega)$**
$$G(j\omega) = \frac{40}{j\omega(j\omega+4)(j\omega+10)}$$

**Step 2: Expand denominator**
$$(j\omega+4)(j\omega+10) = -\omega^2 + 14j\omega + 40$$
$$j\omega \cdot (-\omega^2 + 14j\omega + 40) = -j\omega^3 - 14\omega^2 + 40j\omega = -14\omega^2 + j(40\omega - \omega^3)$$

**Step 3: Phase of $G(j\omega)$**
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{40\omega - \omega^3}{-14\omega^2}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega^2 - 40}{14\omega}\right)$$

**Step 4: Find $\omega_{pc}$ by setting $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$**
$$\frac{\omega_{pc}^2 - 40}{14\omega_{pc}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc}^2 = 40 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10} \approx 6.325 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$**
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{40}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 16}\sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 100}}$$
$$= \frac{40}{\sqrt{40}\sqrt{40+16}\sqrt{40+100}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{40}\cdot\sqrt{56}\cdot\sqrt{140}}$$
$$= \frac{40}{\sqrt{40 \cdot 56 \cdot 140}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{313600}} = \frac{40}{560} = \frac{1}{14}$$

**Step 6: Gain Margin**
$$GM = \frac{1}{1/14} = 14, \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(14) = 22.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 7: Find $\omega_{gc}$**
$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{40}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+16}\sqrt{\omega^2+100}} = 1$$
$$\omega^2(\omega^2+16)(\omega^2+100) = 1600$$

Let $x = \omega^2$:
$$x(x+16)(x+100) = 1600$$
$$x^3 + 116x^2 + 1600x - 1600 = 0$$

Solving numerically: $x \approx 0.928 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{gc} \approx 0.963$ rad/s.

**Step 8: Phase at $\omega_{gc}$**
$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.928 - 40}{14 \times 0.963}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-39.07}{13.48}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(-2.898) = -70.94^\circ$$

**Step 9: Phase Margin**
$$PM = 180^\circ + (-70.94^\circ) = 109.06^\circ$$

> **Result for Q-A (a):** $GM_{dB} = +22.92$ dB, $PM = +109.06^\circ$. The system is **stable with very large margins** (over-damped / sluggish).

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

New gain: $K_{new} = 5 \times 40 = 200$. The open-loop transfer function becomes:
$$G_{new}(s) = \frac{200}{s(s+4)(s+10)}$$

Since $\omega_{pc}$ depends only on phase, it is **unchanged**:
$$\omega_{pc} = 2\sqrt{10} \approx 6.325 \text{ rad/s}$$

**New magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**
$$\vert G_{new}(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{5 \times 40}{\text{(same denominator)}} = 5 \times \frac{1}{14} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.357$$

**New Gain Margin:**
$$GM_{new} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8, \quad GM_{new, dB} = 20\log_{10}(2.8) = 8.94 \text{ dB}$$

The new $\omega_{gc}$ shifts upward (larger gain $\Rightarrow$ magnitude curve shifts up $\Rightarrow$ cross $0$ dB at higher frequency). However, since $GM_{new,dB} = +8.94 > 0$ and the phase margin will still be positive (though reduced), the system **remains stable** but with **reduced relative stability**.

> **Result for Q-A (b):** System is still stable. New $GM_{dB} \approx 8.94$ dB, $PM$ reduced. The system now lies in a more dynamic (less sluggish) operating region, closer to optimal $PM \approx 45^\circ - 60^\circ$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the geometric construction of **Phase Margin** and **Gain Margin** on the Nyquist plot. Clearly mark the critical point $-1+j0$ and discuss what happens if the locus *passes through* this point. (7 marks)

**(b)** A unity-feedback control system has
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(1+0.1s)(1+0.05s)}$$
Determine the stability using the **Bode plot method** by computing the GM (in dB) and PM. Comment on the relative stability. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Phase Margin on the Nyquist plot:** The PM is the angle between the negative real axis and the line joining the origin to the point on the locus where $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ (i.e., where the locus intersects the unit circle). Equivalently, it is the angular "clearance" by which the locus at $\omega_{gc}$ stays away from the $-180^\circ$ ray passing through the critical point.

**Gain Margin on the Nyquist plot:** The GM is the *reciprocal* of the distance from the origin to the point where the Nyquist locus **crosses the negative real axis** (i.e., where the phase equals $-180^\circ$). If this crossing occurs at point $X$ on the negative real axis, then:
$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert X \vert}, \quad GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert X \vert$$

**Critical Point ŌĆö Locus Passes Through $-1+j0$:** If the Nyquist locus passes *exactly* through the point $-1+j0$, then:
- $\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$ **and** $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ simultaneously.
- This satisfies $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ at $s = j\omega_{pc}$, meaning the closed-loop characteristic equation has roots on the imaginary axis.
- The closed-loop system exhibits **sustained undamped oscillations** at frequency $\omega_{pc}$ (marginally stable).

If the locus *encircles* the $-1+j0$ point, the system is **unstable** (by the Nyquist stability criterion, the number of clockwise encirclements $N$ equals the number of RHP poles $P$, and closed-loop stability requires $N = -P$).

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$**
$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(1+0.1j\omega)(1+0.05j\omega)}$$

**Step 2: Phase of $G(j\omega)$**
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.05\omega)$$

**Step 3: Find $\omega_{pc}$ (phase $= -180^\circ$)**
$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.05\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$
$$\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.05\omega_{pc}) = 90^\circ$$

The arctan sum equals $90^\circ$ when the denominators of the two arctan expressions satisfy: $\tan(A+B) = \infty$, i.e., $1 - \tan A \tan B = 0$:
$$1 - (0.1\omega_{pc})(0.05\omega_{pc}) = 0$$
$$0.005\,\omega_{pc}^2 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{200} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$**
$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{10}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{1+(0.1\omega_{pc})^2}\sqrt{1+(0.05\omega_{pc})^2}}$$
$$= \frac{10}{14.14 \cdot \sqrt{1+2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.5}} = \frac{10}{14.14 \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{1.5}}$$
$$= \frac{10}{14.14 \cdot 1.732 \cdot 1.225} = \frac{10}{30.0} = 0.333$$

**Step 5: Gain Margin**
$$GM = \frac{1}{0.333} = 3.0, \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6: Find $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude $= 1$)**
$$\frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{1+0.01\omega^2}\sqrt{1+0.0025\omega^2}} = 1$$
$$\omega^2(1+0.01\omega^2)(1+0.0025\omega^2) = 100$$

Let $x = \omega^2$:
$$x(1+0.01x)(1+0.0025x) = 100$$
$$x(1 + 0.0125x + 0.000025x^2) = 100$$
$$0.000025x^3 + 0.0125x^2 + x - 100 = 0$$

Solving numerically: $x \approx 7.95 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{gc} \approx 2.82$ rad/s.

**Step 7: Phase at $\omega_{gc}$**
$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.1 \times 2.82) - \tan^{-1}(0.05 \times 2.82)$$
$$= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.282) - \tan^{-1}(0.141) = -90^\circ - 15.74^\circ - 8.03^\circ = -113.77^\circ$$

**Step 8: Phase Margin**
$$PM = 180^\circ - 113.77^\circ = 66.23^\circ$$

> **Result for Q-B (b):** $GM_{dB} = +9.54$ dB, $PM \approx +66.23^\circ$.
> - $GM_{dB} > 0$ and $PM > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **closed-loop stable**.
> - $PM = 66.23^\circ$ is **slightly above** the typical $60^\circ$ design target ŌĆö **well-damped, low-overshoot response**.
> - $GM_{dB} = 9.54$ dB is **comfortable** for tolerating gain variations.
> - This is a **well-designed** control system from a relative-stability perspective.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in PM formula:** Students frequently write $PM = \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) - 180^\circ$ instead of the correct $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$. A negative $\angle$ (e.g., $-120^\circ$) gives $PM = 60^\circ$ ŌĆö using the wrong sign yields $PM = -300^\circ$, a sign-flip that loses the entire 7 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\omega_{pc}$ and $\omega_{gc}$:** The phase crossover is found by equating **phase** to $-180^\circ$, not magnitude to unity. Conversely, the gain crossover is found by equating **magnitude** to unity. Mixing these up leads to wrong GM and PM values.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert to dB:** GM is often required in dB. Writing the linear value $GM = 7$ without converting loses the conversion mark. Use $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the $\omega = \infty$ phase contribution:** For an $n$-th order system with integrator, the high-frequency phase asymptote is $-(90^\circ n + \text{type-dependent phase})$. Always verify the high-frequency phase limit.
>
> 5. **Conditional stability mis-judgement:** If the Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis *multiple times*, the conventional "GM > 0 dB $\Rightarrow$ stable" rule breaks down. Always check for multiple encirclements of $-1+j0$ when the order exceeds 3.
>
> 6. **Not specifying units:** $PM$ must carry the degree symbol ($^\circ$); $GM$ in dB must be explicitly stated as "dB". Omitting units costs 0.5ŌĆō1 mark in the KTU scheme.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Relative Stability (Module 3)**

- **Two Margins, Two Frequencies:**
  * $\omega_{pc}$ ŌĆö Phase Crossover Frequency, where $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$.
  * $\omega_{gc}$ ŌĆö Gain Crossover Frequency, where $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ ($0$ dB).

- **Gain Margin:** $GM = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ and $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$.

- **Phase Margin:** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

- **Stability Decision (minimum-phase systems):**
  * $GM_{dB} > 0$ **and** $PM > 0^\circ$ $\Rightarrow$ **Stable**.
  * $GM_{dB} = 0$ or $PM = 0^\circ$ $\Rightarrow$ **Marginally Stable** (sustained oscillations).
  * $GM_{dB} < 0$ and $PM < 0^\circ$ $\Rightarrow$ **Unstable**.

- **Design Benchmarks:**
  * $PM \in [30^\circ, 60^\circ]$ for good transient response; $45^\circ - 60^\circ$ optimal.
  * $GM_{dB} \geq 6$ dB acceptable; $\geq 10$ dB robust.
  * $PM = 60^\circ \Rightarrow \zeta \approx 0.6$ (2nd-order approximation).

- **Special Case ŌĆö Conditional Stability:** Multiple $\omega_{pc}$ crossings $\Rightarrow$ conventional GM rule may be invalid; use full Nyquist criterion (encirclement count).

- **Frequency-Domain Tools:** Bode plot (most common for PM/GM reading), Nyquist plot (geometric intuition), Nichols chart (combined $M$-circles and $N$-circles).

- **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check GM/PM results with Routh-Hurwitz (for the exact stability boundary $K_{max}$) ŌĆö they must be consistent.

- **Engineering Heuristics:** Doubling $K$ in a stable system typically *reduces* PM by ~$10^\circ$ŌĆō$15^\circ$ and *increases* $GM_{dB}$ by ~$6$ dB (or vice versa, depending on the type of system). Useful for quick sanity checks.

- **Sign Convention:** Always quote PM in *positive* degrees for stable systems. A "negative PM" or "negative GM (in dB)" is the unambiguous signature of an **unstable** closed-loop system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gain Margin and Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gain Margin and Phase Margin # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the frequency-domain analysis of linear time-invariant (LTI) control systems, **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** are the two fundamental **relative stability indices** derived from the open-loop frequency response. They quantify *how far* the system is from the **critical point $\left(-1, j0\right)$** in the **Nyquist plane** (or equivalently, its Bode plot equivalent), and are indispensable for assessing both **absolute stability** and the **quality of transient response**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > **Gain Margin (GM)** is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ may be increased before the system reaches the verge of instability, measured at the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ ŌĆö the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle is $-180^\circ$ (or $-\pi$ radians). > > **Phase Margin (PM)** is defined as the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced into the open-loop transfer function at the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes marginally stable. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** along the edge of a cliff. The bicycle is your **control system**, the cliff edge is the **instability boundary**, and your balance adjustments are the **controller corrections**. - **Phase Margin** is like asking: *"If a sudden gust of wind delays my steering by a few extra milliseconds, can I still avoid falling?"* A large PM means the system tolerates significant **phase delay** (timing errors) before losing stability. - **Gain Margin** is like asking: *"If the wind suddenly becomes twice as strong (gain doubles), can I still recover?"* A large GM means the system tolerates significant **amplification errors** before instability sets in. In engineering terms: - A **larger GM** $\Rightarrow$ system can survive a bigger **gain error** without instability. - A **larger PM** $\Rightarrow$ system can survive a bigger **time delay** without instability. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard Target Values (Nise, Ogata, Dorf & Bishop):** > - Acceptable design range: $\mathbf{30^\circ \le PM \le 60^\circ}$ and $\mathbf{GM \ge 6 \text{ dB}}$. > - Optimal: $\mathbf{PM \approx 45^\circ}$ to $\mathbf{60^\circ}$ for a good balance between speed of response and overshoot. --- ## 1.3 The Two Critical Frequencies Two special frequencies on the Bode/Nyquist plot define the margins: | Symbol | Name | Defining Condition | Physical Meaning | |:------:|:----:|:------------------:|:-----------------| | $\omega_{pc}$ | Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ | Frequency where the Nyquist plot **crosses the negative real axis** | | $\omega_{gc}$ | Gain Crossover Frequency | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \; (0 \text{ dB})$ | Frequency where the Bode **magnitude plot crosses 0 dB** | > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** > - **"pc"** = **P**hase **C**rossover ŌåÆ the phase is **$-180^\circ$** (we measure **G**ain here ŌåÆ **GM**) > - **"gc"** = **G**ain **C**rossover ŌåÆ the gain is **0 dB** (we measure **P**hase here ŌåÆ **PM**) --- ## SECTION 1 Summary Callout > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Takeaway:** GM is a **gain** measurement made where the **phase = $-180^\circ$**; PM is a **phase** measurement made where the **gain = 0 dB**. Both are measures of **distance from instability** in the frequency domain. # ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations Consider a unity-feedback closed-loop system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$. The closed-loop system becomes **marginally stable** (i.e., poles lie on the $j\omega$-axis) when the open-loop frequency response $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ passes through the **critical point** $\left(-1, j0\right)$. Marginal stability occurs if and only if: $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert = 1 \quad \text{AND} \quad \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ \;( \text{or } -k\pi, \; k = 1, 3, 5, \dots)$$ This single condition splits naturally into the two margins. --- ## 2.2 Gain Margin (GM) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic The **Gain Margin** is the reciprocal of the magnitude of $G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})$: **Step 1:** Find $\omega_{pc}$ by solving the phase condition: $$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$ **Step 2:** Compute the magnitude at this frequency: $$M_{pc} = \left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$$ **Step 3:** The Gain Margin is the factor by which the gain can be multiplied before instability: $$GM = \frac{1}{M_{pc}} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$$ In **decibels** (more commonly used in Bode plots): $$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(GM) = -20 \log_{10}\left( \left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert \right)$$ --- ## 2.3 Phase Margin (PM) ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1:** Find $\omega_{gc}$ by solving the magnitude condition: $$\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$$ **Step 2:** Compute the phase angle at this frequency: $$\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$ **Step 3:** The Phase Margin is the additional phase lag permissible before the phase reaches $-180^\circ$: $$PM = 180^\circ + \phi_{gc}$$ > [!NOTE] > The expression $180^\circ + \phi_{gc}$ is equivalent to $\left[ -(-180^\circ) - \phi_{gc} \right]$ in **signed form**, or simply the **angular gap** between the actual phase at $\omega_{gc}$ and the critical $-180^\circ$ line. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units / Form | Determined At | |:-:|:--------:|:----------------------:|:------------:|:-------------:| | 1 | Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ | rad/s | ŌĆö | | 2 | Gain Crossover Frequency | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ | rad/s | ŌĆö | | 3 | **Gain Margin (linear)** | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | dimensionless | $\omega_{pc}$ | | 4 | **Gain Margin (dB)** | $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$ | decibels (dB) | $\omega_{pc}$ | | 5 | **Phase Margin (degrees)** | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | degrees ($^\circ$) | $\omega_{gc}$ | | 6 | **Phase Margin (radians)** | $\phi_m = \pi + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | radians | $\omega_{gc}$ | | 7 | Stability Condition | $GM > 1$ (or $GM_{dB} > 0$ dB) **and** $PM > 0^\circ$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | | 8 | Resonant Peak $\rightarrow$ PM (approx) | $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(PM)} \;\;\text{for 2nd-order systems}$ | dimensionless | ŌĆö | | 9 | Damping Ratio $\rightarrow$ PM (approx) | $\zeta \approx \dfrac{PM(\text{in degrees})}{100^\circ}$ | dimensionless | ŌĆö | | 10 | Bandwidth $\rightarrow$ Speed | $\omega_{BW} \uparrow \implies t_s \downarrow$ | rad/s | ŌĆö | > [!WARNING] > **Formula 8 & 9 are valid ONLY for 2nd-order dominant systems.** Do not use them blindly for higher-order systems in the KTU exam ŌĆö this is a very common mark-losing mistake. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of GM and PM | |:-------|:-----------------| | **Aerospace (Aircraft Autopilots)** | Ensures the system remains stable despite aerodynamic gain variations with altitude and airspeed. | | **DC Motor Speed Control** | Designing PI/PID controllers using Ziegler-Nichols rules based on ultimate gain $K_u$ (related to GM). | | **Power Electronics (SMPS)** | Switching power supplies need adequate phase margin ($\approx 45^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$) for stable voltage regulation. | | **Robotics (Joint Actuators)** | High PM ensures smooth motion without oscillatory jerks. | | **Process Control (Chemical Plants)** | Conservative PMs ($45^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$) are mandated to account for plant parameter drift. | > [!TIP] > **Why margins matter in industry:** They give a **robustness** measure ŌĆö guaranteeing stability even if the **mathematical model** of the plant differs from the **real plant** by up to the margin amount. This is the practical heart of **robust control**. # ­¤ōĢ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of GM and PM from the Characteristic Equation Consider a unity-feedback closed-loop system with open-loop transfer function: $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}$$ The **closed-loop characteristic equation** is: $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; D(s) + K \cdot N(s) = 0$$ **At the verge of instability**, the characteristic equation has roots on the $j\omega$-axis. Substitute $s = j\omega$: $$1 + G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -1$$ Splitting this complex equality into its real and imaginary parts (or equivalently, magnitude and phase): $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert \cdot e^{j\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)} = 1 \cdot e^{-j180^\circ}$$ Equating magnitude: $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert = 1 \quad \quad \text{(condition for PM)} \quad \cdots (i)$$ Equating phase: $$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ \quad \quad \text{(condition for GM)} \quad \cdots (ii)$$ These are exactly the definitions of $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$ respectively. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Standard 2nd-Order System) > **Problem:** For $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)}$, determine the GM and PM for $K = 2$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $s = j\omega$:** $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega+2)} = \frac{K}{j\omega \cdot j\omega + 2j\omega} = \frac{K}{-(\omega^2) + j2\omega}$$ Rearranging: $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{-\omega^2 + j2\omega}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Magnitude:** $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{(-\omega^2)^2 + (2\omega)^2}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\omega^4 + 4\omega^2}} = \frac{K}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2 + 4}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Phase:** $$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\omega}{-\omega^2}\right)$$ The denominator is negative for all $\omega > 0$, so we are in the 2nd quadrant: $$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\omega}{\omega^2}\right) = -180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\omega}\right)$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{pc}$ (phase = $-180^\circ$):** $$-180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\omega_{pc}}\right) = -180^\circ \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\omega_{pc}}\right) = 0^\circ \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} \to \infty$$ Since $\omega_{pc} = \infty$, the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is: $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert_{\omega \to \infty} = \lim_{\omega \to \infty} \frac{K}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2 + 4}} = 0$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute GM:** $$GM = \frac{1}{0} = \infty \quad (\text{or } + \infty \text{ dB})$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 1):** $$\frac{K}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2 + 4}} = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K = \omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2 + 4}$$ Squaring both sides: $$K^2 = \omega_{gc}^2 \left(\omega_{gc}^2 + 4\right) = \omega_{gc}^4 + 4\omega_{gc}^2$$ For $K = 2$: $$4 = \omega_{gc}^4 + 4\omega_{gc}^2$$ Let $x = \omega_{gc}^2$: $$x^2 + 4x - 4 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula: $$x = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 16}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{32}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm 4\sqrt{2}}{2} = -2 \pm 2\sqrt{2}$$ Taking the positive root: $$x = \omega_{gc}^2 = -2 + 2\sqrt{2} = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.8284$$ $$\omega_{gc} = \sqrt{0.8284} \approx 0.9102 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:** $$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{0.9102}\right) = -180^\circ + \tan^{-1}(2.197)$$ $$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -180^\circ + 65.53^\circ = -114.47^\circ$$ **Step 8 ŌĆö Compute PM:** $$PM = 180^\circ + (-114.47^\circ) = 65.53^\circ$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\omega_{gc} \approx 0.91 \text{ rad/s},\quad PM \approx 65.53^\circ,\quad GM = \infty}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The infinite GM is a feature of **Type-1 (or higher)** systems where the Nyquist plot starts at infinity along the negative imaginary axis as $\omega \to 0^+$, so it never crosses the negative real axis. Type-0 systems can have finite GM. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Type-0 System with Finite GM) > **Problem:** $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{(s+1)(s+5)}$. Compute GM and PM for $K = 10$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $s = j\omega$:** $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K}{(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5)}$$ Expanding the denominator: $$(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5) = j^2\omega^2 + 5j\omega + j\omega + 5 = -\omega^2 + 5 + j6\omega = (5 - \omega^2) + j6\omega$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Phase condition for $\omega_{pc}$:** $$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6\omega_{pc}}{5 - \omega_{pc}^2}\right) = -180^\circ$$ For the arctan to yield $180^\circ$, the denominator must be negative and the ratio infinite: $$5 - \omega_{pc}^2 < 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc}^2 > 5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} > \sqrt{5}$$ $$\frac{6\omega_{pc}}{5 - \omega_{pc}^2} \to \pm \infty \;\;\text{when}\;\; 5 - \omega_{pc}^2 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5}$:** $$\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{(5 - \omega_{pc}^2)^2 + (6\omega_{pc})^2}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{0 + (6\sqrt{5})^2}} = \frac{K}{6\sqrt{5}}$$ For $K = 10$: $$\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert = \frac{10}{6\sqrt{5}} = \frac{10}{13.416} \approx 0.7454$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Gain Margin:** $$GM = \frac{1}{0.7454} = 1.342 \quad \Rightarrow \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(1.342) \approx 2.56 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Find $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 1):** $$\frac{K^2}{(5 - \omega^2)^2 + 36\omega^2} = 1$$ $$(5 - \omega^2)^2 + 36\omega^2 = K^2 = 100$$ $$25 - 10\omega^2 + \omega^4 + 36\omega^2 = 100$$ $$\omega^4 + 26\omega^2 - 75 = 0$$ Let $y = \omega^2$: $$y^2 + 26y - 75 = 0$$ $$y = \frac{-26 \pm \sqrt{676 + 300}}{2} = \frac{-26 \pm \sqrt{976}}{2} = \frac{-26 \pm 31.24}{2}$$ Taking the positive root: $$y = \frac{5.24}{2} = 2.62 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{gc} = \sqrt{2.62} \approx 1.619 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:** $$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6 \times 1.619}{5 - 2.62}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{9.714}{2.38}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(4.082)$$ $$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -76.22^\circ$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Phase Margin:** $$PM = 180^\circ - 76.22^\circ = 103.78^\circ$$ > [!NOTE] > A very large PM ($> 90^\circ$) usually indicates an **over-damped, slow** closed-loop response. A more typical target is $45^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Interpretation of Results | Result Combination | Stability Status | Engineering Interpretation | |:-------------------|:-----------------|:---------------------------| | $GM_{dB} > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^\circ$ | Ō£ģ **Stable** | System is robustly stable. | | $GM_{dB} = 0$ dB **or** $PM = 0^\circ$ | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **Marginally Stable** | Sustained oscillations at $\omega_{pc}$ or $\omega_{gc}$. | | $GM_{dB} < 0$ dB **or** $PM < 0^\circ$ | ŌØī **Unstable** | Closed-loop poles in the right-half plane. | | Large $GM$ but small $PM$ | Unstable / Marginal | The system is sensitive to **phase delays** (time lag). | | Small $GM$ but large $PM$ | Unstable / Marginal | The system is sensitive to **gain errors**. | | Both $GM$ and $PM$ large | **Very Robust** | Conservative design ŌĆö may be **over-damped** and **slow**. | | $PM = 45^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ | **Optimal Trade-off** | Best balance: low overshoot, fast settling. | # ­¤ōÖ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö How GM and PM Are Measured ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Open-Loop Transfer Function GsHs] --> B[Evaluate Frequency Response Gj omega H j omega] B --> C{Identify Critical Frequencies} C --> D[Phase Crossover omega_pc
where phase = minus 180 deg] C --> E[Gain Crossover omega_gc
where magnitude = 0 dB] D --> F[Measure Magnitude at omega_pc
M_pc equals absolute value of Gj omega_pc H j omega_pc] E --> G[Measure Phase at omega_gc
phi_gc equals angle of Gj omega_gc H j omega_gc] F --> H[Compute Gain Margin
GM equals 1 over M_pc
GM_dB equals 20 log GM] G --> I[Compute Phase Margin
PM equals 180 deg plus phi_gc] H --> J[Stability Verdict] I --> J J --> K{GM_dB greater than 0
AND PM greater than 0 deg} K -->|Yes| L[System is STABLE] K -->|No| M[System is UNSTABLE or MARGINAL] ``` ## 4.2 Nyquist Plot Schematic ŌĆö Graphical Meaning of Margins ```mermaid flowchart LR O[Origin] --> X[Negative Real Axis] Crit[Critical Point minus 1 plus j0]:::crit NY[Nyquist Contour
of G j omega H j omega]:::curve O -.-> NY NY -->|Real Axis Crossing| AxisCross[Point on negative real axis
equals minus M_pc]:::cross AxisCross -.->|Distance to minus 1| Crit Crit -->|GM = 1 over M_pc| AxisCross NY -->|Magnitude equals 1| UC[Unit Circle]:::unit UC -.->|Phase angle phi_gc| Crit Crit -.->|PM = 180 deg plus phi_gc| UC classDef crit fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef curve fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#006644,stroke-width:2px classDef cross fill:#ffd166,stroke:#aa6600,stroke-width:2px classDef unit fill:#c9d4ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Bode Plot Annotation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD Mag[Bode Magnitude Plot in dB] --> MagX[Crosses 0 dB line at omega_gc] MagX --> MagPM[PM is measured at omega_gc
on the PHASE plot] Pha[Bode Phase Plot in degrees] --> PhaX[Crosses minus 180 deg line at omega_pc] PhaX --> PhaGM[GM is measured at omega_pc
on the MAGNITUDE plot] MagPM --> S[Safety Distance from Instability] PhaGM --> S S -->|GM_dB greater than 0 dB| ST[System is STABLE] S -->|PM greater than 0 deg| ST ``` ## 4.4 Decision Logic Flowchart for Stability Check ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Begin: Compute GM and PM] --> Q1{GM_dB positive
0 dB or more} Q1 -->|No| Unstable[Mark system as UNSTABLE
Right Half Plane poles] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{PM positive
0 deg or more} Q2 -->|No| Unstable Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{PM in range 30 to 60 deg
and GM_dB greater than 6 dB} Q3 -->|Yes| Optimal[OPTIMAL design
Good transient response] Q3 -->|No| Q4{PM greater than 60 deg} Q4 -->|Yes| Overdamped[OVERDAMPED
Slow response] Q4 -->|No| Q5{PM less than 30 deg} Q5 -->|Yes| Oscillatory[UNDERDAMPED
High overshoot and ringing] ``` # ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model]** **Define the terms "Gain Margin" and "Phase Margin" of a control system. Mention the standard accepted ranges for a well-designed system.** **Model Answer:** **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ of the system can be increased before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. Mathematically, $$GM = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert} \quad \text{or} \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$$ where $\omega_{pc}$ is the phase crossover frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$. **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced into the open-loop transfer function at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the system becomes marginally stable. $$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$ **Standard Accepted Ranges:** - Phase Margin: $30^\circ \le PM \le 60^\circ$ (optimal: $\approx 45^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$) - Gain Margin: $GM_{dB} \ge 6$ dB *(3 marks ŌĆö KTU rubric: 1 mark each for GM and PM definition, 1 mark for ranges)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]** **Distinguish between Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$) and Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$). What is the significance of these frequencies in stability analysis?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$) | Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$) | |:--------|:--------------------------------------:|:----------------------------------------:| | Defining condition | $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ (0 dB) | $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180^\circ$ | | Read on Bode plot | Where the magnitude curve crosses 0 dB | Where the phase curve crosses $-180^\circ$ | | Used to compute | **Phase Margin (PM)** | **Gain Margin (GM)** | | Geometric meaning on Nyquist plot | Intersection with **unit circle** | Intersection with **negative real axis** | **Significance:** A system is stable if and only if the Nyquist plot encircles the critical point $\left(-1, j0\right)$ an appropriate number of times. The proximity of $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$ to this critical point (captured by GM and PM) provides a quantitative measure of **relative stability** ŌĆö how robust the system is to parameter variations. *(3 marks ŌĆö KTU rubric: 2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for significance)* --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö ESE Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### ­¤ö╣ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Pattern]** **For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine:** **(a)** The phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ and the gain margin $GM$ in dB. **(b)** The gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ and the phase margin $PM$ in degrees. Comment on the stability of the system. --- #### **Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Step 1:** Replace $s$ with $j\omega$: $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)}$$ **Step 2:** Find the angle of the denominator. Let us expand $(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)$: $$(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5) = j^2\omega^2 + 5j\omega + 2j\omega + 10 = (10 - \omega^2) + j7\omega$$ So: $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega \cdot \left[(10 - \omega^2) + j7\omega\right]} = \frac{10}{j\omega(10 - \omega^2) - 7\omega^2}$$ $$= \frac{10}{-7\omega^2 + j\omega(10 - \omega^2)}$$ **Step 3:** Phase of the system: $$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega(10 - \omega^2)}{-7\omega^2}\right)$$ For the phase to equal $-180^\circ$, the denominator must be negative and the ratio must tend to $\pm\infty$: $$-7\omega^2 < 0 \;\;\text{(true for all }\omega > 0\text{)}$$ The phase equals $-180^\circ$ when: $$\omega(10 - \omega^2) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega = 0 \text{ or } \omega^2 = 10 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$ We discard $\omega = 0$ (degenerate). So $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$ rad/s. **Step 4:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$: $$\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{(10 - \omega_{pc}^2)^2 + (7\omega_{pc})^2}}$$ At $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$, we have $10 - \omega_{pc}^2 = 0$, so: $$\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{0 + (7\sqrt{10})^2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{10} \cdot 7\sqrt{10}} = \frac{10}{7 \cdot 10} = \frac{1}{7}$$ **Step 5:** Compute $GM$: $$GM = \frac{1}{1/7} = 7 \quad \text{(linear)}, \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(7) = 20 \times 0.8451 \approx 16.9 \text{ dB}$$ *(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for setup, 2 for $\omega_{pc}$, 2 for magnitude, 1 for GM)* --- #### **Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Step 1:** Solve the magnitude condition $\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$: $$\frac{100}{\omega_{gc}^2 \left[(10 - \omega_{gc}^2)^2 + 49\omega_{gc}^2\right]} = 1$$ $$\omega_{gc}^2 \left[(10 - \omega_{gc}^2)^2 + 49\omega_{gc}^2\right] = 100$$ Let $u = \omega_{gc}^2$: $$u \left[(10 - u)^2 + 49u\right] = 100$$ $$u \left[100 - 20u + u^2 + 49u\right] = 100$$ $$u \left[u^2 + 29u + 100\right] = 100$$ $$u^3 + 29u^2 + 100u - 100 = 0$$ Solving numerically (using trial): $u = 0.957$ works approximately. Check: $0.957^3 + 29(0.957^2) + 100(0.957) - 100 = 0.876 + 26.555 + 95.7 - 100 = 23.13$ ŌĆö too high. Try $u = 0.5$: $0.125 + 7.25 + 50 - 100 = -42.6$. So root lies between 0.5 and 0.957. Try $u = 0.85$: $0.614 + 20.95 + 85 - 100 = 6.57$. Try $u = 0.8$: $0.512 + 18.56 + 80 - 100 = -0.928$. Try $u = 0.81$: $0.531 + 19.04 + 81 - 100 = 0.571$. So $u \approx 0.805$, hence: $$\omega_{gc} \approx \sqrt{0.805} \approx 0.897 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2:** Phase at $\omega_{gc} = 0.897$: $$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{5}\right)$$ $$= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.4485) - \tan^{-1}(0.1794)$$ $$= -90^\circ - 24.16^\circ - 10.18^\circ = -124.34^\circ$$ **Step 3:** Phase Margin: $$PM = 180^\circ - 124.34^\circ = 55.66^\circ$$ **Stability Comment:** Since $GM_{dB} = 16.9$ dB $> 0$ dB and $PM = 55.66^\circ > 0^\circ$, the closed-loop system is **stable** with **good relative stability** (PM is in the optimal range of $30^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$). *(7 marks ŌĆö 3 for $\omega_{gc}$ calculation, 2 for phase at $\omega_{gc}$, 1 for PM, 1 for stability comment)* --- ### ­¤ö╣ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Pattern]** **For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K(1 + 0.5s)}{s(1 + s)(1 + 0.2s)}$:** **(a)** Determine the value of $K$ such that the phase margin is $45^\circ$. Find the corresponding gain margin. **(b)** For the value of $K$ found in part (a), determine the gain crossover frequency and comment on the closed-loop stability. --- #### **Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Step 1:** Open-loop frequency response: $$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 + j0.5\omega)}{j\omega(1 + j\omega)(1 + j0.2\omega)}$$ **Step 2:** Magnitude: $$\left\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{K\sqrt{1 + 0.25\omega^2}}{\omega\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.04\omega^2}}$$ **Step 3:** Phase: $$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)$$ **Step 4:** Apply the PM = $45^\circ$ condition. The phase at $\omega_{gc}$ is: $$\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -180^\circ + 45^\circ = -135^\circ$$ So: $$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{gc}) - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{gc}) = -135^\circ$$ $$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{gc}) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{gc}) = -45^\circ$$ Take tangent on both sides. Using the identity $\tan(A - B) = \dfrac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A \tan B}$: Let $A = \tan^{-1}(\omega_{gc})$ and $B = \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{gc})$, then $A + B = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega_{gc} + 0.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2}\right)$. The equation becomes: $$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{gc}) - \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2}\right) = -45^\circ$$ Using $\tan(A - B)$ formula and equating to $\tan(-45^\circ) = -1$: $$\dfrac{0.5\omega_{gc} - \dfrac{1.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2}}{1 + 0.5\omega_{gc} \cdot \dfrac{1.2\omega_{gc}}{1 - 0.2\omega_{gc}^2}} = -1$$ Let $u = \omega_{gc}^2$ for simplification. Cross-multiplying and simplifying (algebraic steps omitted for brevity but should be shown in exam) yields the polynomial: $$0.1u^2 + 1.1u - 1.2 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; u^2 + 11u - 12 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula: $$u = \frac{-11 \pm \sqrt{121 + 48}}{2} = \frac{-11 \pm 13}{2}$$ Taking the positive root: $u = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_{gc} = 1$ rad/s. **Step 5:** Find $K$ using the magnitude condition at $\omega_{gc} = 1$: $$\left\vert G(j1)H(j1) \right\vert = \frac{K\sqrt{1 + 0.25}}{\sqrt{1} \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1.04}} = 1$$ $$K = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1.04}}{\sqrt{1.25}} = \frac{1.4142 \times 1.0198}{1.1180} = \frac{1.4422}{1.1180} \approx 1.29$$ **Step 6:** Find $\omega_{pc}$ for gain margin: $$\phi(\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$ $$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$ $$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega_{pc}) = -90^\circ$$ This requires the argument inside the combined arctan to be infinite. Numerical solution gives $\omega_{pc} \approx 1.5$ rad/s. **Step 7:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = 1.5$ rad/s: $$\left\vert G(j1.5)H(j1.5) \right\vert = \frac{1.29 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.5625}}{1.5 \times \sqrt{1 + 2.25} \times \sqrt{1 + 0.09}} = \frac{1.29 \times 1.25}{1.5 \times 1.803 \times 1.044}$$ $$= \frac{1.6125}{2.823} \approx 0.571$$ **Step 8:** Gain Margin: $$GM = \frac{1}{0.571} = 1.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(1.75) \approx 4.86 \text{ dB}$$ *(7 marks ŌĆö 3 for finding $\omega_{gc}$, 2 for $K$ calculation, 2 for GM)* --- #### **Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Step 1:** With $K = 1.29$ and $\omega_{gc} = 1$ rad/s, verify stability: - $GM_{dB} = 4.86$ dB $> 0$ dB ŌåÆ **stable by gain margin criterion** - $PM = 45^\circ > 0^\circ$ ŌåÆ **stable by phase margin criterion** **Step 2:** Closed-loop characteristic equation: $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$ $$s(1+s)(1+0.2s) + 1.29(1+0.5s) = 0$$ $$s(1 + 1.2s + 0.2s^2) + 1.29 + 0.645s = 0$$ $$0.2s^3 + 1.2s^2 + s + 0.645s + 1.29 = 0$$ $$0.2s^3 + 1.2s^2 + 1.645s + 1.29 = 0$$ **Step 3:** Routh-Hurwitz check: $$\begin{array}{c|cc} s^3 & 0.2 & 1.645 \\ s^2 & 1.2 & 1.29 \\ s^1 & \frac{1.2 \times 1.645 - 0.2 \times 1.29}{1.2} = \frac{1.974 - 0.258}{1.2} = 1.43 & 0 \\ s^0 & 1.29 & \end{array}$$ All first-column elements ($0.2, 1.2, 1.43, 1.29$) are positive ŌåÆ **no sign changes ŌåÆ closed-loop system is STABLE.** **Stability Comment:** The system is **stable with a moderate gain margin and phase margin**. While the system is theoretically stable, the GM of 4.86 dB is below the recommended 6 dB, suggesting it has limited robustness to gain variations. The PM of $45^\circ$ is at the lower end of the acceptable range, indicating moderate transient response with some overshoot. *(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for Routh array setup, 3 for computation, 2 for stability comment and interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤Ü© KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Always state GM in BOTH linear and dB forms; PM in degrees (or radians with explicit unit). > 2. **Sign error in PM formula:** PM = $180^\circ + \phi_{gc}$ ŌĆö do NOT write PM = $180^\circ - \phi_{gc}$. The phase is negative, so adding the magnitude works. > 3. **$\omega_{pc}$ for Type-1 systems:** Many students forget that for systems with integrators, $\omega_{pc} \to \infty$ and GM = $\infty$. This is correct ŌĆö do not artificially find a finite value. > 4. **Skipping units:** $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$ must be in **rad/s**. Lose 0.5 mark if missing. > 5. **Stability verdict without numerical values:** Always quote numerical GM (dB) and PM ($^\circ$) values before concluding. --- ## 5.3 ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - Ō£ģ **GM** = factor by which gain can be increased before instability; measured at $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180^\circ$). - Ō£ģ **PM** = extra phase lag tolerable; measured at $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 0 dB). - Ō£ģ **GM formula (linear):** $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ - Ō£ģ **GM formula (dB):** $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM) = -20\log_{10}(\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert)$ - Ō£ģ **PM formula:** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ - Ō£ģ **Stability:** $GM > 1$ (or $GM_{dB} > 0$ dB) **AND** $PM > 0^\circ$ ŌåÆ STABLE. - Ō£ģ **Optimal design:** $PM \approx 45^\circ$ŌĆō$60^\circ$, $GM_{dB} \ge 6$ dB. - Ō£ģ **Type-1 and higher systems** usually have **infinite GM** (Nyquist plot never crosses the negative real axis). - Ō£ģ **Type-0 systems** can have **finite GM** (Nyquist plot starts on the negative real axis at $\omega = 0^+$). - Ō£ģ **Approximate relation (2nd-order only):** $\zeta \approx \dfrac{PM \text{ (in deg)}}{100}$ and $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(PM)}$. - Ō£ģ **For Nyquist plot:** GM = distance from origin to negative real axis crossing (reciprocal); PM = angular distance from negative real axis to unit circle crossing. - Ō£ģ **For Bode plot:** GM is a **vertical distance** (in dB) below the 0 dB line at $\omega_{pc}$; PM is a **horizontal/angular distance** (in degrees) above the $-180^\circ$ line at $\omega_{gc}$. - Ō£ģ **GM and PM are independent** ŌĆö knowing one does NOT automatically give the other for higher-order systems. - Ō£ģ **Higher-order systems** are usually designed using the **dominant pole approximation** to apply 2nd-order PM-overshoot relations. - Ō£ģ **Remember:** Negative PM or GM ŌćÆ UNSTABLE. Zero PM or GM ŌćÆ MARGINALLY STABLE (sustained oscillations).

topics.title: State Space Representation of Continuous Time Systems - Transfer Function from State Variable Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Space Representation of Continuous Time Systems - Transfer Function from State Variable Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **State Space Representation** of a continuous-time, linear, time-invariant (LTI) system, the system is described by a set of **n first-order differential equations** rather than a single nth-order differential equation. The internal behavior is captured completely by a set of variables called **state variables**, whose values at any time $t_0$, together with the input for $t \geq t_0$, determine the future behavior of the system.

The two fundamental matrix equations of the state space model are:

**State Equation (Dynamic Equation):**
$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$

**Output Equation (Measurement Equation):**
$$y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ is the **state vector** of dimension $n \times 1$
- $u(t)$ is the **input vector** of dimension $m \times 1$
- $y(t)$ is the **output vector** of dimension $p \times 1$
- $A$ is the **system (state) matrix** of dimension $n \times n$
- $B$ is the **input (control) matrix** of dimension $n \times m$
- $C$ is the **output (sensor) matrix** of dimension $p \times n$
- $D$ is the **direct (feedthrough) matrix** of dimension $p \times m$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The state space form is often called the **"modern control representation"**, while the transfer function is the **"classical control representation"**. The KTU syllabus explicitly tests the *bridge* between these two paradigms.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "GPS-Equipped Car" Intuition

Imagine a car driving in a long tunnel with no windows. The driver can only observe the **speedometer** ($y_1$) and **fuel gauge** ($y_2$). Inside the car, two hidden numbers completely describe the dynamics:

- $x_1$ = current position of the car (in km from tunnel entry)
- $x_2$ = current velocity of the car (in km/hr)

**Why do we need *two* variables (a vector) instead of just one?**
Because position alone cannot predict future position without knowing velocity, and velocity alone cannot tell you where the car will be. The pair $(x_1, x_2)$ forms the **state**. The pair of equations governing their evolution is the **state space representation**. The transfer function, in this analogy, would be a single "transfer characteristic" that takes the accelerator pedal input and tells you the speedometer reading ŌĆö completely hiding the internal mechanics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The state space model is **internal** (describes what's happening inside the system, like the driver knowing speed & position), while the transfer function is **external** (describes only the inputŌĆōoutput relationship, like a passenger who only sees the speedometer).

---

### 1.3 The Transfer Function from State Variable Representation

The **Transfer Function Matrix (TFM)** $H(s)$ is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming **zero initial conditions** $x(0) = 0$.

For a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) system, the transfer function is a scalar:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} \bigg\vert_{x(0)=0}$$

The goal of this topic is to **derive this transfer function directly from the four matrices $(A, B, C, D)$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this derivation crucial for KTU exams?**
> In most real engineering problems (robotic arms, DC motors, aerospace systems), the matrices $A, B, C, D$ are obtained from physical modeling (Newton's laws, Kirchhoff's laws). To design classical PID controllers, frequency response plots (Bode, Nyquist), or root locus, engineers MUST convert these matrices into a transfer function. This makes this topic a **high-frequency examination question**.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Notation

- The matrix dimensions follow the **rule**: Number of state variables = $n$ = order of the system = size of $A$.
- The scalar $s$ is the **Laplace operator** with units of **rad/sec** (or $1/\text{seconds}$).
- The state vector $x$ is conventionally written in lowercase bold, while matrices in uppercase bold.
- The transfer function is dimensionless (for mechanicalŌĆōmechanical systems) or has units (for cross-domain systems, e.g., V/rad/s for motor speed sensitivity).

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the dimensions of $D$. For a SISO system, $D$ is a single scalar. For a MIMO system, $D$ is a $p \times m$ matrix where $p$ = number of outputs and $m$ = number of inputs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Flow ŌĆö From State Equation to Transfer Function

The derivation proceeds in **five rigorous steps**:

1. **Start with the state equation** in the time domain:
   $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$

2. **Take the Laplace transform of both sides** with the property:
   $$\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = sX(s) - x(0)$$

3. **Apply zero initial conditions** $x(0) = 0$ (the cornerstone assumption for transfer function derivation). This collapses the time-domain state memory into a clean algebraic relationship.

4. **Solve algebraically for $X(s)$** in terms of $U(s)$ ŌĆö this isolates the state.

5. **Substitute $X(s)$ into the Laplace transform of the output equation** to obtain $Y(s) / U(s)$.

This produces the **famous "sI minus A" formula**, the cornerstone of modern control theory.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas ŌĆö they appear in nearly every KTU Module 4 question paper.**

| **#** | **Quantity / Formula** | **Symbolic Form** | **Dimensions / Notes** |
|:-----:|:-----------------------|:------------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | State Equation | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | $n \times 1$ vector equation |
| 2 | Output Equation | $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$ | $p \times 1$ vector equation |
| 3 | Resolvent Matrix | $\Phi(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}$ | $n \times n$ matrix |
| 4 | **Transfer Function Matrix (TFM)** | $H(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D$ | $p \times m$ matrix |
| 5 | SISO Transfer Function | $H(s) = C \cdot \text{adj}(sI-A) \cdot B \; / \; \det(sI - A) + D$ | Scalar ratio of polynomials |
| 6 | Characteristic Polynomial | $\Delta(s) = \det(sI - A)$ | Scalar, degree $n$ |
| 7 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(sI - A) = 0$ | Roots = **eigenvalues** of $A$ |
| 8 | Poles of the System | $p_i = \lambda_i(A)$ | The eigenvalues of matrix $A$ |
| 9 | Zeros of the System | Roots of numerator polynomial | From $C \cdot \text{adj}(sI-A) \cdot B$ |
| 10 | Stability Condition | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$ | Equivalent to Routh-Hurwitz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Notational Rule (to prevent table breakage):** When writing determinants, always use `\det` and `\vert \cdot \vert` or `\mid \cdot \mid` ŌĆö never the raw vertical pipe `| |` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the cell separator.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The transfer function derivation from state space is the **workhorse** of:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Converting aerodynamic state matrices (roll, pitch, yaw rates) into transfer functions for autopilot design.
- **Robotics:** Deriving joint-position-to-end-effector transfer functions for manipulator control.
- **DC Motor Control:** The classical armature-controlled DC motor yields:
  $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -b/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ K/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$
  Converting these gives the well-known second-order motor transfer function.
- **Power Electronics:** State-space averaging of DC-DC converters (buck, boost) yields matrices that must be reduced to transfer functions for loop-gain design.
- **Process Control:** MIMO distillation columns use $H(s)$ matrices to design decoupling controllers.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** The matrix $(sI - A)^{-1}$ is called the **"resolvent"** of $A$. In MATLAB, this is computed via the built-in function `ss2tf(A,B,C,D)` or by `C*inv(s*eye(size(A))-A)*B + D`. KTU students should know the manual derivation for exams since programming tools are not allowed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Transfer Function Formula

**Step 1 ŌĆö Begin with the state equation in the time domain:**

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the unilateral Laplace transform to both sides.**

Using the standard property:
$$\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = s X(s) - x(0)$$

Applying this to the entire state equation:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\{A x(t)\} + \mathcal{L}\{B u(t)\}$$

Because $A$ and $B$ are constant matrices (time-invariant), they can be pulled out of the Laplace operator:

$$s X(s) - x(0) = A X(s) + B U(s)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply zero initial conditions** $x(0) = 0$.

This is the **defining assumption** of the transfer function ŌĆö the system starts from rest with no stored energy.

$$s X(s) - 0 = A X(s) + B U(s)$$

$$s X(s) - A X(s) = B U(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Factor out $X(s)$ on the left-hand side.**

Since $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix and $s$ is a scalar, we can write $s X(s) = s I X(s)$ where $I$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix. This is a critical algebraic trick to combine them.

$$(s I - A) X(s) = B U(s)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Pre-multiply both sides by $(sI - A)^{-1}$** (the matrix inverse), assuming it exists (i.e., $\det(sI - A) \neq 0$).

$$X(s) = (s I - A)^{-1} B U(s)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Take the Laplace transform of the output equation** with zero initial conditions.

$$Y(s) = C X(s) + D U(s)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute the expression for $X(s)$ from Step 5 into Step 6.**

$$Y(s) = C \left[ (s I - A)^{-1} B U(s) \right] + D U(s)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Factor out $U(s)$ on the right-hand side.**

Since matrix multiplication is associative, we can write:

$$Y(s) = \left[ C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D \right] U(s)$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Define the Transfer Function Matrix** $H(s)$ as the coefficient relating $Y(s)$ to $U(s)$.

$$\boxed{H(s) = C (s I - A)^{-1} B + D}$$

For a SISO system where $C$, $B$, and $D$ are conformable row/column vectors, $H(s)$ is a scalar rational function of $s$:

$$H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{\text{Numerator Polynomial}}{\text{Characteristic Polynomial}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the denominator always $\det(sI - A)$?**
> The inverse $(sI - A)^{-1} = \dfrac{\text{adj}(sI - A)}{\det(sI - A)}$. The denominator of every term in $H(s)$ is therefore $\det(sI - A)$, and adding $D$ (a constant matrix) only modifies the **numerator**, not the denominator. This is a fundamental KTU result.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example ŌĆö RLC Series Circuit

**Problem:** Consider a series RLC circuit with input voltage $u(t) = v_{in}(t)$ and output voltage across the capacitor $y(t) = v_c(t)$. Derive the transfer function using the state space method.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Choose the state variables:**

The energy-storing elements are the inductor ($L$) and capacitor ($C$). Therefore:
- $x_1 = v_c(t)$ (capacitor voltage)
- $x_2 = i_L(t)$ (inductor current)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the governing differential equations using KVL:**

$$L \frac{di_L}{dt} + R i_L + v_c = v_{in}$$

$$C \frac{dv_c}{dt} = i_L$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express in standard state-space form:**

From the second equation:
$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{v_c} = \frac{1}{C} i_L = \frac{1}{C} x_2$$

From the first equation, solve for $\dot{x}_2 = \dot{i_L}$:
$$\dot{x}_2 = \dot{i_L} = \frac{1}{L}(v_{in} - R i_L - v_c) = -\frac{1}{L} x_1 - \frac{R}{L} x_2 + \frac{1}{L} u(t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the matrices in canonical form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & -\frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} u(t)$$

$$y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0] \cdot u(t)$$

So:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & -\frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Form the matrix $(sI - A)$:**

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & -\frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -\frac{1}{C} \\ \frac{1}{L} & s + \frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the determinant $\det(sI - A)$:**

$$\det(sI - A) = s \left(s + \frac{R}{L}\right) - \left(-\frac{1}{C}\right)\left(\frac{1}{L}\right)$$

$$= s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}$$

This is the **characteristic polynomial** ŌĆö its roots are the natural frequencies (poles) of the circuit.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute the adjugate matrix $\text{adj}(sI - A)$:**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, the adjugate is $\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s + \frac{R}{L} & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Form the resolvent $(sI - A)^{-1}$:**

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}} \begin{bmatrix} s + \frac{R}{L} & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Multiply $C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} \cdot B$:**

First, $C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1}$:

$$C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}} \begin{bmatrix} s + \frac{R}{L} & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}} \begin{bmatrix} s + \frac{R}{L} & \frac{1}{C} \end{bmatrix}$$

Next, multiply by $B$:

$$C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} \cdot B = \frac{1}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}} \begin{bmatrix} s + \frac{R}{L} & \frac{1}{C} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}} \left[ 0 + \frac{1}{C} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \right]$$

$$= \frac{\frac{1}{LC}}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Add $D = 0$ and write the final transfer function:**

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_c(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{LC}}{s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC}}}$$

**Verification:** This matches the well-known result for a series RLC circuit with output across the capacitor, confirming the state space method produces the correct transfer function. The poles are at:
$$s = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}$$

---

### 3.3 Property: Eigenvalues of A are the Poles of the System

The poles of $H(s)$ are precisely the values of $s$ for which $\det(sI - A) = 0$, which are the **eigenvalues** of matrix $A$. This is a critical KTU result because it allows stability analysis **directly from the state matrix** without ever forming the transfer function.

For the RLC example, the eigenvalues of $A$ are the roots of $s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$, which are exactly the poles of $H(s)$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** If $D \neq 0$, the order of the numerator can equal the order of the denominator, but the poles of the system are still strictly the eigenvalues of $A$ ŌĆö $D$ contributes only to the **zeros** and the direct feedthrough term.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of State Space to Transfer Function

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete flow** from the state space matrices to the transfer function, along with the supporting computations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State Equation dx/dt = Ax + Bu"] --> B["Apply Laplace Transform with x sub 0 = 0"]
    B --> C["Obtain sX sub s = AX sub s + BU sub s"]
    C --> D["Rearrange to sI minus A times X sub s = BU sub s"]
    D --> E["Invert matrix sI minus A"]
    E --> F["State in s-domain: X sub s equals sI minus A inverse times BU sub s"]
    F --> G["Output Equation Y sub s = CX sub s + DU sub s"]
    G --> H["Substitute X sub s from previous step"]
    H --> I["Factor out U sub s on right-hand side"]
    I --> J["Transfer Function Matrix H sub s = C sI minus A inverse B + D"]
    J --> K["Compute determinant for denominator polynomial"]
    J --> L["Compute adjugate for numerator polynomial"]
    K --> M["Final SISO Transfer Function H sub s = N sub s over D sub s"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Poles = Eigenvalues of A"]
    M --> O["Zeros = Roots of Numerator"]
    N --> P["Stability and Frequency Response Analysis"]
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from top to bottom. Each rectangular node represents a transformation step. The branching at the bottom indicates that once $H(s)$ is obtained, two parallel analyses (poles for stability, zeros for response shape) can be performed.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the State Space System Itself

This diagram shows how the state space system operates in real time, with the matrices $A, B, C, D$ acting as signal processing elements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["Input u of t"] --> BB["B Matrix"]
    BB --> SUM1["Summing Junction"]
    AA["A Matrix"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> INT["Integrator 1 over s"]
    INT --> X["State Vector x of t"]
    X --> AA
    X --> CC["C Matrix"]
    X --> YPATH["State contributes to output"]
    U --> DD["D Matrix"]
    DD --> SUM2["Summing Junction"]
    YPATH --> SUM2
    CC --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> Y["Output y of t"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The **feedback loop** through matrix $A$ and the integrator is the heart of the state space system. The integrator "remembers" the state ŌĆö its output is the current state $x(t)$.
> - The matrix $A$ multiplies the current state and feeds it back to the input of the integrator, creating the dynamics $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$.
> - The matrices $C$ and $D$ form the output equation: $C$ picks combinations of states (like a sensor), and $D$ allows direct feedthrough of input to output.
> - For a SISO system, the integrator becomes a single block and the matrices become scalars.

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow Graph Representation of the Transfer Function Formula

The signal flow graph below decomposes the formula $H(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ into its constituent signal paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["U sub s"] --> M1["B Matrix"]
    M1 --> ADD1["Add AX sub s term"]
    ADD1 --> INV["Inverse of sI minus A"]
    INV --> XS["X sub s"]
    XS --> M2["C Matrix"]
    M2 --> ADD2["Add D times U sub s term"]
    IN --> M3["D Matrix"]
    M3 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> OUT["Y sub s equals H sub s times U sub s"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Usage:** This signal flow graph is essentially what KTU examiners expect you to redraw for the 14-mark question "Obtain the transfer function from the given state space model." Drawing the block diagram before writing the algebraic derivation fetches **bonus clarity marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define state space representation of a control system. Write the standard form of the state equation and output equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The state space representation is a mathematical model of a physical system described by a set of input, output, and state variables related by first-order differential equations.

The standard form consists of two matrix equations:
- **State Equation:** $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$
- **Output Equation:** $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$

Where $x$ is the state vector, $u$ is the input, $y$ is the output, and $A, B, C, D$ are the system, input, output, and feedthrough matrices respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Definition (1 mark) + State equation (1 mark) + Output equation with matrix descriptions (1 mark).

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is the role of the matrix $(sI - A)^{-1}$ in deriving the transfer function from state space representation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The matrix $(sI - A)^{-1}$ is called the **resolvent matrix** of $A$, denoted $\Phi(s)$.

Its roles are:
1. It transforms the state equation from the time domain to the Laplace domain, allowing algebraic manipulation under zero initial conditions.
2. Its determinant gives the **characteristic polynomial** of the system, whose roots are the **eigenvalues of $A$** and the **poles of the system**.
3. It appears directly in the transfer function formula $H(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$, serving as the core link between state space and classical transfer function models.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Mentioning the term "resolvent matrix" and "eigenvalues/poles" will give you the full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: For a system with the following state space representation:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**Find the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$.**

**(a)** Derive the formula $H(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ from the state space equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply this formula to the given matrices and compute the transfer function. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Start with the state and output equations in time domain:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$
$$y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Laplace transform to the state equation (1 mark):

$$sX(s) - x(0) = A X(s) + B U(s)$$

**Step 3:** Apply zero initial condition $x(0) = 0$ (1 mark):

$$sX(s) = A X(s) + B U(s)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange using $sX(s) = sI X(s)$ and factor (1 mark):

$$(sI - A) X(s) = B U(s)$$

**Step 5:** Pre-multiply by $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (1 mark):

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into Laplace transform of output equation (1 mark):

$$Y(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B U(s) + D U(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor $U(s)$ to obtain the transfer function (1 mark):

$$\boxed{H(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Form $(sI - A)$ (1 mark):

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant (1 mark):

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the adjugate (1 mark):

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Form $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (1 mark):

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply $C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} \cdot B$ (1 mark):

$$C(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$$

**Step 6:** Add $D = 0$ and write the final answer (1 mark):

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$

**Verification:** Poles are at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$, both in the left half-plane, so the system is **stable**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q: For a system with the following state space representation:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**(a)** Compute the characteristic equation and identify the poles of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Form $(sI - A)$ (1 mark):

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s+1 & 0 \\ -1 & s+2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant (2 marks):

$$\det(sI - A) = (s+1)(s+2) - (0)(-1) = s^2 + 3s + 2$$

**Step 3:** Set the characteristic equation to zero (1 mark):

$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Factor to find poles (2 marks):

$$(s+1)(s+2) = 0 \implies s_1 = -1, \quad s_2 = -2$$

**Step 5:** State the stability conclusion (1 mark):

Since both poles have negative real parts ($\text{Re}(s) < 0$), the system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute the adjugate of $(sI - A)$ (1 mark):

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 0 \\ 1 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Form $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (1 mark):

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 0 \\ 1 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply $C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1}$ (2 marks):

$$C \cdot (sI - A)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 0 \\ 1 & s+1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply the result by $B$ (1 mark):

$$C(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & s+1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$$

**Step 5:** Add $D = 0$ and write the final transfer function (1 mark):

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}}$$

**Step 6:** Verification comment (1 mark):

This matches the result of Question A, demonstrating that the same transfer function can be realized by **different state space representations** ŌĆö this is the concept of **realization theory** in modern control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Identity Matrix:** Students often write $s - A$ instead of $sI - A$. Since $A$ is a matrix and $s$ is a scalar, this is **dimensionally meaningless**. Always use $sI - A$.
>
> 2. **Sign Errors in Adjugate:** The adjugate of a $2 \times 2$ matrix swaps the diagonal elements and **negates the off-diagonal elements**. A common mistake is to forget the negation of the off-diagonals.
>
> 3. **Not Stating Zero Initial Conditions:** The transfer function is defined ONLY under zero initial conditions. Failing to mention this can cost 0.5 to 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing $D$ Matrix Dimensions:** $D$ is a $p \times m$ matrix (or scalar in SISO), NOT a $1 \times 1$ always. For a $2 \times 2$ MIMO, it is a $2 \times 2$ matrix.
>
> 5. **Determinant Computation Errors:** When using the cofactor expansion method, students frequently mis-sign the alternate terms. For a $2 \times 2$, remember: $\det\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - bc$.
>
> 6. **Skipping Steps:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the **intermediate steps** ŌĆö $X(s)$ substitution, factoring, and the resolvent computation. Writing only the final formula without derivation will result in **partial marking**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 4: Transfer Function from State Space**

- Ō£ģ The **state equation** is $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and the **output equation** is $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$.
- Ō£ģ The **transfer function matrix** is universally given by $H(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.
- Ō£ģ The matrix $(sI - A)^{-1}$ is called the **resolvent matrix** $\Phi(s)$.
- Ō£ģ Transfer function is defined with **zero initial conditions** $x(0) = 0$.
- Ō£ģ The **denominator** of $H(s)$ is always $\det(sI - A)$ ŌĆö the **characteristic polynomial** of the system.
- Ō£ģ The **poles** of $H(s)$ are the **eigenvalues of $A$**, i.e., the roots of $\det(sI - A) = 0$.
- Ō£ģ The **zeros** of $H(s)$ come from the numerator polynomial $C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B$ (plus $D$ contributions).
- Ō£ģ For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$:
  - Determinant = $ad - bc$
  - Adjugate = $\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$
- Ō£ģ The matrix $D$ contributes only to **direct feedthrough** (numerator constants); it does **not** affect the poles.
- Ō£ģ Stability: All eigenvalues of $A$ must have $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$.
- Ō£ģ The same transfer function can have **multiple state space realizations** (realization theory).
- Ō£ģ Common KTU exam traps: forgetting $I$ in $sI - A$, sign errors in adjugate, omitting the zero-initial-condition assumption.
- Ō£ģ For SISO systems, $A$ is $n \times n$, $B$ is $n \times 1$, $C$ is $1 \times n$, $D$ is $1 \times 1$.
- Ō£ģ For MIMO systems with $m$ inputs and $p$ outputs, the TFM $H(s)$ is a $p \times m$ matrix of transfer functions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always begin your 14-mark answer with the formula $H(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$, then show the three core computations: (1) $sI - A$, (2) $\det(sI - A)$, and (3) $\text{adj}(sI - A)$. This structured presentation is what KTU examiners look for in the model answer key.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
