<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: SOLUTION OF STATE EQUATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of State Equations

In modern control theory, a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** continuous-time system is mathematically described in the state-space domain by two fundamental vector-matrix equations:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t)
$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ is an $n \times 1$ **state vector** (internal memory of the system)
- $u(t)$ is an $r \times 1$ **input vector**
- $y(t)$ is an $m \times 1$ **output vector**
- $A$ is an $n \times n$ **system matrix** (determines natural/unforced dynamics)
- $B$ is an $n \times r$ **input matrix**
- $C$ is an $m \times n$ **output matrix**
- $D$ is an $m \times r$ **feedthrough (direct transmission) matrix**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The *Solution of State Equations* refers to the determination of the state vector $x(t)$ and output vector $y(t)$ for all $t \geq 0$, given the initial state $x(0)$, the input $u(t)$, and the system matrices $(A, B, C, D)$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Car on a Hilly Road

Imagine you are driving a car on a road whose slope changes at every meter. To know **where the car will be** after 10 seconds, you need two things:

1. **Where the car is now** (initial condition $x(0)$ ŌĆö e.g., position, velocity, fuel level).
2. **How the road slope and your acceleration** change over time (the system's natural behavior $A$, and your inputs $B\,u(t)$ ŌĆö gas pedal, brakes, steering).

The "solution" of the state equation is simply the **mathematical prediction** of the car's future position, velocity, and fuel level, given its present state and the control inputs you apply. Just like solving a differential equation predicts the future from the present, the state-space solution tells us how the *entire internal condition* of a system evolves with time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why State-Space Solution Matters in Engineering:** Unlike classical transfer functions (which only describe input-output behavior), the state-space solution reveals the *internal dynamics*. This is critical in designing observers (Kalman filters), optimal controllers (LQR), and stability analysis (Lyapunov methods).

---

### 1.3 The Two-Component Decomposition

The complete solution $x(t)$ of a state equation is the **superposition** of two physically meaningful components:

| Component | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Input Response (ZIR)** | System's natural evolution due to initial energy stored | $e^{At} x(0)$ |
| **Zero-State Response (ZSR)** | System's forced evolution due to external input | $\int_{0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "ZIR" = *Zero Input, non-zero initial state* ŌåÆ describes what the system does on its own. "ZSR" = *Zero initial state, non-zero input* ŌåÆ describes how the system responds to being driven.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in **Bold**

- The **state transition matrix** $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ is **dimensionless** and always **square** ($n \times n$).
- The **time constant** $\tau$ of a system is measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Eigenvalues of A** (denoted $\lambda_i$) determine stability: a system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$.
- The **matrix exponential** $e^{At}$ is evaluated at time $t$ in **seconds**, and $\Phi(0) = I$ (identity matrix) is its anchor point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The State Transition Matrix ŌĆö The Heart of State-Space Solution

The **State Transition Matrix** $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ is the most important concept in this module. It maps the initial state $x(t_0)$ to the state at any later time $t$ in the absence of input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Define the state transition matrix and list its properties." This question has appeared **at least 4 times** in past KTU university exams.

**Definition:**
$$
\Phi(t) \triangleq e^{At} = I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!}
$$

This infinite series is the matrix analog of the scalar Taylor series $e^{at}$.

---

### 2.2 Derivation of the Solution ŌĆö Why $e^{At}$ Appears

Starting from the homogeneous (unforced) equation $\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t)$, we can verify that $x(t) = e^{At} x(0)$ is a solution by differentiation:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} e^{At} = A e^{At} = e^{At} A
$$

This works because $A$ commutes with all of its powers, just like a scalar.

For the **forced equation** $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, we use the **Variation of Parameters** method. Premultiply both sides by $e^{-At}$:

$$
e^{-At} \dot{x}(t) - e^{-At} A x(t) = e^{-At} B u(t)
$$

The left side is $\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-At} x(t)\right]$, so:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{-At} x(t)\right] = e^{-At} B u(t)
$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$
e^{-At} x(t) - x(0) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-A\tau} B u(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

Multiplying by $e^{At}$:

$$
x(t) = \underbrace{e^{At} x(0)}_{\text{Zero-Input Response}} + \underbrace{\int_{0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau}_{\text{Zero-State Response}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The term $e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau$ represents the contribution of the input applied at time $\tau$ to the state at time $t$, propagated forward through the system's natural dynamics $e^{A(t-\tau)}$.

---

### 2.3 The Output Equation Solution

The corresponding output is:

$$
y(t) = C e^{At} x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} C e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau + D u(t)
$$

The three terms have clear meanings:
- $C e^{At} x(0)$ ŌåÆ output due to initial conditions
- $\int_{0}^{t} C e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau$ ŌåÆ output due to input (convolution)
- $D u(t)$ ŌåÆ instantaneous direct feedthrough

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Name / Purpose** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ | State equation | First-order ODE system |
| 2 | $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$ | Output equation | Linear in state and input |
| 3 | $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}$ | State transition matrix (infinite series) | Always defined for any square A |
| 4 | $x(t) = e^{At} x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Complete state solution | Superposition of ZIR + ZSR |
| 5 | $y(t) = C e^{At} x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} C e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau + D u(t)$ | Complete output solution | Full system response |
| 6 | $\Phi(0) = I$ | Initial condition of $\Phi$ | Identity matrix at $t=0$ |
| 7 | $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$ | Semi-group property | Markovian evolution |
| 8 | $\Phi(t_2 - t_1) = \Phi(t_1)^{-1} \Phi(t_2)$ | Transition between any two times | Fundamental property |
| 9 | $\Phi(t)^{-1} = \Phi(-t) = e^{-At}$ | Inverse property | Always exists |
| 10 | $\Phi(t) \Phi(s) = \Phi(s) \Phi(t)$ | Commutativity | All powers of A commute |
| 11 | $\frac{d}{dt}\Phi(t) = A \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) A$ | Differential property | Generator of motion |
| 12 | $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Laplace transform method | Practical computation |
| 13 | $\text{tr}(A)$ | Trace of A | Sum of eigenvalues |
| 14 | $\det(\Phi(t)) = e^{\text{tr}(A) \cdot t}$ | Determinant property | Useful for stability |
| 15 | $[\Phi(t), A] = 0$ | $\Phi$ commutes with A | Matrix exponential uniqueness |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula sheet, students often confuse $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ with $e^{A t}$ where $A$ and $t$ are reversed. Always remember: **A is the matrix, t is the scalar** ŌĆö the correct order is $e^{At}$, not $e^{tA}$.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of State Equation Solutions

The ability to solve state equations is foundational in:

1. **Aerospace Engineering** ŌĆö Computing trajectories of spacecraft given thruster inputs.
2. **Robotics** ŌĆö Predicting joint angles and velocities from torque inputs.
3. **Power Systems** ŌĆö Modeling generator dynamics and load frequency control.
4. **Process Control** ŌĆö Designing Model Predictive Controllers (MPC).
5. **Filter Design** ŌĆö Kalman filter prediction step uses $x_{k+1} = e^{A\Delta t} x_k + \int e^{A(\Delta t - \tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau$.

---

### 2.6 Three Methods to Compute $e^{At}$

KTU students must master **all three** computation methods:

| **Method** | **Formula** | **Best Used When** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Infinite Series (Definition)** | $e^{At} = I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \cdots$ | A is nilpotent ($A^k = 0$ for small k) |
| **2. Laplace Transform** | $e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Generic method; systematic for KTU |
| **3. Eigenvalue / Diagonalization** | $e^{At} = P \, \text{diag}(e^{\lambda_1 t}, \ldots, e^{\lambda_n t}) P^{-1}$ | A has distinct eigenvalues |
| **4. Cayley-Hamilton** | Express $A^n$ as polynomial in $A$ of degree < n | Reduces infinite series to finite sum |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The Laplace transform method is the **most frequently tested** in university exams, because it is purely algebraic and reduces to matrix inversion ŌĆö easy to grade fairly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Solution via Laplace Transform Method

**Given:** $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ with initial state $x(0)$.

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace transform of both sides.

Recall that $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = s X(s) - x(0)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{A x(t)\} = A X(s)$ (since A is a constant matrix). So:

$$
s X(s) - x(0) = A X(s) + B U(s)
$$

**Step 2:** Collect $X(s)$ terms on the left side.

$$
s X(s) - A X(s) = x(0) + B U(s)
$$

Factor out $X(s)$:

$$
(sI - A) X(s) = x(0) + B U(s)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $sI$ and not $s$?** Because $A$ is an $n \times n$ matrix, we cannot subtract a scalar $s$ from a matrix. The identity matrix $I$ is the matrix equivalent of "1", enabling the operation.

**Step 3:** Premultiply both sides by $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (the resolvent matrix).

$$
X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

**Step 4:** Take the inverse Laplace transform.

Using the convolution theorem: $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s) G(s)\} = \int_0^t f(t-\tau) g(\tau) d\tau$:

$$
x(t) = \underbrace{\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\} \, x(0)}_{\text{Zero-Input Response}} + \underbrace{\int_0^t \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\} \, B \, u(t-\tau)\, d\tau}_{\text{Zero-State Response}}
$$

**Step 5:** Identify the state transition matrix.

$$
\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This gives us the famous Laplace-domain identity:** $X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Full Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = [1 \;\; 0]$, $D = 0$, $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $u(t) = $ unit step, find $x(t)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $sI - A$.

$$
sI - A = s \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant.

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inverse (adjugate divided by determinant).

The cofactor matrix is:

$$
\text{cof}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & -2 \\ 1 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait, we need to transpose for adjugate. The adjugate of $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ is $\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

$$
\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Form $(sI - A)^{-1}$.

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Partial fraction decomposition of each element.

For the $(1,1)$ element: $\dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$.

Setting $s = -1$: $A = \dfrac{-1+3}{1} = 2$.
Setting $s = -2$: $B = \dfrac{-2+3}{-1} = -1$.

So: $\dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{2}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$.

For the $(1,2)$ element: $\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$.

For the $(2,1)$ element: $\dfrac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+2}$.

For the $(2,2)$ element: $\dfrac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-1}{s+1} + \dfrac{2}{s+2}$.

**Step 6:** Take inverse Laplace transform.

$$
e^{At} = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute $U(s) = \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B U(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Partial fraction for $\dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1/2}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1/2}{s+2}$.

For the (1,1) entry's contribution to ZSR: $X_1(s)$ numerator work:

Actually, let us compute $(sI-A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$.

Multiplying by $U(s) = 1/s$:

$$
\frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}
$$

The first row (1/s/(s+1)(s+2)) inverse Laplace:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}\right\} = \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t}
$$

The second row is $\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$.

**Step 8:** Combine ZIR and ZSR.

$$
x(t) = e^{At} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \\ e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

First row of ZIR: $(2e^{-t} - e^{-2t})(1) + (e^{-t} - e^{-2t})(0) = 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$.

Adding ZSR row 1: $x_1(t) = 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} = \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t}$.

Second row of ZIR: $(-2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t})(1) + (-e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t})(0) = -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t}$.

Adding ZSR row 2: $x_2(t) = -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} + e^{-t} - e^{-2t} = -e^{-t} + e^{-2t}$.

**Final State Vector:**

$$
x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \\ -e^{-t} + e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 9:** Compute the output.

$$
y(t) = C x(t) = [1 \;\; 0] \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \\ -e^{-t} + e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Properties of State Transition Matrix

**Property 1:** $\Phi(0) = I$

**Proof:** Substitute $t = 0$ into the series: $e^{A \cdot 0} = I + 0 + 0 + \cdots = I$. ŌłÄ

**Property 2:** $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$

**Proof:** Use the series definition and the binomial theorem (since $A$ commutes with itself):

$$
e^{A(t_1+t_2)} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{[A(t_1+t_2)]^k}{k!} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{j=0}^{k} \binom{k}{j} (At_1)^j (At_2)^{k-j}
$$

Rearranging the double sum (this is a known identity):

$$
e^{A(t_1+t_2)} = e^{At_1} e^{At_2} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Property 3:** $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t) = e^{-At}$

**Proof:** $\Phi(t) \Phi(-t) = e^{At} e^{-At} = e^{A(t-t)} = e^0 = I$. Hence $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$. ŌłÄ

**Property 4:** $[\Phi(t), A] = 0$ (Commutativity)

**Proof:** $\Phi(t) A = e^{At} A = A e^{At} = A \Phi(t)$ using the series:

$$
e^{At} A = \left(\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!}\right) A = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^{k+1} t^k}{k!} = A \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!} = A e^{At} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Property 5:** $\det(\Phi(t)) = e^{\text{tr}(A) t}$

**Proof:** Using $\det(e^M) = e^{\text{tr}(M)}$ (a fundamental identity of matrix exponentials):

$$
\det(e^{At}) = e^{\text{tr}(At)} = e^{t \cdot \text{tr}(A)} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Property 6:** $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$

Already derived in Section 3.1. ŌłÄ

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Numerical Check for the Example:** $\text{tr}(A) = 0 + (-3) = -3$. Hence $\det(\Phi(t)) = e^{-3t}$. Verify: $\det(\Phi(t))$ from the computed matrix = $(2e^{-t} - e^{-2t})(-e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t}) - (e^{-t} - e^{-2t})(-2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t})$. Expanding: $-2e^{-2t} + 4e^{-3t} + e^{-3t} - 2e^{-4t} - (-2e^{-2t} + 2e^{-3t} + 2e^{-3t} - 2e^{-4t}) = -2e^{-2t} + 5e^{-3t} - 2e^{-4t} + 2e^{-2t} - 4e^{-3t} + 2e^{-4t} = e^{-3t}$. Ō£ō Matches!

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cayley-Hamilton Method for $e^{At}$

**Theorem (Cayley-Hamilton):** Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation.

If $A$ has characteristic polynomial $\Delta(\lambda) = \lambda^n + a_{n-1} \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 I$, then:

$$
A^n + a_{n-1} A^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 A + a_0 I = 0
$$

**Consequence for $e^{At}$:** Since all powers $A^k$ for $k \geq n$ can be reduced to a polynomial of degree $\leq n-1$ in $A$, the infinite series collapses:

$$
e^{At} = \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A + \alpha_2(t) A^2 + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t) A^{n-1}
$$

The scalar functions $\alpha_i(t)$ are found by substituting the eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ of $A$:

$$
e^{\lambda_j t} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \alpha_i(t) \lambda_j^i, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** If $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $A$ with eigenvector $v$, then $e^{At} v = e^{\lambda t} v$ ŌĆö so the matrix exponential restricted to each eigen-direction behaves like a scalar exponential.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: State-Space System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[u t] --> Sum1((+))
    Noise[n t] -.-> Sum1
    Sum1 --> IntDyn[Integral of Ax Bu]
    IntDyn --> State[x t]
    State --> Out[Cx]
    Out --> Output[y t]
    State --> Feed[Du]
    Feed --> Output
    IntDyn --> STMat[State Transition Phi t eAt]
    STMat --> Next[Next State x t plus dt]
    Next --> IntDyn
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Computation of $e^{At}$ via Laplace Transform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: System Matrix A] --> Form[Form sI minus A]
    Form --> Det[Compute det sI minus A]
    Det --> Inv[Compute Adjugate sI minus A]
    Inv --> Divide[Form inverse: Adj over Det]
    Divide --> PFE[Apply Partial Fraction Expansion]
    PFE --> InvLap[Take Inverse Laplace Transform]
    InvLap --> Result[Result: eAt]
    Result --> Verify[Verify Phi 0 equals I and Phi t inverse equals Phi minus t]
    Verify --> End[Complete]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Components of State Equation Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Solution[State Equation Solution x t] --> ZIR[Zero Input Response]
    Solution --> ZSR[Zero State Response]
    ZIR --> ZIR1[eAt times x0]
    ZIR --> ZIR2[System natural evolution]
    ZSR --> ZSR1[Integral of eA t minus tau B u tau d tau]
    ZSR --> ZSR2[Convolution of input with system impulse response]
    ZIR1 --> PropA[Property: depends only on initial state]
    ZSR1 --> PropB[Property: depends only on input]
    ZIR2 --> Intuition1[Analogy: ball rolling down without push]
    ZSR2 --> Intuition2[Analogy: ball pushed by wind while rolling]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Eigenvalue Stability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stable["Stable Region Re lambda less than 0"]
        S1[Lambda 1 minus 1]
        S2[Lambda 2 minus 3]
    end
    subgraph Marginal["Marginal Re lambda equals 0"]
        M1[Lambda 0 plus 0i]
    end
    subgraph Unstable["Unstable Region Re lambda greater than 0"]
        U1[Lambda 1 plus 2i]
        U2[Lambda 2 plus 0i]
    end
    Stable --> Resp1[Response decays to zero]
    Marginal --> Resp2[Response oscillates bounded]
    Unstable --> Resp3[Response grows unbounded]
```

### 4.5 Process Flow: Three Methods of Computing $e^{At}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[A matrix given] --> Decision{Choose method}
    Decision -->|A is small or nilpotent| M1[Method 1: Direct Series]
    Decision -->|A is general 2x2 or 3x3| M2[Method 2: Laplace Transform]
    Decision -->|A has distinct eigenvalues| M3[Method 3: Diagonalization]
    Decision -->|A has repeated eigenvalues| M4[Method 4: Cayley-Hamilton]
    M1 --> R1[Sum finite terms]
    M2 --> R2[Compute sI minus A inverse and inverse Laplace]
    M3 --> R3[Use P diag e lambda t P inverse]
    M4 --> R4[Solve linear system for alpha coefficients]
    R1 --> Final[eAt]
    R2 --> Final
    R3 --> Final
    R4 --> Final
```

### 4.6 Detailed Time-Domain Response Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Initial[Initial State]
        X0[x at t equals 0]
    end
    subgraph ZIRDomain[Zero Input Response]
        Z1[Free Evolution]
        Z2[Natural Modes e lambda t]
        Z3[Decaying or Growing]
    end
    subgraph ZSRDomain[Zero State Response]
        F1[Forced Evolution]
        F2[Input Coupling B u t]
        F3[Convolution Integral]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output y t]
        Y1[C x t plus D u t]
    end
    X0 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the **state transition matrix** $\Phi(t)$ and list any **four** of its important properties.

**Model Answer:**

The state transition matrix $\Phi(t)$ is defined as the matrix exponential of the system matrix $A$:

$$
\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}
$$

It represents the natural, unforced evolution of the state vector.

**Four Important Properties:**

1. **Initial condition:** $\Phi(0) = I$ (the identity matrix).

2. **Semi-group / Composition:** $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$.

3. **Inverse property:** $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t) = e^{-At}$.

4. **Commutativity with A:** $\Phi(t) A = A \Phi(t)$, equivalently $\frac{d}{dt}\Phi(t) = A \Phi(t)$.

*(Any four of the six properties listed in Section 2.4 are acceptable.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between the **Zero-Input Response (ZIR)** and the **Zero-State Response (ZSR)** in the solution of state equations.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Zero-Input Response (ZIR) | Zero-State Response (ZSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Input $u(t)$ | Zero (system unforced) | Non-zero (system forced) |
| Initial state $x(0)$ | Non-zero | Zero |
| Mathematical form | $e^{At} x(0)$ | $\int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau$ |
| Physical meaning | Natural decay/growth of stored energy | Response due to external input |
| Depends on | System matrix $A$ and initial condition $x(0)$ | System matrices $A, B$ and input $u(t)$ |

The **complete state response** is the linear superposition: $x(t) = \text{ZIR} + \text{ZSR}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** For the system described by:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = [1 \;\; 0], \quad D = 0
$$
with initial state $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and unit step input $u(t) = 1, \; t \geq 0$:

**(a)** Compute the state transition matrix $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ using the **Laplace transform method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the complete state response $x(t)$ and output response $y(t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Form the matrix $sI - A$.

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant.

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) + 2 = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the adjugate (transpose of cofactor matrix).

$$
\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Form the resolvent $(sI - A)^{-1}$.

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Partial fraction expansion of each element.

- $(1,1)$: $\dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{2}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- $(1,2)$: $\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- $(2,1)$: $\dfrac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- $(2,2)$: $\dfrac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-1}{s+1} + \dfrac{2}{s+2}$

**Step 6:** Apply inverse Laplace transform to get $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$.

$$
\boxed{e^{At} = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Verification:** $\Phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2-1 & 1-1 \\ -2+2 & -1+2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I$ Ō£ō

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute the Zero-Input Response (ZIR).

$$
x_{\text{ZIR}}(t) = e^{At} x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Zero-State Response (ZSR). With $U(s) = 1/s$:

$$
X_{\text{ZSR}}(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Partial fraction of $\dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1/2}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1/2}{s+2}$.

So the inverse Laplace of row 1: $\dfrac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \dfrac{1}{2}e^{-2t}$.
The inverse Laplace of row 2: $\dfrac{s}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$.

**Step 3:** Combine ZIR and ZSR.

$$
x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} + e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \\ -e^{-t} + e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the output.

$$
y(t) = C x(t) = [1 \;\; 0] \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \\ -e^{-t} + e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t}}
$$

**As $t \to \infty$:** $y(\infty) = \frac{1}{2}$ (steady-state value), confirming the system is **stable** (both eigenvalues have negative real parts: $-1$ and $-2$).

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:**

**(a)** State and prove the **four fundamental properties** of the state transition matrix $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a system with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $e^{At}$ using the **diagonalization method**, and hence find the state response for $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ with $u(t) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Property 1: $\Phi(0) = I$**

$$
\Phi(0) = e^{A \cdot 0} = I + A \cdot 0 + \frac{(A \cdot 0)^2}{2!} + \cdots = I
$$

**Property 2: $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$ (Semi-group property)**

Using the Taylor series and the commutativity $A^{j} A^{k} = A^{j+k} = A^{k} A^{j}$:

$$
e^{A(t_1 + t_2)} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k (t_1+t_2)^k}{k!} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k}{k!} \sum_{j=0}^{k} \binom{k}{j} t_1^j t_2^{k-j}
$$

Reindexing the double sum:

$$
= \left(\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At_1)^j}{j!}\right) \left(\sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At_2)^m}{m!}\right) = e^{At_1} e^{At_2}
$$

**Property 3: $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$**

$$
\Phi(t) \Phi(-t) = e^{At} e^{-At} = e^{A(t-t)} = e^0 = I
$$

Therefore $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$.

**Property 4: $\frac{d\Phi(t)}{dt} = A \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) A$**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} e^{At} = \frac{d}{dt} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^{k-1}}{(k-1)!} = A \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^m t^m}{m!} = A e^{At}
$$

Similarly, $e^{At} A = A e^{At}$ follows from $A$ commuting with all of its powers.

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** The matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ is already **diagonal**.

So $P = I$ and $\Lambda = A$.

**Step 2:** The matrix exponential is:

$$
e^{At} = P \, e^{\Lambda t} P^{-1} = e^{\Lambda t} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** State response with $u(t) = 0$ (free response).

$$
x(t) = e^{At} x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} \\ e^{2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Stability check.

The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\lambda_2 = 2$, both with **positive real parts**. Hence the system is **unstable** ŌĆö confirmed by the response that grows without bound as $t \to \infty$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Common Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial state $x(0)$ term** in the complete solution ŌĆö partial fraction without including ZIR gives only half the answer (ŌĆō3 to ŌĆō5 marks).
>
> 2. **Wrong order in $e^{At}$ vs. $e^{tA}$:** Since $A$ is a matrix, $e^{At} = e^{tA}$ only because $A$ commutes with itself. But in **scalar expressions inside the exponential**, students write $e^{At}$ when they mean $e^{a t}$ for a scalar. This is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusing $(sI - A)^{-1}$ with $1/(sI - A)$:** The inverse is a matrix whose elements are rational functions of $s$, NOT the reciprocal of a matrix. Many students forget the adjugate/determinant step.
>
> 4. **Skipping partial fraction decomposition** ŌĆö leaving $(sI-A)^{-1}$ un-decomposed and not taking inverse Laplace explicitly. Examiners require the explicit time-domain form.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in cofactor computation:** When inverting a 3x3 or higher matrix, the cofactor signs follow the checkerboard pattern $(+,-,+,-,+,-...)$. A single sign error gives wrong $e^{At}$ ŌĆö and the partial fractions cascade.
>
> 6. **Not verifying $\Phi(0) = I$:** Examiners reward verification ŌĆö it shows the student understands the property. Add this as a final check for **2 bonus marks** in borderline cases.
>
> 7. **Missing units or interpretation:** Although state equations are abstract, in industrial applications, mentioning **physical units** (e.g., position in meters, velocity in m/s) and **stability interpretation** shows mastery.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

**Core Equations (Memorize):**
- $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$
- $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$
- $x(t) = e^{At} x(0) + \int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau) d\tau$

**Six Properties of $\Phi(t)$ (must be memorized verbatim):**
1. $\Phi(0) = I$
2. $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$
3. $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t) = e^{-At}$
4. $[\Phi(t), A] = 0$ (commutes with A)
5. $\frac{d}{dt} \Phi(t) = A \Phi(t)$
6. $\det \Phi(t) = e^{t \cdot \text{tr}(A)}$

**Four Methods to Compute $e^{At}$:**
1. Infinite series (works for nilpotent $A$)
2. Laplace transform: $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ ŌĆö **most common in KTU**
3. Diagonalization: $e^{At} = P e^{\Lambda t} P^{-1}$
4. Cayley-Hamilton: reduces to polynomial in $A$

**Stability Criterion:**
- System is **asymptotically stable** iff $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all eigenvalues of $A$.
- $\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{At} = 0$ matrix iff all eigenvalues have negative real parts.

**Superposition Principle (ZIR + ZSR):**
- The complete state response is the **sum** of zero-input and zero-state responses.
- The output response is $y(t) = C \cdot x(t) + D \cdot u(t)$.

**Special Cases to Remember:**
- If $A$ is diagonal: $e^{At} = \text{diag}(e^{a_{11} t}, e^{a_{22} t}, \ldots)$
- If $A = 0$: $e^{At} = I$
- If $A^2 = 0$ (nilpotent of index 2): $e^{At} = I + At$

**Practical Hints for KTU Exam:**
- Always write $sI - A$, **never** $s - A$.
- Always verify $\Phi(0) = I$ as a check.
- Always state the system is stable/unstable and justify by eigenvalues.
- Always show partial fraction decomposition explicitly (examiners grade step-by-step).

**Conceptual Anchors:**
- **State transition matrix** = "DNA of the system" ŌĆö encodes all natural modes.
- **ZIR** = "memory of the past" ŌĆö what the system was before the input arrived.
- **ZSR** = "reaction to present" ŌĆö what the system does because of the current input.
- **Eigenvalues of A** = "frequencies of vibration" ŌĆö determine decay, growth, or oscillation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** The Laplace transform method is your safest bet ŌĆö it works for all $2 \times 2$ and most $3 \times 3$ systems, doesn't require eigenvalue computation, and is purely algebraic. Practice at least 3 different $A$ matrices using this method before the exam. Combined with memorized properties of $\Phi(t)$ and the ZIR + ZSR decomposition, this topic becomes a guaranteed **full-mark** answer in KTU 2024 scheme exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Transition Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Transition Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **State Transition Matrix (STM)**, denoted as **$\boldsymbol{\phi(t)}$** (or **$\boldsymbol{\Phi(t)}$**, **$e^{At}$**), is a fundamental matrix function that completely characterizes the **free (unforced / zero-input) response** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) continuous-time state-space system.

For a homogeneous LTI system described by:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

the **unique** state trajectory is given by:

$$x(t) = \phi(t) \, x(0) = e^{At} \, x_0$$

Hence, the State Transition Matrix is mathematically defined as the **matrix exponential** of the system matrix $A$:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) \;\triangleq\; e^{At} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!} \;=\; I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 4:** The State Transition Matrix is the *cornerstone* of time-domain state-space analysis. It is essential for solving both **homogeneous (zero-input)** and **non-homogeneous (forced)** state equations, and is repeatedly asked in the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** as well as **internal assessments**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a **tall office building** (the *state space*) and you wish to reach the **ground floor lobby** (the *zero state*).

- Each **step (transition)** moves you from one floor to another, governed by the *building's stairway layout* ŌĆö this layout is the **system matrix $A$**.
- The **State Transition Matrix $\phi(t)$** is like a **complete navigator's map** that tells you, given your *current floor* $x(0)$, exactly *which floor* $x(t)$ you will be on after time $t$, assuming you take the *same stairs* continuously.
- A key property: $\phi(0) = I$ (the **identity navigator**) ŌĆö at time $t=0$, you are *still where you started*, no transition has occurred.

In short: **STM maps "where you were" to "where you will be."**

## 1.3 Physical Significance

> [!NOTE]
> - **$\phi(t)$ is square** with dimension $(n \times n)$, matching the dimension of the state vector.
> - **$\phi(0) = I_n$** ŌĆö the $n \times n$ identity matrix, since no time has elapsed.
> - **$\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t) = \phi(t)A$** ŌĆö the STM itself satisfies the *same* state equation.
> - The STM is **always non-singular (invertible)** for any finite $t$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The State Transition Matrix is derived directly from the unforced LTI system $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t)$. Below is the logical step-by-step reasoning:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Homogeneous Equation Setup:** Start with $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t)$ and assume a trial scalar/matrix solution of the form $x(t) = e^{\lambda t} v$. Substituting yields the eigenvalue problem $Av = \lambda v$.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Extension to Matrix Form:** A natural matrix generalization of the scalar exponential $e^{at} = 1 + at + \frac{a^2 t^2}{2!} + \cdots$ leads directly to the **matrix exponential** $e^{At}$.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Uniqueness:** The PicardŌĆōLindel├Čf theorem guarantees that $x(t) = e^{At} x(0)$ is the **unique** solution of $\dot{x} = Ax$, $x(0)$ given.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Forced Response Extension:** When an input $u(t)$ is present, $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu(t)$ gives the **variation of parameters** solution:
$$x(t) = \phi(t) x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \phi(t - \tau)\, B\, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Meaning:** $\phi(t)$ propagates the initial state forward, and the convolution integral propagates the effect of the input $u(\tau)$ forward in time, weighted by $\phi(t-\tau)$.

## 2.2 Properties of the State Transition Matrix

The following are the **must-know KTU properties** (every KTU paper tests at least 2 of these):

| # | Property | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Initial Value | $\phi(0) = I$ | No time elapsed ŌåÆ state unchanged |
| 2 | Derivative | $\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t) = \phi(t)A$ | STM obeys the same system equation |
| 3 | Inverse | $\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$ | Backward propagation uses negative time |
| 4 | Composition | $\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\,\phi(t_2)$ | Sequential transitions multiply |
| 5 | Determinant | $\det \phi(t) = e^{\mathrm{tr}(A)\,t}$ | Bounded iff $\mathrm{tr}(A) \leq 0$ |
| 6 | Non-singular | $\det \phi(t) \neq 0 \, \forall t$ | Always invertible for LTI systems |
| 7 | State Solution | $x(t) = \phi(t)\,x(0)$ | Direct zero-input state prediction |

## 2.3 Methods of Computing the State Transition Matrix

For KTU exams, you must know **all four** computational methods. The examiner may ask for any one.

| Method | Formula / Procedure | When Preferred |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Laplace Transform** | $\phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Most popular in KTU ŌĆö clean, systematic |
| **Power Series** | $\phi(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}$ | Theoretical derivations, formal proofs |
| **CayleyŌĆōHamilton** | Use $\phi(t) = \alpha_0 I + \alpha_1 A + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1} A^{n-1}$ | Practical, reduces infinite sum to $n$ terms |
| **Diagonalisation** | If $A = V \Lambda V^{-1}$, then $\phi(t) = V e^{\Lambda t} V^{-1}$ | When $A$ has $n$ distinct eigenvalues |
| **Sylvester's Interpolation** | Solve $\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \alpha_j \lambda_i^{\,j} = e^{\lambda_i t}$ for $\alpha_j$ | When CayleyŌĆōHamilton needs simplification |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these formulas ŌĆö they are the **direct answer key** for Module 4 KTU questions.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| F1 | $\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$ | STM via Laplace |
| F2 | $x(t) = \phi(t)\,x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \phi(t-\tau)\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$ | General solution (forced) |
| F3 | $\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\,\phi(t_2)$ | Semi-group property |
| F4 | $\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$ | Inverse via negative time |
| F5 | $\phi(kT) = (e^{AT})^{k} = G^{k}$ where $G = e^{AT}$ | Discretization link |
| F6 | $e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$ | Laplace recipe |
| F7 | $\det\phi(t) = e^{\mathrm{tr}(A)\,t}$ | Determinant formula |
| F8 | $A\phi(t) = \phi(t)A$ | Commutativity with $A$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Trajectory prediction of satellites and aircraft using STM-based linearization.
- **Robotics:** Forward kinematics and motion planning in joint-space control.
- **Power Systems:** Transient stability analysis post-disturbance.
- **Process Control:** State prediction in Model Predictive Control (MPC).
- **Signal Processing:** Kalman filter relies on $\phi(t)$ to propagate covariance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö STM via Laplace Transform (Full Derivation)

We start from the homogeneous state equation:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace transform of both sides. Recall $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = sX(s) - x(0)$.

$$sX(s) - x(0) = A X(s)$$

**Step 2:** Group the $X(s)$ terms on one side. We want to solve for $X(s)$.

$$sX(s) - A X(s) = x(0)$$

$$(sI - A) X(s) = x(0)$$

**Step 3:** Pre-multiply both sides by $(sI - A)^{-1}$, which exists when $\det(sI - A) \neq 0$ (true almost everywhere in the $s$-plane).

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}\, x(0)$$

**Step 4:** Take the inverse Laplace transform of both sides. By linearity:

$$x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\} \; x(0)$$

**Step 5:** Compare this result with the canonical form $x(t) = \phi(t)\,x(0)$. Identification yields:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}}$$

This is the **fundamental Laplace recipe** for STM computation in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Property: $\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t)$

**Step 1:** Start from the power series definition:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!} \;=\; I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate term-by-term. Constant term vanishes; each remaining term acquires a factor of $k$ in the numerator:

$$\dot{\phi}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{A^k \cdot k \, t^{k-1}}{k!} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{A^k \, t^{k-1}}{(k-1)!}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $j = k-1$ (so $k = j+1$):

$$\dot{\phi}(t) = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^{j+1} \, t^{j}}{j!} = A \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^{j} \, t^{j}}{j!}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner sum as $\phi(t)$ itself:

$$\dot{\phi}(t) = A\, \phi(t)$$

By symmetry, one can equally show $\dot{\phi}(t) = \phi(t) A$ because $A$ commutes with all its own powers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Property: $\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\phi(t_2)$

**Step 1:** Use the definition $\phi(t) = e^{At}$. For scalar exponentials we know $e^{a(t_1+t_2)} = e^{at_1} \cdot e^{at_2}$.

**Step 2:** Expand each side as a power series:

$$\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k (t_1 + t_2)^k}{k!}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the right-hand product as a Cauchy product of two series:

$$\phi(t_1)\phi(t_2) = \left(\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^i t_1^i}{i!}\right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^j t_2^j}{j!}\right) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} A^k \sum_{i+j=k} \frac{t_1^i t_2^j}{i!\,j!}$$

**Step 4:** The inner sum is precisely the multinomial expansion coefficient of $(t_1+t_2)^k / k!$:

$$\sum_{i+j=k} \frac{t_1^i t_2^j}{i!\,j!} = \frac{(t_1 + t_2)^k}{k!}$$

**Step 5:** Hence the products are identical:

$$\boxed{\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\,\phi(t_2)}$$

Setting $t_2 = -t_1$ immediately gives $\phi(t)\phi(-t) = \phi(0) = I$, so $\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, find $\phi(t)$ using the Laplace method.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $(sI - A)$:**

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute its determinant:**

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the adjugate and the inverse:**

$$\mathrm{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Partial fraction expansion** of each entry:

$$\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply inverse Laplace transform** to each entry:

$$\phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification at $t = 0$:**

$$\phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 1 & 1 - 1 \\ -2 + 2 & -1 + 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Role of the State Transition Matrix

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional flow** of how the State Transition Matrix operates within an LTI state-space system, illustrating the interconnections between initial state, STM, and forced response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Initial State x of 0"]:::input
    node2["System Matrix A (n x n)"]:::matrix
    node3["Input u of t"]:::input
    node4["Input Matrix B"]:::matrix
    node5["Laplace Block (sI minus A) inverse"]:::compute
    node6["State Transition Matrix phi of t equals e to the power A t"]:::core
    node7["Convolution Integral phi of t minus tau B u of tau d tau"]:::compute
    node8["Current State x of t"]:::output
    node9["Verification Checks phi of 0 equals I"]:::verify
    node10["Property Check phi t1 plus t2 equals phi t1 phi t2"]:::verify

    node1 --> node6
    node2 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
    node6 --> node10

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D5C
    classDef matrix fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A4400

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Time t equals 0: x of 0"]:::startend
    stepB["Apply phi of t = e to the power A t"]:::process
    stepC["Time t: x of t equals phi of t x of 0"]:::state
    stepD["Forced Addition: integral phi of t minus tau B u of tau d tau"]:::process
    stepE["Full Solution x of t"]:::state

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
```

```mermaid

    block2["Form sI minus A"]:::process

    block9["Validate phi of 0 equals I"]:::verify

    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block3
    block2 --> block4
    block3 --> block5
    block4 --> block5
    block5 --> block6
    block6 --> block7
    block7 --> block8
    block8 --> block9
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** Define the State Transition Matrix. State any three of its important properties.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The State Transition Matrix $\phi(t)$ of a continuous-time LTI system $\dot{x} = Ax$ is defined as $\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$. It maps the initial state $x(0)$ to the state $x(t)$ at any time $t$.

**Three key properties:**

1. $\phi(0) = I$ (identity matrix at $t=0$).
2. $\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$ (inverse corresponds to negative-time transition).
3. $\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\phi(t_2)$ (semi-group / composition property).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for any two properties, total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** What is the relationship between the State Transition Matrix and the system matrix $A$? Show that $\phi(0) = I$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The State Transition Matrix is the matrix exponential of the system matrix $A$:

$$\phi(t) = e^{At} = I + At + \frac{(At)^2}{2!} + \cdots$$

From the power series:

$$\phi(0) = e^{A \cdot 0} = e^{0} = I_n$$

Since every term in the power series except the first contains a factor of $t$, evaluating at $t=0$ leaves only the identity term. Hence $\phi(0) = I$, confirming that at $t=0$ the state remains unchanged.

*(1 mark for relation, 2 marks for proof)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the State Transition Matrix of the system whose system matrix is:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

using the **Laplace transform method**. Verify the result at $t = 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the result obtained in (a), find the state $x(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ given the initial condition $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and the input $u(t) = 0$. Discuss the stability of the system.

---

### **Complete Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Computation of $\phi(t)$ via Laplace Method

**Step 1:** Form $(sI - A)$:

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s+5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant:

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+5) - (-1)(6) = s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the adjugate:

$$\mathrm{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Invert:

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Partial fraction expansion:

$$\frac{s+5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{3}{s+2} - \frac{2}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{-6}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{-6}{s+2} + \frac{6}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{s}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{-2}{s+2} + \frac{3}{s+3}$$

**Step 6:** Inverse Laplace transform yields the STM:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} & e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} & -2e^{-2t} + 3e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verification at $t = 0$:**

$$\phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 3 - 2 & 1 - 1 \\ -6 + 6 & -2 + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I \quad \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö State Response and Stability Analysis

With $u(t) = 0$, the unforced response is:

$$x(t) = \phi(t) \, x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} & e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} & -2e^{-2t} + 3e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Stability Analysis:**

The eigenvalues of $A$ are $\lambda_1 = -2$ and $\lambda_2 = -3$, both strictly negative real parts. Hence:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = 0$$

The system is **asymptotically stable** (and BIBO stable since it is also a controllable/observable LTI system in canonical form).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the system $\dot{x} = Ax$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the State Transition Matrix using the **CayleyŌĆōHamilton method** and the **diagonalization method**. Show that both methods give identical results.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A second-order system has the State Transition Matrix:

$$\phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

(i) Identify the system matrix $A$. (ii) Verify the four fundamental properties of the STM.

---

### **Complete Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö STM via CayleyŌĆōHamilton and Diagonalization

**CayleyŌĆōHamilton Method:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Characteristic polynomial:** $\det(\lambda I - A) = (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 2) = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 2$

**Step 2 ŌĆö CayleyŌĆōHamilton states:** $A^2 - 3A + 2I = 0 \implies A^2 = 3A - 2I$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express $\phi(t)$ as:** $\phi(t) = \alpha_0(t)\,I + \alpha_1(t)\,A$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Use eigenvalues as interpolation points:** $e^{\lambda t} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \lambda$ for $\lambda = 1, 2$:

- $e^{t} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1$
- $e^{2t} = \alpha_0 + 2\alpha_1$

Subtracting: $\alpha_1 = e^{2t} - e^{t}$, hence $\alpha_0 = 2e^{t} - e^{2t}$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$\phi(t) = (2e^{t} - e^{2t})I + (e^{2t} - e^{t})A = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{t} - e^{2t} + e^{2t} - e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & 2e^{t} - e^{2t} + 2(e^{2t} - e^{t}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Diagonalization Method:** Since $A$ is already diagonal, $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ with $V = I$ and $\Lambda = \mathrm{diag}(1, 2)$:

$$\phi(t) = V e^{\Lambda t} V^{-1} = e^{\Lambda t} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

Both methods yield **identical** results. Ō£ō

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Identifying $A$ and Verifying STM Properties

**(i) Identifying $A$:** Use $\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t)$. At $t = 0$: $\dot{\phi}(0) = A\phi(0) = A \cdot I = A$, so $A = \dot{\phi}(0)$.

Differentiating each entry of $\phi(t)$:

$$\dot{\phi}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \\ 2e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

Evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Verification of Four Properties:**

1. **$\phi(0) = I$:** Computed earlier: $\phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ Ō£ō
2. **$\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t)$:** Differentiate and compare ŌĆö true by construction. Ō£ō
3. **$\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$:** Compute $\phi(-t)$:
$$\phi(-t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{t} - e^{2t} & e^{t} - e^{2t} \\ -2e^{t} + 2e^{2t} & -e^{t} + 2e^{2t} \end{bmatrix}$$
One can verify $\phi(t) \phi(-t) = I$. Ō£ō
4. **Composition property:** $\phi(t_1)\phi(t_2) = \phi(t_1 + t_2)$ ŌĆö verified by matrix multiplication for arbitrary $t_1, t_2$. Ō£ō

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\phi(0) = I$** ŌĆö easy 1 mark lost. Always end your solution with this check.
> 2. **Sign errors in $(sI - A)$** ŌĆö most students incorrectly form $(sI + A)$ or $(A - sI)$. Remember: it is $s$ times identity **minus** $A$.
> 3. **Missing partial fractions** ŌĆö never attempt to invert $(sI-A)^{-1}$ directly via term-by-term inverse Laplace; use **partial fraction expansion** rigorously.
> 4. **Confusing $\phi(t)$ and $\phi(t-\tau)$** ŌĆö the convolution integral uses $\phi(t-\tau)$, not $\phi(\tau - t)$.
> 5. **Skipping stability discussion** ŌĆö examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks for relating eigenvalues to system stability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö State Transition Matrix**

- **Definition:** $\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$ ŌĆö memorize this verbatim.
- **Zero-input response:** $x(t) = \phi(t) x(0)$ ŌĆö fundamental identity.
- **Forced response:** $x(t) = \phi(t) x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$ ŌĆö second fundamental identity.
- **$\phi(0) = I$** ŌĆö always true; use as a verification step in every problem.
- **$\dot{\phi}(t) = A\phi(t) = \phi(t) A$** ŌĆö STM satisfies the same state equation.
- **$\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$** ŌĆö invert by negating time.
- **$\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1) \phi(t_2)$** ŌĆö semi-group property.
- **$\det \phi(t) = e^{\mathrm{tr}(A) t}$** ŌĆö never zero; STM is always non-singular.
- **Computation method priority for KTU:** (1) Laplace ŌåÆ (2) CayleyŌĆōHamilton ŌåÆ (3) Diagonalization ŌåÆ (4) Power series.
- **CayleyŌĆōHamilton trick:** Reduce $e^{At}$ (infinite series) to $\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \alpha_j(t) A^j$ using $n$ interpolation conditions at eigenvalues.
- **Stability link:** System is asymptotically stable **iff** all eigenvalues of $A$ have strictly negative real parts, which implies $\lim_{t \to \infty} \phi(t) = 0$.
- **Discretization link:** $\phi(T) = G$ is the discrete-time system matrix for sampling period $T$.
- **Avoid common errors:** sign of $(sI-A)$, missing partial fractions, forgetting to verify $\phi(0) = I$, and confusing $\phi(t)$ with $\phi^{-1}(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controllability and Observability - Kalman's Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET501 - CONTROL SYSTEM THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Controllability and Observability - Kalman's Test ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Controllability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complete State Controllability (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > A linear time-invariant (LTI) system described by the state equation $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ is said to be **completely state controllable** if it is possible to transfer the system from any arbitrary initial state $x(t_0)$ to any desired final state $x(t_f)$ in a finite time interval $[t_0, t_f]$ by applying an unconstrained control input $u(t)$. The formal mathematical condition is that the **Controllability Matrix** $Q_c$ must have **full rank** equal to the order of the system $n$: $$\text{rank}(Q_c) = n \quad \text{where} \quad Q_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$$ ### 1.2 Formal Definition of Observability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complete Observability (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > A system is said to be **completely observable** if every initial state $x(t_0)$ can be uniquely determined by observing only the output $y(t)$ over a finite time interval $[t_0, t_f]$, assuming the input $u(t)$ is known. The formal condition uses the **Observability Matrix** $Q_o$: $$\text{rank}(Q_o) = n \quad \text{where} \quad Q_o = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Car on a Hill** > - **Controllability** is like asking: *"Can a driver (input) steer a car (state) to *any* location on a hill by choosing the right accelerator and steering angle?"* If the car's drivetrain is decoupled from the rear wheels, you can never make the car go leftŌĆöso it is **not completely controllable**. > - **Observability** is like asking: *"Can a camera (output sensor) see *every* part of the car from its mounting position?"* If the camera points only at the front, the rear wheels' positions remain a mysteryŌĆöhence the system is **not completely observable**. The two properties are **dual** concepts: a system that is controllable via $(A, B)$ has a dual system $(A^T, C^T)$ that is observable. This is the **Principle of Duality**. ### 1.4 Kalman's Test ŌĆö Overview > [!NOTE] > **Kalman's Test** is a direct algebraic rank test that checks whether the controllability and observability matrices are non-singular. It was formulated by **Rudolf E. K├Īlm├Īn** in 1960 and remains the standard analytical tool for state-space system analysis in modern control engineering. The system order $n$ is the **canonical reference**; for an $n$-th order SISO system, both $Q_c$ and $Q_o$ are $n \times n$ square matrices whose determinants must be **non-zero** for complete controllability/observability. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Kalman's Test Works ŌĆö The "How" Behind the Logic The derivation rests on the **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem** and the series expansion of the state transition matrix $e^{At}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Solution of State Equation:** Using $x(t) = e^{At}x(0) + \int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)}Bu(\tau)d\tau$, the final state depends on a linear combination of the control input applied over time. **Step 2 ŌĆö Cayley-Hamilton Application:** Since $e^{At} = \alpha_0(t)I + \alpha_1(t)A + \alpha_2(t)A^2 + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t)A^{n-1}$, the entire state response collapses to: $$x(t_f) = M \cdot v$$ where $M = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ and $v$ is a vector of weighted time-functions of $u(t)$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Existence of a Solution:** A control $u(t)$ achieving any desired $x(t_f)$ exists **if and only if** the columns of $M$ span $\mathbb{R}^n$, i.e., $\text{rank}(M) = n$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Higher-Order Power Shortcut:** Since $A^kB$ for $k \geq n$ can be expressed as a linear combination of $\{B, AB, \ldots, A^{n-1}B\}$ (by Cayley-Hamilton), the matrix $Q_c$ only needs up to $A^{n-1}B$. ### 2.2 The Duality Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Principle of Duality (K├Īlm├Īn, 1960):** > The system $(A, B, C)$ is completely controllable **if and only if** the dual system $(A^T, C^T, B^T)$ is completely observable. > $$\text{Controllable}(A, B) \iff \text{Observable}(A^T, C^T)$$ This means a single computation on $A^T$ and $C^T$ can answer both questions. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for Kalman's Test** | # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Dimension | KTU Quick Check | |---|---------|---------------------|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | State-space form | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$ | $A: n \times n$, $B: n \times m$, $C: p \times n$ | Standard assumption | | 2 | Controllability matrix | $Q_c = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ | $n \times nm$ | For SISO: $n \times n$ | | 3 | Observability matrix | $Q_o = [C^T, A^TC^T, \ldots, (A^T)^{n-1}C^T]^T$ | $np \times n$ | For SISO: $n \times n$ | | 4 | Controllability rank test | $\text{rank}(Q_c) = n$ | Scalar | $\det(Q_c) \neq 0$ for SISO | | 5 | Observability rank test | $\text{rank}(Q_o) = n$ | Scalar | $\det(Q_o) \neq 0$ for SISO | | 6 | Cayley-Hamilton bound | $A^n = -\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} a_i A^i$ | $n \times n$ matrix identity | Why only $n$ terms needed | | 7 | Duality relation | $(A, B)$ controllable $\iff (A^T, C^T)$ observable | Conceptual | Saves computation time | | 8 | Number of $Q_c$ columns | $n \cdot m$ | Integer | Stops at $A^{n-1}B$ | | 9 | Number of $Q_o$ rows | $n \cdot p$ | Integer | Stops at $(A^T)^{n-1}C^T$ | | 10 | Uncontrollability measure | $\rho_c = n - \text{rank}(Q_c)$ | Integer | Zero $\Rightarrow$ controllable | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where this matters in industry:** > - **Spacecraft attitude control (ISRO, NASA):** A satellite with reaction wheels is controllable only if all rotational axes are coupled to the wheels. > - **Autonomous vehicles:** Sensor placement (LIDAR, cameras) must satisfy observability so that position and velocity states are recoverable from measurements. > - **Robotic manipulators:** Each joint's state (angle, velocity) must be both controllable by motor torques **and** observable by encoders; otherwise, the system cannot track desired trajectories. > - **State-feedback pole placement (Module 5 prerequisite):** $A - BK$ design is **only possible** if the system is controllable. This is why Module 4 directly enables Module 5. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Controllability Matrix Starting from the unforced/controlled state equation: $$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$ The Laplace-domain solution yields: $$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}x_0 + (sI - A)^{-1}B \cdot U(s)$$ **Logic Step 1 ŌĆö Use of the Cayley-Hamilton Theorem:** The characteristic equation of $A$ is $\Delta(\lambda) = \lambda^n + a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1\lambda + a_0 = 0$. By Cayley-Hamilton, $A$ satisfies its own characteristic equation: $$A^n = -a_{n-1}A^{n-1} - a_{n-2}A^{n-2} - \cdots - a_1A - a_0I$$ **Logic Step 2 ŌĆö Express $(sI - A)^{-1}$ via the Adjoint:** $$\begin{aligned} (sI - A)^{-1} &= \frac{\text{adj}(sI - A)}{\det(sI - A)} \\ &= \frac{I s^{n-1} + (A + a_{n-1}I)s^{n-2} + \cdots}{s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0} \end{aligned}$$ **Logic Step 3 ŌĆö Substitution Back into the State Equation:** The product $(sI - A)^{-1}B \cdot U(s)$ becomes a sum of terms involving $B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B$ multiplied by scalar functions of $s$. In the time domain, the final state is: $$x(t_f) = e^{At_f}x_0 + \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A^k B \cdot \underbrace{\int_0^{t_f} \alpha_k(\tau) u(\tau) d\tau}_{\beta_k}$$ **Logic Step 4 ŌĆö Linear Independence Requirement:** Reaching an arbitrary $x(t_f)$ from any $x_0$ is equivalent to solving the linear system $Q_c \beta = \Delta x$ where $\Delta x = x(t_f) - e^{At_f}x_0$ and $Q_c = [B, AB, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$. This system has a unique solution for $\beta$ **if and only if** $Q_c$ has full rank $n$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Observability Matrix From the output equation $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$, substitute the time-domain solution for $x(t)$: $$y(t) = Ce^{At}x(0) + \int_0^t C e^{A(t-\tau)}Bu(\tau)d\tau + Du(t)$$ **Logic Step 1 ŌĆö Isolate the $x(0)$ Dependence:** Since $u(t)$ is assumed known, the term depending on $x(0)$ is purely $Ce^{At}x(0)$. **Logic Step 2 ŌĆö Series Expansion of $e^{At}$:** $$e^{At} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Ce^{At}x(0) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} C\frac{A^k t^k}{k!} x(0)$$ **Logic Step 3 ŌĆö Truncation by Cayley-Hamilton:** Because $A^k$ for $k \geq n$ is expressible in terms of $A^0, A^1, \ldots, A^{n-1}$: $$Ce^{At} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \alpha_k(t) CA^k$$ **Logic Step 4 ŌĆö Form the Linear System:** Evaluating $y(t)$ and its derivatives at $t = 0$ produces the system: $$\begin{aligned} y(0) &= Cx(0) \\ \dot{y}(0) &= CAx(0) \\ \ddot{y}(0) &= CA^2x(0) \\ &\vdots \\ y^{(n-1)}(0) &= CA^{n-1}x(0) \end{aligned}$$ Stacked vertically, this is: $$\begin{bmatrix} y(0) \\ \dot{y}(0) \\ \ddot{y}(0) \\ \vdots \\ y^{(n-1)}(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} x(0) = Q_o \cdot x(0)$$ A unique $x(0)$ exists **if and only if** $\text{rank}(Q_o) = n$. ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö Kalman's Test on a 3rd-Order System > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard Textbook Problem:** Apply Kalman's Test on the system with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = [1, 0, 0]$. #### Part A ŌĆö Controllability **Step 1:** Compute $AB$: $$AB = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $A^2B$: $$\begin{aligned} A^2B = A(AB) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(-6) \\ (0)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(-6) \\ (-6)(0) + (-11)(1) + (-6)(-6) \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -6 \\ -11 + 36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -6 \\ 25 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Form the Controllability Matrix $Q_c$: $$Q_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \\ 1 & -6 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** Compute $\det(Q_c)$ (expanding along the first row): $$\begin{aligned} \det(Q_c) &= 0 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -6 \\ -6 & 25 \end{bmatrix} - 0 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -6 \\ 1 & 25 \end{bmatrix} + 1 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= 0 - 0 + 1 \cdot (0 \cdot (-6) - 1 \cdot 1) \\ &= -1 \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$ Since $\det(Q_c) = -1 \neq 0$, $\text{rank}(Q_c) = 3 = n$. **The system is completely state controllable.** #### Part B ŌĆö Observability **Step 1:** Compute $CA$ and $CA^2$: $$CA = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ For $CA^2$, we need $A^2$ first. We compute row 1 of $A^2$ by multiplying row 1 of $A$ with $A$: $$A^2_{\text{row 1}} = [0, 1, 0] \cdot A = [0, 0, 1]$$ Thus $CA^2 = [0, 0, 1]$. **Step 2:** Form the Observability Matrix $Q_o$: $$Q_o = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_3$$ **Step 3:** Determinant: $$\det(Q_o) = 1 \neq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{rank}(Q_o) = 3 = n$$ **The system is completely observable.** > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** This canonical-form system is **both completely controllable and completely observable** ŌĆö a common exam result that you should be able to verify in under 5 minutes. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Kalman's Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: System A, B, C given]) --> dimA{Determine
system order n} dimA --> qc1[Compute B] qc1 --> qc2[Compute A times B] qc2 --> qc3[Compute A squared B] qc3 --> qcLoop{More powers
needed?} qcLoop -->|Yes| qc2 qcLoop -->|No, up to n-1| qc4[Form Qc matrix] qc4 --> qc5[Find rank of Qc] qc5 --> qcDecide{rank Qc equals n?} qcDecide -->|Yes| controllable[System is completely state controllable] qcDecide -->|No| notControllable[System is NOT completely controllable] startB([Start Observability test]) --> dimB{Determine order n} dimB --> qo1[Compute C] qo1 --> qo2[Compute C times A] qo2 --> qo3[Compute C times A squared] qo3 --> qoLoop{More powers needed?} qoLoop -->|Yes| qo2 qoLoop -->|No, up to n-1| qo4[Form Qo matrix] qo4 --> qo5[Find rank of Qo] qo5 --> qoDecide{rank Qo equals n?} qoDecide -->|Yes| observable[System is completely observable] qoDecide -->|No| notObservable[System is NOT completely observable] controllable --> final[Record final result] notControllable --> final observable --> final notObservable --> final final([End of Kalman's Test]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö State-Space System under Test ```mermaid flowchart LR inputNode[Input vector u] --> summNode((+)) summNode --> sysPlant[System Matrix A and Input Matrix B] sysPlant --> stateOut[State vector x dot] stateOut --> integrNode[Integrator] integrNode --> stateVec[State vector x] stateVec --> sysPlant stateVec --> outputBlock[Output Matrix C] outputBlock --> outputNode[Output y] stateVec --> obsMatrix[Observability Matrix Qo] obsMatrix --> obsCheck{rank Qo equals n?} obsCheck -->|Yes| obsYes[Observable] obsCheck -->|No| obsNo[Not observable] inputNode --> ctrlMatrix[Controllability Matrix Qc] sysPlant --> ctrlMatrix ctrlMatrix --> ctrlCheck{rank Qc equals n?} ctrlCheck -->|Yes| ctrlYes[Controllable] ctrlCheck -->|No| ctrlNo[Not controllable] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Duality Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph originalSystem[Original System Sigma A B C] dirA[State matrix A] dirB[Input matrix B] dirC[Output matrix C] end subgraph dualSystem[Dual System Sigma Dual A trans C trans B trans] dualA[State matrix A transpose] dualB[Input matrix C transpose] dualC[Output matrix B transpose] end originalSystem -.Controllable.-> dualSystem dualSystem -.Observable.-> originalSystem note1[Kalman Duality Theorem 1960]:::importantNode originalSystem --> note1 dualSystem --> note1 classDef importantNode fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematics:** > - **Section 4.1** maps the procedural algorithm ŌĆö useful for "show your steps" credit. > - **Section 4.2** shows where the $Q_c$ and $Q_o$ matrices are extracted from in the physical state-space block diagram. > - **Section 4.3** visualises the **Principle of Duality** ŌĆö controllability of $(A, B)$ mirrors observability of $(A^T, C^T)$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö 2-Mark Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]** State the conditions for complete state controllability and complete observability of an LTI system in state-space form. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > For the LTI system $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, $y(t) = Cx(t)$ of order $n$: > - The system is **completely state controllable** if and only if the controllability matrix $Q_c = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ has rank $n$, i.e., $\det(Q_c) \neq 0$ for a SISO system. > - The system is **completely observable** if and only if the observability matrix $Q_o = [C^T, A^TC^T, (A^T)^2C^T, \ldots, (A^T)^{n-1}C^T]^T$ has rank $n$, i.e., $\det(Q_o) \neq 0$ for a SISO system. --- > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]** State and explain the **Principle of Duality** as proposed by K├Īlm├Īn. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **Principle of Duality** states that the system $(A, B, C)$ is completely state controllable **if and only if** the dual system $(A^T, C^T, B^T)$ is completely observable. This is because the controllability matrix of $(A, B)$ is $[B, AB, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$, and the observability matrix of $(A^T, C^T)$ is $[C, C A^T, \ldots, C(A^T)^{n-1}]^T = [C^T, A^TC^T, \ldots, (A^T)^{n-1}C^T]$. Transposing one gives the other, proving that the rank conditions are identical. ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style]* > For the system defined by: > $$A = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ > **(a)** Test the system for complete state controllability using Kalman's Test. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Test the system for complete observability using Kalman's Test. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Form the Controllability Matrix:** We need $Q_c = [B, AB, A^2B]$ since $n = 3$. **Compute $AB$:** $$AB = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $A^2B = A(AB)$:** $$A^2B = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2-2 \\ 4 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ 4 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Form $Q_c$:** $$Q_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & -4 \\ 1 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & -3 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Determinant:** $$\begin{aligned} \det(Q_c) &= 0 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -2 & 4 \\ -3 & 9 \end{vmatrix} - 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 9 \end{vmatrix} + (-4) \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= 0 - 1 \cdot (9 - 4) + (-4) \cdot (-3 + 2) \\ &= -5 + (-4)(-1) \\ &= -5 + 4 = -1 \end{aligned}$$ Since $\det(Q_c) = -1 \neq 0$, we have $\text{rank}(Q_c) = 3 = n$. **Conclusion:** The system is **completely state controllable**. ##### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Form the Observability Matrix:** We need $Q_o = [C^T, A^TC^T, (A^T)^2C^T]^T$. **Compute $A^T$:** $$A^T = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $C^T A^T$ (or equivalently $(CA)^T$):** $$C^T A^T = (AC)^T = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $(A^T)^2 C^T = A^T(C^T A^T)$:** $$(A^T)^2 C^T = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -2+2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Form $Q_o$:** $$Q_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ -2 & -1 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Determinant:** $$\begin{aligned} \det(Q_o) &= 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} - 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 \end{vmatrix} + 0 \\ &= 1 \cdot (0 - 0) - 1 \cdot (0 - 0) + 0 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ Since $\det(Q_o) = 0$, $\text{rank}(Q_o) < 3 = n$. **Conclusion:** The system is **NOT completely observable** ŌĆö a hidden mode exists. The state variable $x_3$ (corresponding to eigenvalue $-3$) cannot be reconstructed from $y(t)$ because $C = [1, 1, 0]$ has no coupling to the third state. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Style]* > Consider the system: > $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ > **(a)** Apply Kalman's Test to verify whether the system is completely state controllable. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Apply Kalman's Test to verify whether the system is completely observable. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Form $Q_c = [B, AB]$:** **Compute $AB$:** $$AB = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Form $Q_c$:** $$Q_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Determinant:** $$\det(Q_c) = (0)(-3) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$ Since $\det(Q_c) \neq 0$, $\text{rank}(Q_c) = 2 = n$. **Conclusion:** The system is **completely state controllable**. ##### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Form $Q_o = [C^T, A^TC^T]^T$:** **Compute $A^T$:** $$A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $A^T C^T$:** $$A^T C^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Form $Q_o$:** $$Q_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Determinant:** $$\det(Q_o) = 1 \neq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{rank}(Q_o) = 2 = n$$ **Conclusion:** The system is **completely observable**. **Overall Conclusion:** The system is **both completely controllable and completely observable** ŌĆö a "minimum phase"-like well-behaved system suitable for state-feedback design (Module 5). ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the upper bound:** Many students compute $A^3B$ or $A^4B$ for a 2nd or 3rd-order system. By **Cayley-Hamilton**, $Q_c$ only needs columns up to $A^{n-1}B$. Computing extra columns wastes time and shows conceptual weakness. > 2. **Wrong transpose in $Q_o$:** Students often write $Q_o = [C; CA; CA^2]$ correctly but then forget that the observability matrix can equivalently be expressed as $[C^T, A^TC^T, (A^T)^2C^T]^T$. Mixing these two forms is the most common algebraic slip-up. > 3. **Rank vs Determinant Confusion:** For SISO systems, $\det(Q_c) \neq 0$ is sufficient, but for MIMO systems, you must explicitly compute $\text{rank}(\cdot)$. Don't apply the determinant shortcut blindly. > 4. **Conflating "Controllable" with "Stabilizable":** A system may be **uncontrollable** but still **stabilizable** if the uncontrollable modes are stable. KTU 2024 examiners specifically test this distinction in 7-mark questions. > 5. **Duality Misapplication:** Some students apply duality to write $Q_c$ for the dual system without properly transposing $A$ **and** $C$. Both must be transposed; only $B$ is replaced by $C^T$. > 6. **Sign Errors in $AB$:** Matrix multiplication is row-by-column. Many students multiply column-by-column, giving the wrong answer. Always check dimensions: $(n \times n) \cdot (n \times 1) = (n \times 1)$. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Kalman's Test** > - **Controllability matrix** for an $n$-th order system: $Q_c = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$ ŌĆö exactly $n$ block columns. > - **Observability matrix**: $Q_o = [C^T, A^TC^T, (A^T)^2C^T, \ldots, (A^T)^{n-1}C^T]^T$ ŌĆö exactly $n$ block rows. > - **SISO test**: $\det(Q_c) \neq 0$ and $\det(Q_o) \neq 0$. > - **MIMO test**: $\text{rank}(Q_c) = n$ and $\text{rank}(Q_o) = n$. > - **Cayley-Hamilton** is the *why* behind stopping at $A^{n-1}B$ ŌĆö higher powers are linearly dependent. > - **Duality**: Controllability of $(A, B) \iff$ Observability of $(A^T, C^T)$. This **halves the computation** if both tests are needed. > - **Engineering impact**: A controllable system admits state-feedback pole placement; an observable system admits a state observer design (Luenberger observer) ŌĆö both foundational for Module 5. > - **Common test cases** in KTU: companion-form matrices with $B = [0, 0, \ldots, 1]^T$ and $C = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]$ are always both controllable and observable (canonical controllable/observable forms). > - **Decoupled systems**: A system in block-diagonal form is controllable **only if** each diagonal block is reachable from the corresponding input ŌĆö a frequent KTU 2024 trap. > - **Always recheck dimensions** before computing the determinant ŌĆö a mismatch signals an error in $A^k$ computation. > **End of Module 4 Notes ŌĆö Kalman's Test** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PCAET501 guidelines.*
